> The pitch formula **changes** between single-slice and multi-slice CT. In **single-slice helical**, $p = d_{table} / w$. In **multi-slice helical**, $p = d_{table} / (N \cdot w)$. Mixing them is a common mark-loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist (for the night before the exam):**

- ✅ CT = **Computed Tomography**; uses X-ray projections + Radon inversion to reconstruct cross-sectional images.
- ✅ **7 generations** are commonly enumerated (some texts combine 6 & 7); know each one's **beam shape, detector count, and motion pattern**.
- ✅ **1st Gen:** Pencil beam, 1 detector, **translate–rotate**, ~5 min/slice. Original EMI Mark I (Hounsfield, 1971).
- ✅ **2nd Gen:** Narrow fan (~3°–10°), 3–30 detectors, **translate–rotate**, ~10–90 s/slice.
- ✅ **3rd Gen:** Wide fan (~30°–55°), 300–1000+ curved detectors, **rotate–rotate** (source + detector both rotate), 1–5 s/slice. **Ring artifacts** possible.
- ✅ **4th Gen:** Wide fan, **stationary detector ring (1200–4800+)**, only source rotates. **No ring artifacts** but expensive.
- ✅ **5th Gen (EBCT):** No mechanical motion; **electron beam is magnetically steered** onto a stationary tungsten target ring; ~50–100 ms/slice. Used for **cardiac** imaging.
- ✅ **6th Gen (Helical/Spiral):** **Slip-ring** enables continuous gantry rotation + continuous table translation → X-ray source traces a **helix**. Volumetric single breath-hold scans.
- ✅ **7th Gen (MDCT):** Multi-row (4–320) detector array, **cone-beam** geometry, 3D FDK or iterative reconstruction, sub-second whole-body scans.
- ✅ **Pitch** $p = d_{table} / (N \cdot w)$; $p<1$ = overlap (better z-resolution, higher dose); $p=1$ = standard; $p>1$ = faster (lower dose, lower SNR).
- ✅ **HU scale:** air = −1000, water = 0, dense bone ≈ +1000. $HU = 1000 \cdot (\mu - \mu_{water}) / \mu_{water}$.
- ✅ **FBP** = Filtered Back-Projection = convolution with filter kernel (Ram-Lak, Shepp-Logan) + back-projection over $\pi$ radians.
- ✅ **Ring artifact** → 3rd gen (a single bad detector); absent in 4th gen because each ray uses a different detector.
- ✅ **Windmill artifact** → helical/MDCT at high pitch.
- ✅ **Cone-beam artifact** → 4th gen and MDCT, especially at peripheral slices.
- ✅ **Beer–Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\int \mu \, ds}$ is the starting equation for *every* CT measurement.
- ✅ **Slip-ring** is the *enabling technology* of helical CT — remember to mention it explicitly in any Gen-6 question.
- ✅ **EBCT vs MDCT:** MDCT (≥64 slice) has *largely replaced* EBCT in clinical cardiac imaging due to better spatial resolution and lower cost — mention this when comparing 5th and 7th gen.

> [!TIP]
> **Final 14-Mark Strategy:** For a generation-comparison question, always structure your answer as: (1) **diagram** of each generation, (2) **beam shape + detector count**, (3) **motion pattern**, (4) **scan time**, (5) **advantages**, (6) **limitations**, (7) **clinical relevance today**. A side-by-side **comparison table** at the end is a high-yield presentation tool that examiners reward.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Principles of Image Reconstruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT602 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles of Image Reconstruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Image Reconstruction

> [!NOTE]
> **Image Reconstruction** is the mathematical and computational process of generating a two-dimensional (2D) or three-dimensional (3D) spatial map of an object's internal structure from a series of one-dimensional (1D) projection measurements acquired at multiple angles around the object. In the context of medical imaging, this process is fundamental to modalities such as **Computed Tomography (CT)**, **Positron Emission Tomography (PET)**, **Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography (SPECT)**, and **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)**.

In a formal KTU 2024 syllabus sense, image reconstruction refers to the algorithmic inversion of the **Radon Transform** (the forward projection model) to recover the linear attenuation coefficient distribution $\mu(x, y)$ of a tissue slice from a set of sinogram measurements $P(\rho, \theta)$.

The forward model is given by:

$$P(\rho, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x, y) \, \delta(x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta - \rho) \, dx \, dy$$

where $f(x, y)$ is the 2D image to be reconstructed, $\rho$ is the perpendicular distance of the ray from the origin (the **radial coordinate**), and $\theta$ is the projection angle. The reconstruction problem is to invert this mapping.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Tomographic Puzzle" Analogy**
> Imagine you have a transparent cake with hidden fruit pieces inside. You cannot see them directly. However, you can shine a thin laser beam across the cake and measure the total "fruit density" the beam encounters along its straight path. By rotating the laser around the cake at 360° and recording measurements at hundreds of angles, you collect a huge dataset of "line integrals." Image reconstruction is the mathematical "unscrambling" process that uses these 1D line sums to compute where the fruit must be located. Mathematically, you have the *sums* (the projections) and you want the *summands* (the local densities) — this is the classic inverse problem.

## 1.3 Standard Constants and Metrics

- **Spatial Resolution:** typically $\mathbf{0.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \text{ mm}}$ for clinical CT.
- **Sampling rate (Nyquist):** must be $\geq 2 \times$ the highest spatial frequency in the object.
- **Number of projections:** governed by the **Crowther criterion**, $N_\theta \geq \pi \cdot N_p / 2$, where $N_p$ is the number of detector samples per projection.
- **Hounsfield Unit (HU):** the standardized attenuation scale for CT, defined as:

$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}$$

where $\mu$ is the linear attenuation coefficient of the voxel and $\mu_{water}$ is that of water at the same beam energy.

## 1.4 Scope of the Topic

This topic covers the **forward projection model (Radon Transform)**, its Fourier-domain dual (**Projection-Slice / Central-Slice Theorem**), and the principal analytical and algebraic reconstruction methods used to invert it. Mastery of this material underpins every quantitative imaging system in modern radiology.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Forward Projection Model — Radon Transform

The **Radon Transform** $\mathcal{R}\{f(x, y)\}$ of a 2D function $f(x, y)$ is defined as the integral of $f$ along a straight line parameterized by $(\rho, \theta)$:

$$\mathcal{R}\{f\}(\rho, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(\rho \cos\theta - s \sin\theta, \rho \sin\theta + s \cos\theta) \, ds$$

A more compact form uses the Dirac delta:

$$\mathcal{R}\{f\}(\rho, \theta) = \iint f(x, y) \, \delta(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta - \rho) \, dx \, dy$$

**Why this matters:** in CT, an X-ray tube emits a fan or pencil beam, the detector measures $I = I_0 \, e^{-\int \mu \, ds}$, and after logarithmic conversion we obtain exactly $\int \mu \, ds$ — the Radon transform of $\mu(x, y)$.

## 2.2 The Projection-Slice Theorem (Central Slice Theorem)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Projection-Slice Theorem (PST)** is the cornerstone of analytical image reconstruction. It states that the 1D Fourier Transform of a parallel-beam projection $P(\rho, \theta)$ acquired at angle $\theta$ is equal to a 1D radial slice through the 2D Fourier Transform of the object $F(u, v)$, taken along the same angle $\theta$.

Mathematically:

$$\mathcal{F}_1\{P(\rho, \theta)\} = F(\rho \cos\theta, \rho \sin\theta) = F(k, \theta)$$

where $k$ is the spatial frequency along the radial line and $F(u, v) = \mathcal{F}_2\{f(x, y)\}$ is the 2D Fourier transform.

**Why it matters:** it bridges the spatial domain (where projections live) and the frequency domain (where reconstruction is sometimes performed). It also explains **why interpolation in Fourier space is unavoidable** when we move between polar and Cartesian sampling grids.

## 2.3 The Inverse Radon Transform

Combining the PST with the 2D inverse Fourier Transform in polar coordinates gives the **Inverse Radon Transform**:

$$f(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(\rho, \theta) \, |k| \, e^{j 2\pi k (x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta)} \, dk \, d\theta$$

The factor $|k|$ is the **ramp filter** (also called the **Ram-Lak filter**), which is essential to undo the $1/k$ density-of-sampling bias introduced when sampling 2D Fourier space in polar coordinates.

## 2.4 Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)

FBP is the practical realization of the inverse Radon transform. It consists of two steps:

1. **Filtering step:** each projection $P(\rho, \theta)$ is convolved with the spatial-domain equivalent of $|k|$, denoted $h(\rho)$:

$$\tilde{P}(\rho, \theta) = P(\rho, \theta) * h(\rho) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(\rho', \theta) \, h(\rho - \rho') \, d\rho'$$

2. **Back-projection step:** the filtered projection is "smeared" back across the image plane at angle $\theta$:

$$f(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} \tilde{P}(x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta, \theta) \, d\theta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the filter?** A naive back-projection (without filtering) reconstructs a blurred image — the point spread function is the famous $1/r$ blur. The ramp filter $|k|$ in frequency space (or its spatial-domain equivalent $h(\rho)$) is the exact deconvolution kernel that sharpens the image back to the correct delta-function point-spread response.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name / Use |
|---|----------|------------|
| 1 | $\mathcal{R}\{f\}(\rho, \theta) = \iint f(x, y) \delta(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta - \rho) dx\,dy$ | Radon Transform (forward projection) |
| 2 | $\mathcal{F}_1\{P(\rho, \theta)\} = F(\rho \cos\theta, \rho \sin\theta)$ | Projection-Slice Theorem |
| 3 | $H(k) = \vert k \vert$ | Ramp filter (frequency domain) |
| 4 | $h(\rho) = \dfrac{1}{2 (\Delta \rho)^2}\left[\text{sinc}\left(\dfrac{\pi \rho}{\Delta \rho}\right) - \text{sinc}^2\left(\dfrac{\pi \rho}{2\Delta \rho}\right)\right]$ | Ram-Lak filter (spatial domain, sampled) |
| 5 | $f(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} \tilde{P}(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta, \theta) d\theta$ | Back-projection integral |
| 6 | $HU = 1000 \times \dfrac{\mu - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}$ | Hounsfield Unit conversion |
| 7 | $N_\theta \geq \dfrac{\pi N_p}{2}$ | Crowther sampling criterion |
| 8 | $f_{Nyquist} = \dfrac{1}{2 \Delta \rho}$ | Maximum reconstructable frequency |
| 9 | $\int_0^{\pi} P(\rho, \theta) d\theta = \text{constant}$ | Radon identity (uniform disc) |
| 10 | $B(\rho, \theta) = \int_0^{\pi} P(\rho, \theta) d\theta$ | Unfiltered back-projection |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical CT scanners** in every hospital use FBP (or iterative variants) — the same mathematics described above.
- **PET/SPECT** systems use the related **MLEM/OSEM** iterative algorithms.
- **Industrial non-destructive testing** uses tomographic reconstruction to inspect turbine blades and rocket motors.
- **Electron microscopy** in materials science applies weighted back-projection to recover 3D structures from tilted 2D projections.
- **Geophysics / seismic imaging** uses the same Radon-inversion mathematics to map subsurface structures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Projection-Slice Theorem

We begin with the parallel-beam projection:

$$P(\rho, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x, y) \, \delta(x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta - \rho) \, dx \, dy$$

Take the 1D Fourier Transform of $P$ with respect to $\rho$:

$$S(k, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(\rho, \theta) \, e^{-j 2\pi k \rho} \, d\rho$$

Substitute the expression for $P$:

$$S(k, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x, y) \, \delta(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta - \rho) \, dx \, dy \right] e^{-j 2\pi k \rho} d\rho$$

Swap the order of integration (Fubini's theorem, justified since $f$ is band-limited):

$$S(k, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x, y) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta - \rho) \, e^{-j 2\pi k \rho} d\rho \right] dx \, dy$$

Use the sifting property of the Dirac delta, $\int g(\rho) \, \delta(\rho - \rho_0) d\rho = g(\rho_0)$:

$$S(k, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi k (x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta)} \, dx \, dy$$

Recognize the right-hand side as the 2D Fourier Transform of $f(x, y)$ evaluated along the radial line $(\rho\cos\theta, \rho\sin\theta)$:

$$S(k, \theta) = F(k \cos\theta, k \sin\theta)$$

**Conclusion:** This is the Projection-Slice Theorem. Q.E.D.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Inverse Radon Transform

Starting from the 2D inverse Fourier Transform in Cartesian form:

$$f(x, y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F(u, v) \, e^{j 2\pi (u x + v y)} \, du \, dv$$

Change variables to polar coordinates: $u = k \cos\theta$, $v = k \sin\theta$, with the Jacobian $|J| = k$:

$$f(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F(k \cos\theta, k \sin\theta) \, e^{j 2\pi k (x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta)} \, |k| \, dk \, d\theta$$

By the PST, $F(k \cos\theta, k \sin\theta) = \mathcal{F}_1\{P(\rho, \theta)\} = S(k, \theta)$. Substituting:

$$f(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S(k, \theta) \, |k| \, e^{j 2\pi k (x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta)} \, dk \, d\theta$$

Recognize the inner integral as the inverse 1D Fourier Transform of $S(k, \theta) \cdot |k|$, which equals the convolution $P(\rho, \theta) * h(\rho)$ evaluated at $\rho = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta$:

$$f(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} \left[ P(\rho, \theta) * h(\rho) \right]_{\rho = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta} \, d\theta$$

Defining $\tilde{P}(\rho, \theta) = P(\rho, \theta) * h(\rho)$, we arrive at the canonical FBP formula:

$$\boxed{f(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} \tilde{P}(x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta, \theta) \, d\theta}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Ramp Filter Response

The ramp filter $|k|$ is not directly realizable (it has infinite energy). In practice, we apply a **band-limited ramp**:

$$H(k) = |k| \cdot \text{rect}\left(\frac{k}{2 k_c}\right)$$

where $k_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \Delta \rho}$ is the Nyquist frequency. The spatial-domain equivalent via inverse Fourier Transform:

$$h(\rho) = \int_{-k_c}^{k_c} |k| \, e^{j 2\pi k \rho} \, dk = 2 \int_0^{k_c} k \cos(2 \pi k \rho) \, dk$$

Evaluating:

$$h(\rho) = 2 \left[ \frac{k \sin(2\pi k \rho)}{2\pi \rho} + \frac{\cos(2\pi k \rho)}{(2\pi \rho)^2} \right]_0^{k_c}$$

$$h(\rho) = 2 \left[ \frac{k_c \sin(2\pi k_c \rho)}{2\pi \rho} + \frac{\cos(2\pi k_c \rho) - 1}{(2\pi \rho)^2} \right]$$

With $k_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \Delta \rho}$ and simplifying using sinc notation, this becomes the discrete **Ram-Lak** kernel listed in the formula sheet.

## 3.4 Discretized FBP Algorithm (Standard Implementation)

1. Acquire projections $P[m, n]$ for $m = 0, \ldots, M-1$ detector bins and $n = 0, \ldots, N-1$ angles.
2. Apply 1D FFT along the detector axis: $S[k, n] = \text{FFT}_1\{P[m, n]\}$.
3. Multiply by the ramp filter (possibly with a window): $\tilde{S}[k, n] = S[k, n] \cdot H[k]$.
4. Apply 1D IFFT: $\tilde{P}[m, n] = \text{IFFT}_1\{\tilde{S}[k, n]\}$.
5. Back-project: $f[i, j] = \dfrac{\pi}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \tilde{P}[i \cos\theta_n + j \sin\theta_n, n]$.
6. Scale by $M \Delta \rho$ to account for detector pitch and finite angular range.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Image Reconstruction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Object f of x y] --> B[Parallel Beam Projection]
    B --> C[Sinogram P of rho theta]
    C --> D[Logarithm and Pre-processing]
    D --> E[1D FFT along detector axis]
    E --> F[Multiply by Ramp Filter H of k]
    F --> G[1D IFFT to get filtered projection]
    G --> H[Back-project over all angles theta]
    H --> I[Reconstructed Image f hat of x y]
    I --> J[Display or Quantification HU]
```

## 4.2 Forward vs Inverse Transform Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORWARD[Forward Model]
        F1[Object f of x y] --> F2[Radon Transform]
        F2 --> F3[Projection P of rho theta]
    end
    subgraph INVERSE[Inverse Reconstruction]
        I1[Projection P of rho theta] --> I2[1D FFT]
        I2 --> I3[Ramp Filter multiplication]
        I3 --> I4[1D IFFT]
        I4 --> I5[Back-project over theta]
        I5 --> I6[Reconstructed f hat of x y]
    end
    F3 -. acquired data .-> I1
    F1 -. ground truth .-> I6
```

## 4.3 Frequency-Domain View via Projection-Slice Theorem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[2D Fourier Transform F of u v] --> B[Polar Grid Sampling]
    B --> C[Each angle theta gives one radial line in F]
    C --> D[1D FFT of Projection P of rho theta]
    D --> E[Identical to radial slice of F at angle theta]
    E --> F[Interpolate to Cartesian Grid]
    F --> G[2D IFFT for Direct Fourier Reconstruction]
    G --> H[Reconstructed Image]
```

## 4.4 Algebraic Reconstruction (ART) Iterative Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize f to zero or prior] --> B[Project forward to get simulated p]
    B --> C[Compare with measured p measured]
    C --> D[Compute residual r = p measured minus p simulated]
    D --> E[Back-project residual update delta f]
    E --> F[Update estimate f new = f old plus lambda times delta f]
    F --> G{Convergence criterion met}
    G -- No --> B
    G -- Yes --> H[Final Reconstructed Image]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the Projection-Slice Theorem. Why is it the cornerstone of CT image reconstruction?**

**Model Answer:**

The Projection-Slice Theorem (also called the Central Slice Theorem) states that the 1D Fourier Transform of a parallel-beam projection of an object, taken at angle $\theta$, is identical to a 1D radial slice through the 2D Fourier Transform of the same object, evaluated along the same angle $\theta$.

Mathematically: $\mathcal{F}_1\{P(\rho, \theta)\} = F(\rho \cos\theta, \rho \sin\theta)$.

**Why cornerstone:**
- It connects the **spatial domain** (where the projection data is acquired) to the **frequency domain** (where inversion is mathematically natural).
- It is the foundation of **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)**, the most widely used clinical reconstruction algorithm.
- It also enables **direct Fourier reconstruction** by interpolation.

The theorem is what makes it possible to recover $f(x, y)$ from $P(\rho, \theta)$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between simple back-projection and filtered back-projection. Why is a filter necessary in FBP?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Simple Back-Projection | Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) |
|---|---|---|
| Filter applied? | **No** | **Yes (Ramp filter $|k|$)** |
| Image quality | **Blurred** ($1/r$ PSF) | **Sharp** (delta PSF) |
| Frequency content | Falls off as $1/k$ (low-pass biased) | Restored to flat spectrum |
| Mathematical form | $f_{blurred} = \int_0^\pi P d\theta$ | $f = \int_0^\pi \tilde{P} d\theta$, with $\tilde{P} = P * h$ |
| Clinical use | **Obsolete** | **Standard** in CT |

**Why a filter is necessary:** When projections are back-projected without filtering, the 2D Fourier Transform of the result is multiplied by $1/|k|$, producing a characteristic $1/r$ blur of every point. The ramp filter $|k|$ exactly compensates this $1/|k|$ attenuation, restoring the true spatial frequency content of the object.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for KTU 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the forward and inverse Radon transform in image reconstruction. Derive the expression for the inverse Radon transform starting from the Projection-Slice Theorem. **  (7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a parallel-beam CT system with detector spacing $\Delta \rho = 1 \text{ mm}$ and 360 projections uniformly distributed over 180°, compute:
   (i) the Nyquist-limited maximum spatial frequency, and
   (ii) the minimum number of projections required by the Crowther criterion for a 512-detector array.
   Comment on the implications for sampling. **  (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Block diagram** (as in Section 4.2). The forward Radon transform maps a 2D image to its 1D line integrals $P(\rho, \theta)$, while the inverse Radon transform maps a complete set of projections back to the image.

**Derivation** of the inverse Radon transform:

Start with the 2D inverse Fourier Transform of $f(x, y)$:

$$f(x, y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F(u, v) \, e^{j 2\pi (u x + v y)} \, du \, dv$$

Convert to polar coordinates $u = k \cos\theta$, $v = k \sin\theta$, $du\, dv = k \, dk \, d\theta$:

$$f(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F(k\cos\theta, k\sin\theta) \, e^{j 2\pi k (x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta)} \, k \, dk \, d\theta$$

Apply the PST, $F(k\cos\theta, k\sin\theta) = \mathcal{F}_1\{P(\rho, \theta)\} = S(k, \theta)$:

$$f(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S(k, \theta) \, |k| \, e^{j 2\pi k (x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta)} \, dk \, d\theta$$

Recognize the inner integral as the 1D IFFT of $S(k,\theta) \cdot |k|$, which gives the filtered projection $\tilde{P}(\rho, \theta) = (P * h)(\rho, \theta)$ evaluated at $\rho = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta$:

$$\boxed{f(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} \tilde{P}(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta, \theta) \, d\theta}$$

#### Model Solution (b):

**(i) Nyquist frequency:**

$$f_{Nyquist} = \frac{1}{2 \Delta \rho} = \frac{1}{2 \times 1 \text{ mm}} = 0.5 \text{ cycles/mm}$$

**(ii) Crowther criterion:**

$$N_\theta \geq \frac{\pi N_p}{2} = \frac{\pi \times 512}{2} = 256\pi \approx 804.25$$

Rounding up, $N_\theta = 805$ projections. Since the system has 360 projections (over 180°), it is **slightly under-sampled angularly**, which may lead to **streak artefacts** in the reconstruction. Doubling to 720 projections (over 360° in 1° steps) would satisfy the criterion with margin.

**Implications:** angular under-sampling causes radial streak artefacts; detector under-sampling causes aliasing and limits spatial resolution. Both must be respected for artefact-free reconstruction.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified for KTU 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of the Radon transform. For a 2D phantom consisting of a single point source of unit intensity at $(x_0, y_0)$, derive the resulting sinogram expression $P(\rho, \theta)$. **  (7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various sources of artefacts in FBP reconstruction. How do the choice of filter (Shepp-Logan, Hamming, Hanning) and the number of projections influence image quality? **  (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Radon Transform Concept:** The Radon transform of a 2D function $f(x, y)$ is the integral of $f$ along all possible straight lines in the plane, parameterized by perpendicular distance $\rho$ and angle $\theta$. In CT, the line integral is the logarithmic attenuation measurement.

**Derivation for a point source:**

Consider $f(x, y) = \delta(x - x_0) \, \delta(y - y_0)$. Substituting into the Radon transform:

$$P(\rho, \theta) = \iint \delta(x - x_0) \, \delta(y - y_0) \, \delta(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta - \rho) \, dx \, dy$$

Integrate over $x$ first using $\delta(x - x_0)$:

$$P(\rho, \theta) = \int \delta(y - y_0) \, \delta(x_0 \cos\theta + y \sin\theta - \rho) \, dy$$

Then integrate over $y$ using $\delta(y - y_0)$:

$$P(\rho, \theta) = \delta(x_0 \cos\theta + y_0 \sin\theta - \rho)$$

**Interpretation:** the sinogram of a single point is a **sinusoidal curve** $P(\rho, \theta) = \delta(\rho - x_0 \cos\theta - y_0 \sin\theta)$, oscillating in $\rho$ as $\theta$ varies, with amplitude $\sqrt{x_0^2 + y_0^2}$ and phase related to the point's location. This is the origin of the term "sinogram."

#### Model Solution (b):

**Artefact Sources in FBP:**

1. **Aliasing** — caused by insufficient detector sampling. Solution: finer detectors or anti-aliasing pre-filtering.
2. **Angular under-sampling** — produces **streak / star artefacts** radiating from high-contrast edges.
3. **Beam hardening** — polychromatic X-rays mean low-energy photons are absorbed preferentially, causing cupping artefacts and dark streaks between bones.
4. **Scatter** — adds a low-frequency haze.
5. **Motion** — patient movement produces blurring or double-edge artefacts.
6. **Noise** — quantum (Poisson) and electronic noise; FBP amplifies high-frequency noise.
7. **Partial volume** — finite voxel size blurs boundaries.

**Filter Choice and Image Quality:**

| Filter | Frequency response | Image appearance |
|---|---|---|
| **Ram-Lak** ($|k|$ rect) | Pure ramp, sharpest | Maximum resolution, maximum noise |
| **Shepp-Logan** ($|k| \text{sinc}$) | Ramp × sinc | Slightly smoother, classic CT default |
| **Hamming / Hanning** | Ramp × raised cosine | Smoothest, best for low-dose, blurred edges |
| **Cosine / Parzen** | Ramp × smooth window | Lowest noise, lowest resolution |

**Number of projections:**

- Following the **Crowther criterion** $N_\theta \geq \pi N_p / 2$ avoids streak artefacts.
- More projections → less angular aliasing, but more dose and longer scan.
- Fewer projections → more streaks, but lower dose (low-dose CT).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Reconstruction Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Jacobian factor $|k|$** in the polar-coordinate change of variables during the inverse Radon derivation. KTU examiners explicitly test this; losing it costs **2 marks** easily.
> 2. **Confusing the Projection-Slice Theorem statement** — many students write the *2D* Fourier transform of the projection instead of the *1D* Fourier transform. Always specify "1D FFT along the detector axis."
> 3. **Sign errors** in the sifting property of the Dirac delta: $\delta(a - b)$ vs $\delta(b - a)$. These are equivalent, but you must be consistent.
> 4. **Skipping the filter justification** — a complete answer to "Why a ramp filter?" must mention (a) the $1/|k|$ bias introduced by polar sampling and (b) the $1/r$ blur it removes.
> 5. **Not specifying units** for Nyquist frequency, Crowther counts, or HU.
> 6. **In numerical parts**, students often confuse the projection count with the angular range. With 360 projections **over 180°**, the angular step is $\Delta \theta = 180°/360 = 0.5°$, which is **not the same** as 360 projections over 360°.
> 7. **Failing to draw the block diagram** in 7-mark derivation questions — block diagrams carry **at least 1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Image reconstruction** = inverse problem of recovering $f(x, y)$ from $P(\rho, \theta)$.
- **Radon transform** = forward model; **Inverse Radon / FBP** = inversion.
- **Projection-Slice Theorem**: 1D FT of projection = radial slice of 2D FT of object.
- **Ramp filter $|k|$** is mandatory in FBP to undo the $1/|k|$ density bias of polar Fourier sampling.
- **Ram-Lak** filter is the canonical spatial-domain implementation; **Shepp-Logan** and **Hamming** are smoother variants.
- **Nyquist frequency** = $1/(2 \Delta \rho)$; **Crowther criterion** = $N_\theta \geq \pi N_p / 2$.
- **Sinogram of a point** is a sinusoid — origin of the word "sinogram."
- **Back-projection alone** (without filter) gives $1/r$ blur — clinically unusable.
- **FBP** = filter each projection, then back-project and integrate over $[0, \pi]$.
- **Discrete FBP algorithm** has 5 steps: FFT → ramp filter → IFFT → back-project → scale.
- **CT numbers** are quantified in **Hounsfield Units (HU)** with water = 0 HU, air = −1000 HU.
- **Iterative methods (ART, MLEM, OSEM)** are alternatives to FBP, dominant in PET/SPECT.
- **Artefacts**: streaks (under-sampling), cupping (beam hardening), noise (quantum + ramp amplification).
- **Filter trade-off**: sharper filter → more resolution, more noise; smoother filter → less noise, blurred edges.
- **Number of projections trade-off**: more → better quality, more dose; fewer → streaks, less dose.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic Imaging: Physical Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT602 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Imaging: Physical Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultrasonic Imaging** is a non-invasive, real-time diagnostic medical imaging modality that uses **high-frequency mechanical sound waves (ultrasound)** typically in the frequency range of **1 MHz to 20 MHz**, well above the human audible threshold of **20 kHz**. The technique is based on the **pulse-echo principle**, where short bursts of acoustic energy are transmitted into biological tissue using a **piezoelectric transducer**, and the returning echoes (produced by acoustic impedance mismatches at tissue interfaces) are processed to construct a visual map of internal anatomical structures.

The image contrast in ultrasound does **not** arise from X-ray absorption (as in CT) or nuclear magnetic resonance (as in MRI) but from differences in the **mechanical/acoustic properties** of tissues — primarily density (ρ) and the velocity of sound propagation (c) — encapsulated in the parameter **Acoustic Impedance (Z)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sonar in a Bathtub" Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a long, dark tunnel and you clap your hands once. The echo that returns to you carries information: if it comes back quickly, the wall is close; if it returns later, the wall is far. If the echo is loud, the wall is hard (concrete); if it is faint, the wall is soft (curtains).

**Ultrasound works exactly the same way**, except:
- The "clap" is a tightly focused, millisecond-long **pulse of high-frequency sound** (millions of cycles per second), generated by a vibrating crystal.
- The "tunnel" is your body — a complex layered medium of skin, fat, muscle, blood, and bone.
- The "echoes" are partial reflections that occur whenever the sound wave crosses a boundary between two tissues of different acoustic impedance.
- A microsecond-precise **stopwatch** (the scanner's timing electronics) measures the round-trip time, and a computer converts that time into depth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Module 1 Focus**
> Module 1 is purely concerned with the **PHYSICAL PRINCIPLES** (wave physics, acoustic impedance, reflection, attenuation, transducer physics) — NOT with image reconstruction algorithms, Doppler, or clinical applications (which appear in later modules).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Diagnostic Ranges

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Audible sound upper limit | $f_{aud}$ | **20,000 (20 kHz)** | Hz |
| Diagnostic ultrasound range | $f_{diag}$ | **1 – 20** | MHz |
| Therapeutic ultrasound range | $f_{ther}$ | **0.8 – 3** | MHz |
| Speed of sound in soft tissue (avg) | $c$ | **1540** | m/s |
| Speed of sound in air | $c_{air}$ | **330** | m/s |
| Speed of sound in bone | $c_{bone}$ | **4080** | m/s |
| Speed of sound in fat | $c_{fat}$ | **1450** | m/s |
| Speed of sound in blood | $c_{blood}$ | **1570** | m/s |
| Diagnostic intensity (SPTA) | $I$ | **0.01 – 0.1** | W/cm² |
| Attenuation in soft tissue | $\alpha$ | **0.5 – 1.0** | dB/cm/MHz |

> [!NOTE]
> The value **1540 m/s** is the KTU-board-standard textbook average used universally for **all time-of-flight depth calculations** in soft-tissue ultrasound, unless a specific medium is named.

### 1.4 Why Ultrasound? — Clinical Motivation

- **Non-ionizing** (no X-ray radiation risk) — safe for **pregnant women and fetuses**.
- **Real-time** imaging (frame rates up to **50–100 fps**).
- **Portable** and inexpensive compared to CT/MRI.
- **Echocardiography, obstetrics, abdominal imaging, vascular Doppler** are routine applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Nature of the Ultrasound Wave

A medical ultrasound wave is a **longitudinal mechanical wave** — the particles of the medium oscillate **parallel to the direction of wave propagation**, producing alternating regions of **compression** (high pressure) and **rarefaction** (low pressure).

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike X-rays or visible light, ultrasound **cannot travel through a vacuum** — it requires a physical medium. This is why a coupling gel (acoustic gel) is used between the transducer and the patient's skin: to expel air, which would otherwise reflect **99.9 %** of the beam.

The general one-dimensional wave equation governing propagation in a lossless medium is:

$$\frac{\partial^2 p(x,t)}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 p(x,t)}{\partial t^2}$$

where $p(x,t)$ is the acoustic pressure at position $x$ and time $t$, and $c$ is the speed of sound.

### 2.2 Key Wave Parameters — The Building Blocks

- **Frequency (f):** Number of pressure cycles per second, measured in **Hz**.
- **Period (T):** Time for one cycle, $T = 1/f$.
- **Wavelength (λ):** Spatial length of one cycle, $\lambda = c / f$.
- **Acoustic Pressure (p):** Instantaneous pressure deviation from ambient (units: **Pa**).
- **Acoustic Intensity (I):** Time-averaged energy flux per unit area (units: **W/m²** or **W/cm²**).
- **Particle Velocity (u):** Velocity of medium particles during oscillation (units: **m/s**).

### 2.3 Acoustic Impedance (Z) — The Central Concept

Acoustic impedance is the **most important physical parameter in ultrasound imaging**, because it is the mismatch in Z between adjacent tissues that produces the diagnostic echo.

$$Z = \rho \cdot c$$

where $\rho$ is the **density** of the medium (kg/m³) and $c$ is the **speed of sound** (m/s). Units: **Rayl** (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹) or **kg/(m²·s)**.

| Tissue | Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | Speed $c$ (m/s) | Acoustic Impedance $Z$ (× 10⁶ Rayl) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air | 1.2 | 330 | **0.0004** |
| Fat | 952 | 1450 | **1.38** |
| Water (20 °C) | 998 | 1480 | **1.48** |
| Blood | 1060 | 1570 | **1.66** |
| Muscle | 1080 | 1580 | **1.70** |
| Kidney | 1040 | 1560 | **1.62** |
| Liver | 1060 | 1550 | **1.64** |
| Bone (cortical) | 1910 | 4080 | **7.80** |

### 2.4 Reflection, Transmission & the Reflection Coefficient

When an ultrasound wave strikes a planar boundary between two tissues with acoustic impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$, part of the energy is **reflected** (echo) and part is **transmitted** (continues deeper).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a wave striking at **normal incidence** (perpendicular to the boundary), the **Intensity Reflection Coefficient** $\alpha_R$ and **Intensity Transmission Coefficient** $\alpha_T$ are:

$$\alpha_R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2 \quad ; \quad \alpha_T = \frac{4 \, Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_2 + Z_1)^2}$$

These satisfy the **conservation of energy**: $\alpha_R + \alpha_T = 1$.

The corresponding **Pressure Reflection Coefficient** (amplitude ratio, not intensity) is:

$$R_p = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \quad ; \quad T_p = \frac{2 Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — The High-Yield Set

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete, must-memorize formula set** for Module 1 problems. KTU questions almost always draw from this list.

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $c = f \cdot \lambda$ | Wave equation linking speed, frequency, wavelength |
| 2 | $Z = \rho c$ | Acoustic impedance definition |
| 3 | $\alpha_R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | Fraction of intensity reflected at boundary |
| 4 | $\alpha_T = \dfrac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_2 + Z_1)^2}$ | Fraction of intensity transmitted |
| 5 | $I(z) = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\alpha z / 10}$ | Attenuation of intensity with depth $z$ (α in dB/cm) |
| 6 | $I(d) = I_0 \cdot e^{-2 \mu d}$ | Exponential attenuation (μ in Np/cm) |
| 7 | $\alpha_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_0}{p}\right)$ | Attenuation in dB (pressure form) |
| 8 | $\alpha_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | Attenuation in dB (intensity form) |
| 9 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Depth of reflector (round-trip time $t$) |
| 10 | $L_{axial} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$ | Axial (range) resolution |
| 11 | $L_{lateral} = \dfrac{1.22 \cdot \lambda \cdot F}{D}$ | Lateral resolution (focal length F, aperture D) |
| 12 | $\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_{ref}}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level (dB) |

### 2.6 Attenuation — Why Deep Tissues Look Dark

As the beam propagates, its intensity decreases exponentially due to three mechanisms:

1. **Absorption** — conversion of acoustic energy to heat via viscous friction.
2. **Scattering** — redirection by small structures (e.g., red blood cells).
3. **Reflection** — losses at macroscopic interfaces.

The net effect is captured by the **attenuation coefficient** $\alpha$ (units: **dB/cm** or **dB/cm/MHz**). The latter form — **dB/cm/MHz** — accounts for the strong frequency dependence and is the **KTU-preferred form** for soft tissue, with a typical value of **0.5 dB/cm/MHz**.

### 2.7 Time-Gain Compensation (TGC) — The Engineering Countermeasure

Because attenuation makes deep echoes look weak, scanners use a depth-dependent amplifier called **Time-Gain Compensation (TGC)** that boosts signals from greater depths by an amount equal to the estimated round-trip attenuation loss. This restores the visual brightness of deep structures — a critical concept for image quality.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Intensity Reflection Coefficient at a Tissue Boundary

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Starting from Newton's 2nd law and the continuity equation for a 1-D longitudinal wave at a planar interface, derive the formula $\alpha_R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$.

**Step 1 — Set up the three waves at the interface.**

Consider a boundary at $x = 0$ separating medium 1 (left) from medium 2 (right). Let an incident wave of pressure amplitude $P_i$ travel to the right, a reflected wave of amplitude $P_r$ travel to the left (in medium 1), and a transmitted wave of amplitude $P_t$ travel to the right (in medium 2).

The total pressure in medium 1 is: $P_1(x,t) = P_i e^{j(\omega t - k_1 x)} + P_r e^{j(\omega t + k_1 x)}$.

The pressure in medium 2 is: $P_2(x,t) = P_t e^{j(\omega t - k_2 x)}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the boundary condition on pressure (continuity of pressure).**

At the boundary $x = 0$, the pressure must be continuous (no gap opens between media):

$$P_i + P_r = P_t$$

**Step 3 — Apply the boundary condition on particle velocity.**

Continuity of particle velocity (no slip / no tearing) gives:

$$u_i - u_r = u_t$$

where the minus sign on $u_r$ accounts for the reflected wave traveling in the $-x$ direction.

**Step 4 — Relate particle velocity to pressure via acoustic impedance.**

For a plane wave, the acoustic impedance relates pressure to velocity: $P = Z \cdot u$, so $u = P/Z$.

Substituting:

$$\frac{P_i}{Z_1} - \frac{P_r}{Z_1} = \frac{P_t}{Z_2}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the two linear equations.**

From Step 2: $P_r = P_t - P_i$.

Substitute into Step 4:

$$\frac{P_i}{Z_1} - \frac{P_t - P_i}{Z_1} = \frac{P_t}{Z_2}$$

$$\frac{2 P_i - P_t}{Z_1} = \frac{P_t}{Z_2}$$

Cross-multiply:

$$Z_2 (2 P_i - P_t) = Z_1 P_t$$

$$2 Z_2 P_i = Z_1 P_t + Z_2 P_t = P_t (Z_1 + Z_2)$$

$$P_t = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} \, P_i$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the reflected pressure amplitude.**

Using $P_r = P_t - P_i$:

$$P_r = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} P_i - P_i = \left( \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} - 1 \right) P_i$$

$$P_r = \frac{2 Z_2 - (Z_1 + Z_2)}{Z_1 + Z_2} P_i = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2} \, P_i$$

This is the **pressure reflection coefficient**:

$$R_p = \frac{P_r}{P_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to the intensity reflection coefficient.**

Intensity is proportional to the **square of pressure amplitude** (for a given Z): $I \propto P^2 / Z$.

$$\alpha_R = \frac{I_r}{I_i} = \frac{P_r^2 / Z_1}{P_i^2 / Z_1} = \left( \frac{P_r}{P_i} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2} \right)^2$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2} \right)^2}$$

This is the **fundamental result** of ultrasound physics at boundaries.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Reflection at a Fat–Muscle Interface

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem (8 marks equivalent):** A 5 MHz ultrasound beam travelling in fat strikes a fat–muscle interface at normal incidence. Given $Z_{fat} = 1.38 \times 10^6$ Rayl and $Z_{muscle} = 1.70 \times 10^6$ Rayl, compute the **intensity reflection coefficient** and the **percentage of intensity transmitted** into the muscle.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

Step 1 — Write the reflection coefficient formula:

$$\alpha_R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$

Step 2 — Substitute $Z_1 = 1.38 \times 10^6$ (fat) and $Z_2 = 1.70 \times 10^6$ (muscle):

$$Z_2 - Z_1 = (1.70 - 1.38) \times 10^6 = 0.32 \times 10^6 \text{ Rayl}$$

$$Z_2 + Z_1 = (1.70 + 1.38) \times 10^6 = 3.08 \times 10^6 \text{ Rayl}$$

Step 3 — Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} = \frac{0.32 \times 10^6}{3.08 \times 10^6} = 0.1039$$

Step 4 — Square it to get $\alpha_R$:

$$\alpha_R = (0.1039)^2 = 0.01080 \approx 1.08\%$$

Step 5 — Compute the transmission coefficient using energy conservation:

$$\alpha_T = 1 - \alpha_R = 1 - 0.0108 = 0.9892 \approx 98.92\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Only ~1 % of the intensity is reflected at the fat–muscle boundary, while ~99 % is transmitted. This is why **most soft-tissue interfaces are weak echo generators** — but the small reflected fraction is sufficient because the scanner is exquisitely sensitive (~−100 dB dynamic range).

### 3.3 Worked Example — Air–Skin Interface (The Gel Demonstration)

Same problem, but now $Z_1 = Z_{air} = 0.0004 \times 10^6$ Rayl and $Z_2 = Z_{skin} \approx 1.60 \times 10^6$ Rayl:

$$\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \approx \frac{1.60 - 0.0004}{1.60 + 0.0004} \approx 0.9995$$

$$\alpha_R = (0.9995)^2 \approx 0.999 \quad \text{i.e.,} \quad 99.9\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is why the coupling gel is MANDATORY.** Without it, **99.9 % of the ultrasound energy is reflected by the air gap**, and the scanner sees nothing of the tissue beneath. The gel (with $Z \approx 1.5 \times 10^6$ Rayl, close to skin) eliminates this impedance mismatch.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Depth Calculation Using Round-Trip Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem:** An ultrasound pulse is transmitted into soft tissue. An echo returns **39 microseconds** later. Calculate the depth of the reflecting interface. (Use $c = 1540$ m/s.)

**Solution:**

The pulse travels **to the reflector and back**, so the total distance is $c \cdot t$ and the one-way depth $d$ is half of that:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{1540 \text{ m/s} \times 39 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{1540 \times 39 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{60{,}060 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 30{,}030 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

$$d = 0.030 \text{ m} = 3.0 \text{ cm}$$

The reflector lies at a depth of **3 cm**.

### 3.5 Worked Example — Attenuation with Depth

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem:** A 4 MHz ultrasound beam with initial intensity $10$ mW/cm² travels through soft tissue ($\alpha = 0.7$ dB/cm/MHz). Find the intensity at **8 cm** depth.

**Solution:**

The total attenuation coefficient in dB/cm is $\alpha_{total} = 0.7 \times 4 = 2.8$ dB/cm.

Total attenuation over 8 cm: $2.8 \times 8 = 22.4$ dB.

Using $I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\alpha_{dB\,total} / 10}$:

$$I(8) = 10 \times 10^{-22.4 / 10} = 10 \times 10^{-2.24}$$

$$10^{-2.24} = 10^{-3} \times 10^{0.76} = 0.001 \times 5.75 = 5.75 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$I(8) = 10 \times 5.75 \times 10^{-3} = 5.75 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mW/cm}^2 = 0.0575 \text{ mW/cm}^2$$

The intensity has dropped to **~0.058 mW/cm²** — a ~174-fold reduction. This is exactly why **TGC** is applied to brighten deep echoes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Ultrasonic Imaging Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PulseGen["Pulse Generator"] --> TransmitSw["Transmit Receive Switch"]
    TransmitSw --> Transducer["Piezoelectric Transducer"]
    Transducer -->|Acoustic Pulse| BodyTissue["Patient Body Tissue"]
    BodyTissue -->|Reflected Echo| Transducer
    Transducer --> TransmitSw
    TransmitSw --> Preamp["Pre Amplifier Low Noise"]
    Preamp --> TGC["Time Gain Compensation Amplifier"]
    TGC --> Detector["Envelope Detector"]
    Detector --> ADC["Analog to Digital Converter"]
    ADC --> DSP["Digital Signal Processing Scan Conversion"]
    DSP --> Display["Display Monitor B Mode Image"]
    MClock["Master Clock Timing"] -.-> PulseGen
    MClock -.-> TGC
    MClock -.-> ADC
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Reflection and Transmission at a Tissue Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TransducerSource["Transducer Acoustic Source"] -->|Incident Wave Ii| InterfaceNode["Tissue 1 to Tissue 2 Interface"]
    InterfaceNode -->|Reflected Wave Ir Echo| TransducerSource
    InterfaceNode -->|Transmitted Wave It Deeper| DeeperTissue["Deeper Tissue Layers"]
    Z1Note["Z1 rho1 times c1"] -.-> InterfaceNode
    Z2Note["Z2 rho2 times c2"] -.-> InterfaceNode
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Piezoelectric Transducer Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ElecInput["High Voltage Electrical Pulse"] --> ElectrodeTop["Top Electrode"]
    ElectrodeTop --> PZTCrystal["Piezoelectric Crystal PZT Lead Zirconate Titanate"]
    PZTCrystal -->|Mech Vibration| MatchingLayer["Acoustic Matching Layer"]
    MatchingLayer -->|Couples to Tissue| TissueOut["Body Tissue"]
    ElectrodeBot["Bottom Electrode"] --> PZTCrystal
    DampingMat["Backing Damping Material"] --> PZTCrystal
    DampingMat -.->|Absorbs Backward Vibration| DampingMat
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Attenuation Mechanisms and Their Physical Origin

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InitialBeam["Incident Ultrasound Beam Intensity I0"] --> Absorpt["ABSORPTION Viscous Friction converts to heat"]
    InitialBeam --> Scatter["SCATTERING Redirection by small particles RBCs"]
    InitialBeam --> ReflectLoss["REFLECTION Loss at tissue interfaces"]
    Absorpt --> AttenuatedBeam["Attenuated Beam after depth z"]
    Scatter --> AttenuatedBeam
    ReflectLoss --> AttenuatedBeam
    AttenuatedBeam --> TGCBlock["Compensated by TGC in scanner"]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Relationship Between Acoustic Impedance Mismatch and Echo Strength

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DeltaZ["Delta Z equals absolute value of Z2 minus Z1"] -->|Large Mismatch| StrongEcho["STRONG ECHO Bone Air Interfaces"]
    DeltaZ -->|Small Mismatch| WeakEcho["WEAK ECHO Most Soft Tissue Interfaces"]
    DeltaZ -->|Near Zero| NoEcho["NO ECHO Homogeneous Fluid Like Urine"]
    StrongEcho --> BModeBright["Bright Pixel on B Mode Image"]
    WeakEcho --> BModeGray["Gray Level Pixel on B Mode Image"]
    NoEcho --> BModeDark["Dark Anechoic Region on B Mode Image"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define acoustic impedance. Why is it the most important physical property governing ultrasound image contrast?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Acoustic impedance ($Z$) of a tissue is defined as the product of its physical density ($\rho$) and the speed of sound ($c$) in that tissue:

$$Z = \rho \cdot c$$

It is measured in **Rayl** (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹).

**Importance for image contrast (1.5 marks):** Ultrasound image contrast does NOT arise from absorption (as in X-rays), but from the **partial reflection of the ultrasound beam at boundaries** between adjacent tissues. The fraction of intensity reflected at a normal-incidence boundary is given by:

$$\alpha_R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$

Therefore, the **difference in acoustic impedance** ($\Delta Z$) between neighbouring tissues directly determines the strength of the returning echo, which in turn determines the **brightness of the corresponding pixel** on the B-mode image. A large $\Delta Z$ → strong echo → bright pixel. A small $\Delta Z$ → weak echo → dark/gray pixel. Hence, $Z$ is the fundamental parameter governing image contrast.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is a coupling gel used between the ultrasound transducer and the patient's skin during scanning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Reason (1 mark):** Air has a very low acoustic impedance ($Z_{air} \approx 0.0004 \times 10^6$ Rayl) compared to skin ($Z_{skin} \approx 1.6 \times 10^6$ Rayl). Computing the reflection coefficient:

$$\alpha_R = \left( \frac{1.6 - 0.0004}{1.6 + 0.0004} \right)^2 \approx 0.999 \;\; (\text{99.9\%})$$

So, without gel, **99.9 % of the ultrasound intensity is reflected back** at the air–skin interface, leaving virtually no acoustic energy to enter the body.

**Role of gel (1.5 marks):** The coupling gel is formulated to have an acoustic impedance close to that of soft tissue (~$1.5 \times 10^6$ Rayl). Applied as a thin layer, it:
- Eliminates the air gap between transducer and skin.
- Drastically reduces the impedance mismatch.
- Allows >99 % transmission of the acoustic energy into the body.
- Provides good acoustic continuity for accurate diagnostic imaging.

In addition, the gel is water-based, non-toxic, water-soluble, and hypoallergenic, making it safe for patient use.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Module Choice Rule:** A typical Module 1 question in the End Semester Exam (ESE) carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Both are shown below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a) Derive the intensity reflection coefficient and intensity transmission coefficient for a normal-incidence ultrasound wave at a planar interface between two tissues. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Define the three waves.**
At a planar interface at $x = 0$ between medium 1 (left) and medium 2 (right), we have:
- Incident wave: $P_i e^{j(\omega t - k_1 x)}$
- Reflected wave: $P_r e^{j(\omega t + k_1 x)}$ (in medium 1)
- Transmitted wave: $P_t e^{j(\omega t - k_2 x)}$ (in medium 2)

**Step 2 (1 mark): Boundary condition — continuity of pressure at $x = 0$.**
$$P_i + P_r = P_t \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): Boundary condition — continuity of particle velocity at $x = 0$.**
$$u_i - u_r = u_t \implies \frac{P_i}{Z_1} - \frac{P_r}{Z_1} = \frac{P_t}{Z_2} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks): Solve the system for $P_t$.**
Multiply (ii) by $Z_1$: $P_i - P_r = \dfrac{Z_1 P_t}{Z_2}$.
From (i): $P_r = P_t - P_i$. Substitute:
$$P_i - (P_t - P_i) = \frac{Z_1 P_t}{Z_2}$$
$$2 P_i - P_t = \frac{Z_1 P_t}{Z_2}$$
$$2 P_i = P_t \left(1 + \frac{Z_1}{Z_2}\right) = P_t \frac{Z_2 + Z_1}{Z_2}$$
$$P_t = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} P_i$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks): Solve for $P_r$ and derive intensity reflection coefficient.**
$P_r = P_t - P_i = \left(\dfrac{2 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} - 1\right) P_i = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2} P_i$

Pressure reflection coefficient: $R_p = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}$

Intensity reflection coefficient (since $I \propto P^2$):
$$\boxed{\alpha_R = R_p^2 = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark): Intensity transmission coefficient (energy conservation).**
$$\boxed{\alpha_T = 1 - \alpha_R = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}}$$

**(b) A 3 MHz ultrasound beam in soft tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s) strikes a tissue interface at depth 4 cm. The intensity reflection coefficient is 0.04. Calculate the (i) wavelength of the beam, (ii) depth of the interface in terms of round-trip time, and (iii) the fraction of intensity transmitted. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Wavelength (2 marks):**
Using $c = f \lambda$:
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1540 \text{ m/s}}{3 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} = 5.13 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.513 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Round-trip time (2.5 marks):**
The one-way depth is 4 cm = 0.04 m. The pulse must travel to the interface and back, so the total path is $2 \times 0.04 = 0.08$ m.
$$t = \frac{2d}{c} = \frac{0.08 \text{ m}}{1540 \text{ m/s}} = 5.19 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 51.9 \text{ μs}$$

**(iii) Fraction transmitted (2.5 marks):**
By conservation of energy:
$$\alpha_T = 1 - \alpha_R = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96$$

So **96 %** of the incident intensity is transmitted deeper into the tissue.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]*

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the basic pulse-echo ultrasonic imaging system. List the major components and state the function of each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
```
Pulse Generator → T/R Switch → Transducer → [Patient Body] 
       ↑                                          ↓
       └──── TGC ← Envelope Detector ← Pre-amplifier ← Echo
                            ↓
                     ADC → Scan Converter → Display
```

**Components and Functions (4 marks):**

1. **Pulse Generator (Master Clock):** Produces short, high-voltage electrical pulses (typically 0.1–2 μs wide) at a chosen Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF, typically 1–10 kHz). It synchronises the entire scanner.

2. **Transmit/Receive (T/R) Switch:** Routes the high-voltage transmit pulse to the transducer, then rapidly switches to the sensitive receive path to capture the weak returning echoes (which can be ~100 dB smaller than the transmit pulse).

3. **Piezoelectric Transducer:** Converts the electrical pulse into an acoustic pulse (using the **inverse piezoelectric effect**) on transmit, and converts returning echoes back into electrical signals (using the **direct piezoelectric effect**) on receive. Common material: **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)**.

4. **Pre-amplifier (Low-Noise Amplifier):** Amplifies the very weak echo signals (often microvolts) by 40–60 dB without adding significant noise.

5. **Time-Gain Compensation (TGC) Amplifier:** Applies depth-dependent gain to compensate for attenuation losses. Echoes from deeper tissues are amplified more than echoes from superficial tissues.

6. **Envelope Detector / Rectifier:** Extracts the envelope of the high-frequency RF echo signal, producing a low-frequency signal proportional to echo amplitude.

7. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Samples the envelope into digital form for processing.

8. **Digital Signal Processor / Scan Converter:** Converts polar-coordinate echo data into a Cartesian raster suitable for TV/monitor display; performs logarithmic compression.

9. **Display Monitor:** Shows the final 2-D B-mode (Brightness-mode) grayscale image.

**(b) An ultrasound beam with initial intensity $I_0 = 50$ mW/cm² travels through liver tissue with attenuation coefficient $\alpha = 0.6$ dB/cm/MHz at 5 MHz. Calculate the intensity at depths of 2 cm, 5 cm, and 10 cm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the total attenuation coefficient in dB/cm:
$$\alpha_{total} = 0.6 \text{ dB/cm/MHz} \times 5 \text{ MHz} = 3.0 \text{ dB/cm}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Use the attenuation formula:
$$I(z) = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\alpha_{total} \cdot z \, / \, 10}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks): At $z = 2$ cm:**
$$I(2) = 50 \times 10^{-3.0 \times 2 / 10} = 50 \times 10^{-0.6} = 50 \times 0.2512 = 12.56 \text{ mW/cm}^2$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks): At $z = 5$ cm:**
$$I(5) = 50 \times 10^{-3.0 \times 5 / 10} = 50 \times 10^{-1.5} = 50 \times 0.0316 = 1.58 \text{ mW/cm}^2$$

**Step 5 (2 marks): At $z = 10$ cm:**
$$I(10) = 50 \times 10^{-3.0 \times 10 / 10} = 50 \times 10^{-3} = 50 \times 0.001 = 0.05 \text{ mW/cm}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The intensity has dropped by a factor of **1000** at 10 cm depth. This huge dynamic range (>60 dB) is why modern scanners use **logarithmic compression** and **TGC** to display a usable image.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSING PITFALLS IN MODULE 1 (Examiner's Perspective):**
> 1. **Confusing pressure vs. intensity reflection coefficient.** Many students write $\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}$ when the question asks for *intensity* reflection — always SQUARE it!
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in depth calculation.** Depth = c·t/2, NOT c·t. The pulse travels to the reflector AND back.
> 3. **Using $c = 330$ m/s (speed in air) instead of $c = 1540$ m/s (speed in soft tissue).** This is the single most common error in KTU answer sheets.
> 4. **Mixing up the order of $Z_1$ and $Z_2$** — $Z_1$ is ALWAYS the medium the wave is *leaving*, $Z_2$ is the medium the wave is *entering*.
> 5. **Using linear units vs. dB inconsistently** — when a coefficient is given in dB/cm, the formula $I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\alpha z/10}$ MUST be used (note division by 10 inside the exponent).
> 6. **Skipping the "Why"** — KTU examiners reward **conceptual justification** as much as calculation. Always state *why* a particular formula applies.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Ultrasound = mechanical, longitudinal, non-ionizing** sound waves with $f > 20$ kHz; diagnostic range is **1–20 MHz**.
- [ ] **Speed of sound in soft tissue** = **1540 m/s** (always use this unless otherwise stated).
- [ ] **Acoustic impedance** $Z = \rho c$ — the most important physical property for ultrasound.
- [ ] **Reflection coefficient (intensity)**: $\alpha_R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^2$ — squares around the difference of impedances.
- [ ] **Transmission coefficient**: $\alpha_T = 1 - \alpha_R$ (conservation of energy).
- [ ] **Depth from echo time**: $d = ct/2$ — always halve the round-trip.
- [ ] **Attenuation** in soft tissue: ~**0.5 dB/cm/MHz**; intensity decays as $I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\alpha z / 10}$.
- [ ] **Wavelength** $\lambda = c/f$; higher frequency → smaller wavelength → better resolution but more attenuation.
- [ ] **Coupling gel is mandatory** — air–skin mismatch reflects 99.9 % of beam.
- [ ] **Piezoelectric effect** — PZT crystal converts electrical ↔ mechanical energy (transmit & receive).
- [ ] **Axial resolution** $\approx c/(2 \Delta f)$ — depends on pulse bandwidth.
- [ ] **Lateral resolution** $\approx 1.22 \lambda F/D$ — depends on beam width at focus.
- [ ] **Pulse-echo principle** — single transducer both transmits and receives.
- [ ] **TGC** (Time-Gain Compensation) restores brightness of deep echoes.
- [ ] **Acoustic gel impedance ≈ 1.5 × 10⁶ Rayl** — matches soft tissue.
- [ ] **Bone (Z ≈ 7.8 × 10⁶)** and **air (Z ≈ 0.0004 × 10⁶)** are the strongest echo generators (and also the strongest obstacles to deeper imaging).
- [ ] **Acoustic pressure in Pa**, **Intensity in W/cm²**, **Attenuation in dB**, **Impedance in Rayl** — keep units straight.
- [ ] In the **B-mode image**, brightness ↔ echo amplitude ↔ acoustic impedance mismatch at that location.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transducer Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT602 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transducer Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Medical Image Processing**, a **transducer** is a precisely engineered device that converts one physical form of energy into another. In diagnostic imaging systems, transducers function as the bidirectional gateway between the patient (biological tissue domain) and the electronic signal-processing domain of the imaging console. The most clinically relevant application is the **piezoelectric ultrasound transducer**, which uses the **direct piezoelectric effect** (mechanical stress → electrical charge) for reception and the **converse piezoelectric effect** (applied voltage → mechanical deformation) for transmission.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **transducer parameter** is a quantitatively measurable characteristic (sensitivity, frequency response, bandwidth, directivity, impedance, etc.) that governs how faithfully and efficiently a transducer couples energy across its input-output interface within the operational frequency band of the imaging modality.

The clinical image quality — spatial resolution, contrast, depth penetration, and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) — is **fundamentally upper-bounded** by these transducer parameters. No downstream digital post-processing can recover information that the transducer failed to acquire.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are shouting into a long cylindrical pipe and your friend at the other end is recording what they hear:

- **Sensitivity** is how loudly your friend hears you — a more efficient "coupling" of sound into the pipe gives louder reception.
- **Resonant frequency** is the natural "musical note" of the pipe — sound travels best near that specific pitch.
- **Bandwidth** is the range of musical notes the pipe can transmit well — a wider bandwidth means the pipe carries bass AND treble faithfully.
- **Directivity** is how narrowly the pipe focuses the sound — a wide pipe broadcasts in all directions; a narrow horn focuses forward.
- **Impedance matching** is the "translator layer" at each end of the pipe that prevents sound from bouncing back.

In an ultrasound probe, the piezoelectric crystal is the "pipe," the matching layer is the "translator," and the patient is your friend's ear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:**
> Every imaging modality — Ultrasound, CT, MRI, PET, X-ray — uses a transducer. Although the underlying physics differs, the **engineering performance metrics** (sensitivity, bandwidth, linearity, dynamic range, SNR) are common and **directly determine the diagnostic value of the final image**.

### 1.3 Classification of Transducer Parameters

Transducer parameters are traditionally classified into three engineering families:

| Family | Purpose | Representative Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Parameters** | Performance under DC / quasi-static conditions | Sensitivity, Linearity, Hysteresis, Drift, Range |
| **Dynamic Parameters** | Performance under time-varying signals | Frequency response, Bandwidth, Resonant frequency, Phase response |
| **System / Environmental Parameters** | Behavior under real operating conditions | Impedance, Loading effect, Insertion loss, Directivity, Noise floor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard**: The IEEE Standard **176-1987** (reaffirmed for piezoelectric crystals) and **IEC 60469** define measurement conventions for many of these parameters in biomedical transducers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Piezoelectric Transducer — Operational Foundations

A piezoelectric ultrasound transducer is, at its core, a **resonant electromechanical oscillator**. The active element is a slab of piezoelectric material (e.g., PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate, or PVDF polymer) with two electrodes deposited on opposite faces. When excited by an RF pulse (typically 1–20 MHz in medical imaging), the slab vibrates at its **thickness-mode resonant frequency**, launching longitudinal acoustic waves into the tissue via a multi-layer front face.

The equivalent circuit is the **Van Dyke / Mason equivalent model**, treating the crystal as a series RLC branch (mechanical resonance) in parallel with a static capacitor $C_0$ (clamped capacitance).

### 2.2 Static / Quasi-Static Parameters

**2.2.1 Sensitivity (S)**
The ratio of output signal magnitude to input stimulus magnitude. For a piezoelectric sensor:
$$S = \frac{Q}{F} = d_{ij} \cdot A \quad \text{(units: C/N or V/m)}$$
where $Q$ is generated charge, $F$ is applied force, $d_{ij}$ is the relevant piezoelectric charge coefficient, and $A$ is electrode area.

For piezoelectric actuation (inverse effect):
$$S_{inv} = \frac{\Delta t}{V} = d_{ij} \quad \text{(units: m/V)}$$

**2.2.2 Linearity**
A transducer is linear if output $\propto$ input over its operating range. Non-linearity is expressed as percentage of full-scale output (FSO):
$$\text{Nonlinearity \%} = \frac{(\text{Actual Output} - \text{Ideal Linear Output})_{\max}}{FSO} \times 100$$

**2.2.3 Hysteresis**
Output difference between ascending and descending input sweeps at the same stimulus value, expressed as % of FSO.

**2.2.4 Dynamic Range**
The ratio, expressed in decibels, between the largest and smallest detectable signal:
$$DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{max}}{V_{min}}\right) \quad \text{(for voltage signals)}$$

### 2.3 Dynamic / Frequency-Domain Parameters

**2.3.1 Resonant Frequency $f_r$**
The frequency at which the transducer exhibits maximum mechanical vibration amplitude for a given drive. For a thickness-mode piezoelectric slab of thickness $t$ and longitudinal acoustic velocity $c_L$:
$$f_r = \frac{c_L}{2t}$$
This is the **half-wavelength resonance** condition.

**2.3.2 Anti-Resonant Frequency $f_a$**
The frequency at which electrical impedance is maximum (mechanical anti-resonance). For an undamped lossless resonator:
$$f_a = \frac{c_L}{2t}\sqrt{1 + \frac{8 k_{31}^2}{\pi^2}}$$
where $k_{31}$ is the electromechanical coupling coefficient.

**2.3.3 Quality Factor (Q)**
The "sharpness" of resonance. Defined as:
$$Q = \frac{f_r}{\Delta f} = \frac{f_r}{f_2 - f_1}$$
where $f_1$ and $f_2$ are the **−3 dB half-power points** flanking $f_r$.

- **High Q** → narrowband, long ring-down, poor axial resolution (used in continuous-wave Doppler).
- **Low Q** → broadband, short pulses, excellent axial resolution (used in B-mode imaging).

**2.3.4 Bandwidth (BW)**
$$BW = f_2 - f_1 = \frac{f_r}{Q}$$
Expressed in **percentage fractional bandwidth**:
$$BW_\% = \frac{\Delta f}{f_c}\times 100$$
where $f_c$ is the center frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard:** Diagnostic ultrasound transducers are typically **broadband** with $BW_\%$ between **60 % and 90 %** (e.g., 2–5 MHz probe ⇒ fractional BW ≈ 86 %), achieved via **damping backing layers** of high acoustic impedance (e.g., tungsten-epoxy composite).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Legend:** All boundary conditions assume linear, time-invariant, lossless piezoelectric medium in thickness mode unless explicitly noted. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (rendered as $\lvert \cdot \rvert$) to denote magnitude.

| # | Parameter | Governing Equation | Units | Physical Meaning | Typical Medical Imaging Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resonant frequency | $f_r = c_L / (2t)$ | Hz | Thickness-mode resonance | 1 – 20 MHz |
| 2 | Anti-resonant frequency | $f_a = f_r \sqrt{1 + 8k_{31}^2/\pi^2}$ | Hz | Electrical impedance maximum | Slightly above $f_r$ |
| 3 | Quality factor | $Q = f_r / \Delta f$ | dimensionless | Resonance sharpness | 2 – 15 (imaging), 50 – 200 (CW Doppler) |
| 4 | Fractional bandwidth | $BW_\% = \Delta f / f_c \times 100$ | % | Spectral spread | 60 % – 90 % (imaging) |
| 5 | Sensitivity (sensor) | $S = d_{ij} A$ | C/N | Charge per unit force | 10⁻¹⁰ to 10⁻⁸ C/N (PZT) |
| 6 | Dynamic range | $DR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\lvert V_{max}/V_{min} \rvert\right)$ | dB | Largest-to-smallest signal | 100 – 120 dB (ultrasound) |
| 7 | Non-linearity | $NL\% = \lvert \Delta_{max} \rvert / FSO \times 100$ | % | Deviation from ideal line | < 1 % (clinical) |
| 8 | Hysteresis | $H\% = \lvert V_{up} - V_{dn} \rvert / FSO \times 100$ | % | Path-dependence | < 0.5 % |
| 9 | Insertion loss | $IL = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\lvert P_{trans} / P_{inc} \rvert\right)$ | dB | Power loss on insertion | 1 – 6 dB |
| 10 | Near-field length (Fresnel) | $N = D^2 / (4\lambda)$ | m | Focal zone of collimated beam | 1 – 50 mm |
| 11 | Fraunhofer distance | $R_F = D^2 / \lambda$ | m | Far-field onset (engineering) | 2 – 100 mm |
| 12 | Half-angle beam divergence | $\sin \theta_{1/2} = 1.22 \lambda / D$ | rad | Far-field angular spread | 1° – 10° |
| 13 | Axial resolution | $\delta_{ax} = c / (2 \cdot BW)$ | m | Minimum resolvable axial spacing | 0.2 – 1.0 mm |
| 14 | Lateral resolution | $\delta_{lat} = 1.22 \lambda F / D$ | m | Beam-width at focus | 0.3 – 2.0 mm |
| 15 | Coupling coefficient | $k^2 = \text{mech energy} / \text{elect energy}$ | dimensionless | Energy conversion efficiency | 0.3 – 0.6 (PZT) |
| 16 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho c$ | kg/(m²·s) (Rayl) | Resistance to acoustic wave | 1.5 MRayl (PZT), 1.6 MRayl (tissue) |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The trade-off between axial resolution and depth penetration — central to every ultrasound system design — is **mathematically governed by equations 1, 3, 4, 10, 13, 14 in the table**. A KTU examiner can ask you to compute any of these from given $c_L$, $t$, $D$, and $f_c$ values.

### 2.5 Loading, Impedance Matching, and Impedance

A piezoelectric element is acoustically coupled to tissue (Z ≈ 1.6 MRayl) via a **matching layer** of quarter-wave thickness $t_m = \lambda/4$ with impedance:
$$Z_m = \sqrt{Z_{PZT} \cdot Z_{tissue}}$$

This **two-port acoustic transmission line analogy** minimizes reflection and maximizes energy transfer — identical in form to optical anti-reflection coatings.

On the electrical side, the **electrical input impedance** at resonance is purely resistive and equal to:
$$R_e = \frac{1}{\omega_r^2 C_0^2 R_m} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad R_e = \frac{1}{k^2 Q \omega_r C_0}$$
This resistive level must be matched to the 50 Ω transmit/receive electronics via an **impedance matching network** (typically a wideband transformer or L-network).

### 2.6 Directivity and Beam Pattern

The transducer is a **spatial filter** that defines a directional acoustic beam. The far-field (Fraunhofer) directivity function for a circular piston of diameter $D$ is the **Airy pattern**:
$$H(\theta) = \frac{2 J_1\!\left(\pi D \sin\theta / \lambda\right)}{\pi D \sin\theta / \lambda}$$
where $J_1$ is the Bessel function of the first kind, order 1. The angular position of the first null is:
$$\sin\theta_{null} = 1.22 \,\lambda / D$$
and the **−6 dB beamwidth** is approximately:
$$\theta_{1/2} \approx 0.71 \,\lambda / D$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Resonant Frequency of a Thickness-Mode Piezoelectric Slab

**Statement:** Show that the half-wavelength thickness-mode resonant frequency of a piezoelectric plate of thickness $t$ is $f_r = c_L / (2t)$.

**Starting Physical Setup:** Consider a piezoelectric slab of thickness $t$ with free surfaces (stress-free boundaries). An RF voltage applied across the electrodes generates a longitudinal stress wave that propagates along the thickness direction at the longitudinal acoustic velocity $c_L = \sqrt{c_{33}^D / \rho}$.

**Step 1 — Write the one-dimensional wave equation.** For a plane longitudinal wave traveling along the $x_3$ direction, the particle displacement $u(x_3, t)$ obeys:
$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = c_L^2 \, \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x_3^2}$$

**Step 2 — General solution.** Separation of variables yields standing-wave solutions:
$$u(x_3, t) = \left(A \cos \beta x_3 + B \sin \beta x_3\right) e^{j\omega t}$$
where $\beta = \omega / c_L$ is the wavenumber.

**Step 3 — Apply free-surface boundary conditions.** At the free surfaces $x_3 = 0$ and $x_3 = t$, the stress $T_3 = c_{33}^D \partial u/\partial x_3$ must vanish:
$$\left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial x_3}\right|_{x_3 = 0} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad B = 0$$
$$\left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial x_3}\right|_{x_3 = t} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad -A \beta \sin \beta t = 0$$

**Step 4 — Non-trivial solution condition.** For $A \ne 0$:
$$\sin \beta t = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \beta t = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 5 — Fundamental mode ($n = 1$).** Substituting $\beta = \omega / c_L$:
$$\frac{\omega_r t}{c_L} = \pi \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_r = \frac{\pi c_L}{t}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Hz.** Since $\omega_r = 2\pi f_r$:
$$2\pi f_r = \frac{\pi c_L}{t} \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{\,f_r = \frac{c_L}{2t}\,}$$
$\blacksquare$

This is the **half-wavelength resonance** condition — the slab thickness equals exactly $\lambda / 2$ at $f_r$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Doubling the operating frequency requires halving the crystal thickness. This is why 20 MHz intravascular probes have active elements only ~100 µm thick — a fundamental manufacturing constraint.

### 3.2 Derivation — Quality Factor from the Equivalent RLC Circuit

**Statement:** For the Van Dyke equivalent circuit of a piezoelectric resonator near resonance, show that $Q = \omega_r L_m / R_m = 1/(\omega_r C_m R_m)$.

**Step 1 — Define the equivalent circuit.** Near resonance the piezoelectric element is modeled as a series RLC branch: $R_m$ (mechanical losses), $L_m$ (mass), $C_m$ (compliance), in parallel with the static clamped capacitance $C_0$. At mechanical resonance $\omega_r$, the series branch is purely resistive.

**Step 2 — Write the impedance of the series branch.**
$$Z_s = R_m + j\!\left(\omega L_m - \frac{1}{\omega C_m}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Identify resonance condition.** Resonance occurs when the imaginary part of $Z_s$ is zero:
$$\omega_r L_m - \frac{1}{\omega_r C_m} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_r^2 = \frac{1}{L_m C_m}$$
Therefore:
$$\omega_r = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_m C_m}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the −3 dB frequencies.** The half-power frequencies $\omega_1, \omega_2$ satisfy $\vert Z_s \vert = \sqrt{2}\,R_m$. Solving:
$$\omega L_m - \frac{1}{\omega C_m} = \pm R_m$$
Selecting the lower branch:
$$\omega_1 = -\frac{R_m}{2L_m} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{R_m}{2L_m}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{L_m C_m}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the bandwidth.** Using the standard series-RLC approximation for high-Q systems ($R_m \ll \omega_r L_m$):
$$\Delta \omega = \omega_2 - \omega_1 \approx \frac{R_m}{L_m}$$

**Step 6 — Form the quality factor.** By definition $Q = \omega_r / \Delta \omega$:
$$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{\omega_r L_m}{R_m} = \frac{1}{\omega_r C_m R_m} = \frac{1}{R_m}\sqrt{\frac{L_m}{C_m}}\,}$$
$\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation — Near-Field Length (Fresnel Zone)

**Statement:** Derive the axial extent $N$ of the near-field region of a circular piston source of diameter $D$ operating at wavelength $\lambda$.

**Step 1 — Define the on-axis pressure field.** For a baffled circular piston of radius $a = D/2$ vibrating uniformly, the on-axis (axial) acoustic pressure amplitude is:
$$\lvert p(z) \rvert = 2 p_0 \lvert \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi a^2}{2 \lambda z}\right) \rvert$$
where $z$ is the distance from the piston face and $p_0$ is the source pressure.

**Step 2 — Identify the last axial maximum.** Maxima occur when the argument of the sine equals $\pi/2, 3\pi/2, \ldots$ The **last on-axis pressure maximum** within the near field occurs at the smallest $z$ satisfying:
$$\frac{\pi a^2}{2 \lambda z_{\max}} = \frac{\pi}{2} \;\Rightarrow\; z_{\max} = \frac{a^2}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to diameter form.** Substituting $a = D/2$:
$$z_{\max} = \frac{(D/2)^2}{\lambda} = \frac{D^2}{4\lambda}$$
This distance defines the **boundary of the near field (Fresnel zone)**:
$$\boxed{\,N = \frac{D^2}{4\lambda}\,}$$
$\blacksquare$

Beyond $z = N$, the beam begins to diverge linearly and the field is the far field (Fraunhofer region), described by the Airy directivity function.

### 3.4 Derivation — Axial Resolution in Pulse-Echo Imaging

**Statement:** Show that the axial resolution of a pulse-echo ultrasound system equals half the spatial pulse length: $\delta_{ax} = \text{SPL}/2 = c/(2 \cdot BW)$.

**Step 1 — Define spatial pulse length.** A pulsed ultrasound wave of temporal duration $\tau$ and speed $c$ has a spatial extent:
$$\text{SPL} = c \cdot \tau$$

**Step 2 — Relate pulse duration to bandwidth.** For a Gaussian-shaped pulse, the time-bandwidth product is approximately $\tau \cdot BW \approx 1$. For rectangular pulses it is $\tau \cdot BW \approx 0.886$. In the engineering approximation:
$$\tau \approx \frac{1}{BW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute SPL.** Substituting:
$$\text{SPL} = \frac{c}{BW}$$

**Step 4 — Apply round-trip geometry.** In pulse-echo, the wave traverses a reflector twice. Two reflectors separated by less than SPL/2 produce overlapping echo pulses and cannot be resolved. Therefore:
$$\boxed{\,\delta_{ax} = \frac{\text{SPL}}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot BW}\,}$$
$\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Illustration:** For $c = 1540$ m/s (soft tissue) and $BW = 4$ MHz, $\delta_{ax} = 1540/(2 \times 4 \times 10^6) = 0.193$ mm ≈ 0.2 mm — sub-millimeter resolution, well within clinical diagnostic needs.

### 3.5 Derivation — Far-Field Beamwidth and Angular Resolution

**Statement:** Show that the first-null beamwidth of a uniformly illuminated circular aperture of diameter $D$ is $\theta_{null} = 1.22 \lambda / D$.

**Step 1 — Start with the Airy directivity function.**
$$H(\theta) = \frac{2 J_1\!\left(\pi D \sin\theta / \lambda\right)}{\pi D \sin\theta / \lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the first zero of the Bessel function.** $J_1(x) = 0$ at $x = 3.8317$ (the first positive root of $J_1$).

**Step 3 — Solve for the angle.** Set the argument of $J_1$ equal to its first root:
$$\frac{\pi D \sin\theta_{null}}{\lambda} = 3.8317$$
$$\sin\theta_{null} = \frac{3.8317 \lambda}{\pi D} = \frac{1.2197 \lambda}{D} \approx \frac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$$

**Step 4 — Small-angle approximation.** For medical imaging ($\theta \ll 1$ rad):
$$\boxed{\,\theta_{null} \approx 1.22 \,\frac{\lambda}{D}\,}$$
$\blacksquare$

The corresponding **lateral resolution at the focal distance $F$** is therefore:
$$\delta_{lat} = F \cdot \theta_{null} \approx 1.22 \,\frac{\lambda F}{D}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Medical Ultrasound Transducer System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Pulser RF Source] --> B[Impedance Matching Network]
    B --> C[Piezoelectric Crystal Active Element]
    C --> D[Acoustic Matching Layer]
    D --> E[Patient Tissue Acoustic Medium]
    E --> F[Backscattered Echo Wave]
    F --> D
    D --> C
    C --> G[Low Noise Amplifier]
    G --> H[Time Gain Compensation TGC]
    H --> I[Beamformer Digital]
    I --> J[Scan Converter]
    J --> K[Display Medical Image]

    L[Battery Power Supply] --> A
    L --> G

    M[Backing Layer Damping] --- C
    N[Acoustic Lens Focusing] --- D

    style A fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#d2691e
    style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#1e5fa8
    style E fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e8b57
    style K fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style M fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#7b3f99
    style N fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
```

**Interpretation of the block diagram:**

- **Pulser RF Source** generates the high-voltage, short-duration RF excitation (typically ±100 V, 100–500 ns).
