- **Impedance Matching Network** electrically matches the 50 Ω pulser to the high-impedance crystal.
- **Piezoelectric Crystal** is the active element, converting electrical to acoustic (transmit) and acoustic to electrical (receive).
- **Backing Layer** (left of crystal) is a high-acoustic-impedance, high-loss material (tungsten-epoxy) that **damps the rear face** to broaden bandwidth.
- **Matching Layer(s)** (right of crystal) are quarter-wave acoustic transformers that minimize the PZT–tissue impedance mismatch.
- **Acoustic Lens** (optional) provides geometric focusing in the elevation plane.
- **Tissue** is the propagation medium; backscatter returns along the same path.
- **LNA + TGC + Beamformer + Scan Converter** form the receive chain producing the final image.

### 4.2 Frequency-Domain Behavior of Transducer Parameters

```mermaid
graph LR
    F[Frequency f] --> FA[Anti Resonance fa]
    F --> FR[Resonance fr Maximum Amplitude]
    F --> FB[Bandwidth Lower f1]
    F --> FC[Bandwidth Upper f2]

    FR --> Q1[Quality Factor Q = fr divided by f2 minus f1]
    FB --> Q1
    FC --> Q1

    Q1 --> APP[Application Branching]
    APP --> APP1[B Mode Imaging Low Q Broadband]
    APP --> APP2[CW Doppler High Q Narrowband]

    style FR fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    style APP1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e8b57
    style APP2 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000
```

### 4.3 Beam Field Geometry — Near Field vs Far Field

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Piston Transducer Diameter D] --> NF[Near Field Fresnel Zone]
    NF --> BOUND[Boundary at z = N = D squared divided by 4 lambda]
    BOUND --> FF[Far Field Fraunhofer Zone]
    FF --> DIV[Beam Diverges Linearly with angle theta]
    DIV --> AX[Axial Resolution SPL over 2]
    DIV --> LAT[Lateral Resolution 1.22 lambda F over D]

    NF --> COLL[Quasi Collimated Beam]
    COLL --> IMPED[Impedance Matching Critical in NF]
    BOUND --> FOC[Focal Point Often Placed Here]

    style P fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#1e5fa8
    style NF fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    style FF fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#7b3f99
    style FOC fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e8b57
```

### 4.4 Transducer Parameter Family Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    TP[Transducer Parameters] --> STAT[Static Parameters]
    TP --> DYN[Dynamic Parameters]
    TP --> SYS[System Level Parameters]

    STAT --> S1[Sensitivity]
    STAT --> S2[Linearity]
    STAT --> S3[Hysteresis]
    STAT --> S4[Drift]

    DYN --> D1[Resonant Frequency]
    DYN --> D2[Anti Resonant Frequency]
    DYN --> D3[Quality Factor Q]
    DYN --> D4[Bandwidth]
    DYN --> D5[Phase Response]

    SYS --> Y1[Input Output Impedance]
    SYS --> Y2[Insertion Loss]

    SYS --> Y5[Coupling Coefficient k]

    style TP fill:#1e5fa8,color:#ffffff

```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Strategy for KTU Exams:** When a question provides a "block diagram" of a transducer, identify the **active element first**, then trace the **forward (transmit)** and **return (receive)** paths. Every block in the path contributes at least one transducer parameter to the overall performance — and examiners expect you to name it.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Part A carries 3 marks each (answer in ~5 lines). Part B carries 14 marks each, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Module-end questions should integrate numericals and derivations.

---

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define the term **transducer**. List any **four static and four dynamic parameters** that characterize a medical imaging transducer.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A transducer is a device that converts one form of energy into another. In medical imaging, it typically converts electrical energy into acoustic (or other) energy and vice versa.

*Static parameters (any four, ½ mark each):*
1. Sensitivity
2. Linearity
3. Hysteresis
4. Drift
5. Range
6. Repeatability

*Dynamic parameters (any four, ½ mark each):*
1. Resonant frequency
2. Anti-resonant frequency
3. Quality factor (Q)
4. Bandwidth
5. Phase response
6. Frequency response

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
What is meant by the **quality factor (Q)** of a transducer? Explain its significance in **B-mode ultrasound imaging** versus **continuous-wave (CW) Doppler** applications.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Quality factor $Q = f_r/\Delta f$ is the ratio of the resonant frequency to the **−3 dB bandwidth** of the transducer. It quantifies the sharpness of resonance.

*Significance:*
- **B-mode imaging** requires **short, broadband pulses** to achieve high axial resolution $\delta_{ax} = c/(2 BW)$. Hence **low Q (typically 2–5)** is preferred, achieved by adding a highly damped backing layer to the piezoelectric crystal.
- **CW Doppler** requires sustained narrowband sinusoidal transmission to measure small frequency shifts from moving blood. Hence **high Q (typically 50–200)** is preferred for maximum sensitivity and frequency precision.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **piezoelectric ultrasound transducer**. Clearly identify the role of the **piezoelectric crystal, matching layer, and backing layer** in determining transducer performance. (7 marks)

**(b)** A medical imaging ultrasound probe uses a **PZT-5H crystal** with longitudinal velocity $c_L = 4500$ m/s. If the required operating frequency is $f_r = 5$ MHz, determine:
  (i) the required crystal thickness, (3 marks)
  (ii) the −3 dB bandwidth and axial resolution, assuming $Q = 5$ and tissue speed $c = 1540$ m/s, and (2 marks)
  (iii) the minimum resolvable axial separation between two reflectors. (2 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

The piezoelectric ultrasound transducer is a multi-layer electromechanical resonator. Its layered construction is:

1. **Piezoelectric crystal (active element):** Typically PZT ceramic, 0.1–1 mm thick, with electrodes sputtered on both faces. Functions:
   - **Transmit mode:** Converse piezoelectric effect — applied RF voltage (1–15 MHz) causes mechanical deformation, launching an acoustic wave.
   - **Receive mode:** Direct piezoelectric effect — returning echoes stress the crystal, producing a small voltage proportional to the echo amplitude.

2. **Matching layer(s):** Quarter-wave ($\lambda/4$) acoustic transformer placed on the tissue-facing side. Typically one or two layers with impedance $Z_m = \sqrt{Z_{PZT} \cdot Z_{tissue}}$ to maximize forward transmission and minimize reflection at the crystal–tissue interface.

3. **Backing layer (damping block):** High-acoustic-impedance, lossy material (e.g., tungsten–epoxy or air-backed in some designs) bonded to the rear face. Its role:
   - **Absorbs the backward-traveling wave** generated when the RF pulse is applied, preventing re-excitation of the crystal.
   - **Broadens the bandwidth** (reduces Q), giving short pulses and high axial resolution.
   - Trade-off: increased damping reduces sensitivity by 1–6 dB — a fundamental engineering compromise.

**Working Principle:** An RF pulse from the pulser excites the crystal at its thickness-resonant frequency. The crystal vibrates, the matching layer couples this vibration efficiently into the tissue, the backing layer absorbs the rear-going wave, and acoustic pulses propagate into the body. Returning echoes retroreflect, recompress the crystal, and generate small voltages which the LNA amplifies for image formation.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Crystal thickness (3 marks):**

Using $f_r = c_L / (2t)$:
$$t = \frac{c_L}{2 f_r} = \frac{4500}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6} = \frac{4500}{10^7} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = \boxed{0.45\,\text{mm}}$$

**(ii) Bandwidth and axial resolution (2 marks):**

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_r}{Q} = \frac{5\,\text{MHz}}{5} = \boxed{1\,\text{MHz}}$$

$$\delta_{ax} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot BW} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 1 \times 10^6} = 7.7 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = \boxed{0.77\,\text{mm}}$$

**(iii) Minimum resolvable axial separation (2 marks):**

The axial resolution **is** the minimum resolvable separation:
$$\delta_{ax,\,\min} = 0.77\,\text{mm}$$

This represents the smallest distance between two reflectors along the beam axis that can be distinguished in the image.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **near field (Fresnel zone)** and **far field (Fraunhofer zone)** for a circular piston transducer. Derive the expression for **near-field length** and discuss its clinical significance. (7 marks)

**(b)** A diagnostic ultrasound probe has an **active aperture diameter $D = 20$ mm** and operates at **$f = 3.5$ MHz** in soft tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s). Determine: (i) wavelength $\lambda$, (1 mark) (ii) near-field length $N$, (2 marks) (iii) −6 dB lateral resolution at the natural focus, and (2 marks) (iv) the half-angle of beam divergence in the far field. (2 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Near Field vs Far Field — Derivation and Significance (7 marks):**

The acoustic field radiated by a circular piston transducer of diameter $D$ is divided into two distinct regions:

*Near field (Fresnel zone), $0 \le z \le N$:* The beam is quasi-collimated with strong axial intensity oscillations. Energy is exchanged between the on-axis pressure and the off-axis Fresnel diffraction zones. The on-axis pressure is given by $p(z) = 2p_0 \sin(\pi D^2 / 8\lambda z)$, which exhibits a series of maxima and minima before the last on-axis maximum.

*Far field (Fraunhofer zone), $z > N$:* The beam diverges smoothly with distance; the field is well described by the **Airy pattern** directivity function $H(\theta) = 2 J_1(\pi D \sin\theta / \lambda) / (\pi D \sin\theta / \lambda)$. On-axis pressure decays as $1/z$.

*Derivation of Near-Field Length:*
The last on-axis pressure maximum occurs at the smallest $z$ for which $\sin(\pi D^2 / 8\lambda z) = 1$, i.e., $\pi D^2 / 8\lambda z = \pi/2$. Solving:
$$z = \frac{D^2}{4\lambda} = N$$
This defines the boundary between near and far fields.

*Clinical Significance:*
- Imaging is **best performed within the near field** because the beam is narrow and well-collimated. The natural focus is conventionally placed at $z = N$.
- Beyond $N$, the beam diverges and lateral resolution degrades linearly with depth.
- For deep imaging (e.g., abdominal), electronic focusing and dynamic aperture are used to push the focal zone deeper.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Wavelength (1 mark):**
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1540}{3.5 \times 10^6} = 4.4 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = \boxed{0.44\,\text{mm}}$$

**(ii) Near-field length (2 marks):**
$$N = \frac{D^2}{4\lambda} = \frac{(0.020)^2}{4 \times 4.4 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{1.76 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.227\,\text{m} = \boxed{22.7\,\text{cm}}$$

**(iii) −6 dB lateral resolution at focus (2 marks):**
Using the first-null beamwidth $\theta_{null} \approx 1.22 \lambda / D$ and focusing at $z = N$:
$$\delta_{lat} \approx 1.22 \,\frac{\lambda \cdot N}{D} = 1.22 \times \frac{4.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.227}{0.020}$$
$$= 1.22 \times \frac{9.99 \times 10^{-5}}{0.020} = 1.22 \times 4.99 \times 10^{-3} = 6.09 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} = \boxed{6.1\,\text{mm}}$$

**Note:** The −6 dB (full) beamwidth is approximately $0.71 \times 1.22 \lambda N/D \approx 3.5$ mm, the conventional lateral resolution figure.

**(iv) Half-angle of beam divergence (2 marks):**
$$\theta_{1/2} \approx 0.71 \,\frac{\lambda}{D} = 0.71 \times \frac{4.4 \times 10^{-4}}{0.020} = 0.71 \times 0.022 = 0.01562\,\text{rad}$$
$$= \boxed{0.0156\,\text{rad} \approx 0.89^\circ}$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 1 — Transducer Parameters):**
> 1. **Mixing up resonant and anti-resonant frequency.** Anti-resonance is *above* resonance; students often write $f_a < f_r$. **Correct:** $f_a > f_r$ (typically by 1–5 %).
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in axial resolution.** Pulse-echo axial resolution is $c/(2 BW)$, not $c/BW$. The factor 2 comes from round-trip travel — losing 1 mark is common.
> 3. **Confusing near-field length with Fraunhofer distance.** $N = D^2 / (4\lambda)$ is the *last on-axis maximum*; $R_F = D^2/\lambda$ is the engineering far-field criterion (4× larger).
> 4. **Unit errors in Q-factor.** Q is dimensionless. Writing it in Hz or dB loses ½ mark.
> 5. **Writing sensitivity as "high" or "low" without numbers.** KTU 2024 rubric demands quantitative justification (units, typical value, or formula).
> 6. **Omitting the backing layer's role in bandwidth.** Many students describe matching layer but forget that *backing* is what controls Q and pulse length.
> 7. **Treating $\sin \theta$ as $\theta$ without stating the small-angle assumption.** Always write the small-angle condition $\theta \ll 1$ rad explicitly for full credit.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Transducer Parameters**

- **Transducer** = energy converter; parameters characterize **how faithfully** it does so.
- **Piezoelectric effect** (direct and converse) is the physical basis of ultrasound transducers.
- **Sensitivity** $S = d_{ij} A$ (sensor) or $\Delta t / V$ (actuator). Higher for PZT than PVDF.
- **Resonant frequency** $f_r = c_L / (2t)$; **thickness mode** = half-wavelength resonance.
- **Quality factor** $Q = f_r / \Delta f = \omega_r L_m / R_m$. **Low Q** ⇒ broadband imaging; **high Q** ⇒ CW Doppler.
- **Bandwidth** $BW = f_r / Q$ (in Hz) or $BW_\% = \Delta f / f_c \times 100$ (dimensionless %).
- **Dynamic range** $DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{max}/V_{min})$; medical ultrasound typically 100–120 dB.
- **Non-linearity** and **hysteresis** are static errors; clinical limit < 1 % and < 0.5 % FSO respectively.
- **Insertion loss** $IL = -10 \log_{10}(P_{trans}/P_{inc})$ in dB.
- **Near-field length** $N = D^2 / (4\lambda)$ — best imaging region; **Far field** = $z > N$.
- **Far-field beam divergence** $\theta_{1/2} \approx 0.71 \lambda / D$; **first null** $\theta_{null} = 1.22 \lambda / D$.
- **Axial resolution** $\delta_{ax} = c / (2 \cdot BW)$ — round-trip factor 2 is mandatory.
- **Lateral resolution** $\delta_{lat} = 1.22 \lambda F / D$ at focal distance $F$.
- **Quarter-wave matching layer** $t_m = \lambda/4$, $Z_m = \sqrt{Z_{PZT} \cdot Z_{tissue}}$.
- **Backing layer** = high-Z lossy material that **damps** the rear face, lowers Q, broadens BW.
- **Coupling coefficient** $k$ — measures electromechanical energy conversion efficiency (0.3–0.6 for PZT).
- **Acoustic impedance** $Z = \rho c$ in Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹); PZT ≈ 30 MRayl, soft tissue ≈ 1.6 MRayl.
- **Trade-off mantra:** High frequency ⇒ better resolution but less penetration; low Q ⇒ better imaging but lower sensitivity.
- **Standard references:** IEEE Std 176-1987 (piezoelectric), IEC 60469 (transducer measurements).
- **Units to memorize:** Hz, dB, Rayl, C/N, m/V, rad, dimensionless (Q, k, BW%).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** When a question gives you $D$, $f$, and $c$ — immediately write down $f_r = c/(2t)$, $N = D^2/(4\lambda)$, $\delta_{ax} = c/(2 BW)$, and $\theta_{1/2} = 0.71 \lambda/D$. These four equations cover ~80 % of numerical problems in this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**A-Mode (Amplitude Mode)** is the earliest and simplest form of pulse-echo ultrasound display. It is a **one-dimensional (1-D) time-amplitude representation** in which a short ultrasonic pulse is transmitted into the body by a single transducer, and the returning echoes are displayed as vertical deflections (spikes) on a cathode ray oscilloscope (CRO). The **horizontal axis represents time (or depth)**, while the **vertical axis represents the amplitude (strength) of the reflected echo**.

Each vertical spike corresponds to an acoustic interface (boundary between two tissues of differing acoustic impedance), and the **height of the spike is proportional to the magnitude of the reflected pressure wave**. The **horizontal position of the spike is proportional to the round-trip time of flight** between the transducer and the reflecting interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A-Mode is classified as a *pre-image* or *signal-mode* ultrasound modality. It does NOT produce a two-dimensional anatomical picture; instead, it produces a precise *depth profile* along a single ultrasound beam axis. This is a frequently tested distinction in KTU university examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Acoustic Impedance (Z)** — defined as the product of tissue density $\rho$ and the speed of sound $c$ in that tissue — is the fundamental property governing echo generation:
>
> $$Z = \rho \cdot c$$
>
> The reflection coefficient $R$ at an interface is given by:
>
> $$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$
>
> A large difference in $Z$ between adjacent tissues produces a strong echo (tall spike in A-mode).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a deep well in complete darkness. You shout *"Hello!"* into the well and listen carefully. The time delay between your shout and the returning echo tells you **how deep the water is**; the loudness of the returning echo tells you **something about the surface** (a hard stone bottom produces a louder echo than muddy water).

**A-Mode ultrasound works on exactly this principle:**

| Real-World Action (Echo in a Well) | A-Mode Ultrasound Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You (the source) | Piezoelectric transducer (pulses ultrasound into tissue) |
| Your shout | Short burst of high-frequency sound (1–15 MHz) |
| Time until echo returns | Depth of reflecting tissue interface |
| Loudness of echo | Amplitude of vertical spike on display |
| "Plot" on a timeline | Oscilloscope trace (spike position vs. amplitude) |

So in A-mode, the physician literally *listens with mathematics* — measuring precise microsecond intervals to map the depths of internal structures like the eye's lens, the brain's midline, or a fetus's biparietal diameter.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** **A**-Mode = **A**mplitude spikes on a **A**xis of time. **No picture, just spikes.**

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics must be memorized for KTU 2024 examinations:

- **Speed of sound in soft tissue: $c = 1540 \text{ m/s}$** (often rounded to **$1500 \text{ m/s}$** for mental math)
- **Density of soft tissue: $\rho \approx 1060 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
- **Acoustic impedance of soft tissue: $Z \approx 1.63 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$** (Rayl)
- **Transducer frequencies used in A-mode: 1 MHz – 15 MHz**
- **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) for A-mode: typically 1–4 kHz**
- **Pulse duration: typically 1–5 µs**
- **Standard display unit: 1 division on time base ≈ 0.5 µs to 1 µs**

> [!NOTE]
> In diagnostic A-mode ultrasound, the factor of **2** appears in every depth equation because the sound pulse must travel *down to the interface AND back* to the transducer. Always remember: **"round trip, not one way."**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Structured Step-by-Step Logic

The A-mode system operates on the following sequential logic chain. Understanding each step is essential for KTU essay-type questions.

**Step 1 — Pulse Generation (Transmitter Stage)**
A high-voltage electrical spike excites the piezoelectric crystal inside the transducer. The crystal vibrates at its resonant frequency and emits a short burst of ultrasound (typically 2–3 cycles) into the tissue.

**Step 2 — Wave Propagation Through Tissue**
The acoustic wave propagates through tissue layers. At each interface where acoustic impedance changes, a portion of the wave is **reflected** (creating an echo) and the remainder is **transmitted** (continuing deeper).

**Step 3 — Echo Reception (Receiver Stage)**
The returning echoes strike the same transducer (acting now as a receiver). The piezoelectric effect converts the mechanical pressure wave back into a small electrical voltage.

**Step 4 — Time-Gated Amplification**
The receiver is **time-gated**: it only listens during specific time windows after pulse transmission. This prevents the strong initial transmit pulse from saturating the display.

**Step 5 — Display on CRO**
The amplified echo voltages deflect the CRO beam vertically. A horizontal time base sweeps from left to right at a calibrated rate (e.g., 1 µs/division). The result is a series of vertical spikes whose **x-position = depth** and **y-amplitude = echo strength**.

**Step 6 — Interpretation by the Clinician**
The clinician interprets the spike pattern:
- A **single tall spike** = strong specular reflector (e.g., organ capsule)
- A **group of closely spaced spikes** = tissue layers of slightly different impedance
- A **flat baseline with no spikes** = homogeneous fluid (no internal reflectors) → typical of a cyst or vitreous humor
- The **distance between two spikes** = thickness of a structure (e.g., lens, fetal skull diameter)

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|
| Pulse-echo (not continuous wave) | Allows depth measurement using time-of-flight; continuous wave would only measure Doppler shift with no range information |
| Single transducer (transmit + receive) | Avoids complex beamforming; keeps the system simple and inexpensive |
| Time-gated amplification | The transmit pulse is ~1000× stronger than returning echoes; without gating, the receiver would be saturated for several microseconds |
| Calibrated time base | Directly converts time into distance via $d = ct/2$, giving a *quantitative* depth measurement |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **complete mathematical toolkit** required to solve any A-mode numerical or derivation problem in KTU 2024 examinations. All variables are defined within the table.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Depth of reflecting interface | $d$ = depth (m), $c$ = speed of sound in tissue ($\approx 1540$ m/s), $t$ = round-trip time (s) |
| 2 | $t = \dfrac{2 \cdot d}{c}$ | Round-trip time of flight (for depth $d$) | $t$ (s), $d$ (m), $c$ (m/s) |
| 3 | $\Delta d = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$ | Maximum unambiguous depth | $\text{PRF}$ = pulse repetition frequency (Hz) |
| 4 | $\Delta x = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$ | Axial (range) resolution | $\Delta f$ = transducer bandwidth (Hz) |
| 5 | $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | Intensity reflection coefficient | $Z_1, Z_2$ = acoustic impedances (Rayl) |
| 6 | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Acoustic impedance of a medium | $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $c$ = speed of sound (m/s) |
| 7 | $I_r = R \cdot I_0$ | Reflected intensity | $I_0$ = incident intensity (W/m²) |
| 8 | $I_t = (1 - R) \cdot I_0$ | Transmitted intensity | $I_t$ (W/m²) |
| 9 | $\alpha_{dB} = \dfrac{20}{x} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_x}\right)$ | Attenuation coefficient (one-way) | $\alpha$ in dB/cm, $x$ = path length, $P$ = pressure amplitude |
| 10 | $f_d = \dfrac{2 \cdot v \cdot f_0 \cos\theta}{c}$ | Doppler shift (for moving reflectors, advanced A-mode) | $v$ = velocity, $f_0$ = transmit frequency, $\theta$ = angle |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical pitfall:** In all A-mode depth formulas, the factor **2** must appear in the numerator because the wave travels a *round trip*. A common KTU examiner deduction is *–2 marks* for forgetting this factor.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

A-mode, despite being obsolete as a primary imaging modality, remains the **conceptual foundation of all modern ultrasound**. Every clinical ultrasound system — 2-D B-mode, 3-D/4-D, Doppler, elastography — uses A-mode signals as their **raw data** before scan conversion. Specific legacy clinical applications that still appear in KTU question papers include:

- **Ophthalmology (Ocular biometry):** measuring axial length of the eye, lens thickness, anterior chamber depth to compute intraocular lens (IOL) power before cataract surgery.
- **Echoencephalography (historic):** detecting midline shift of the brain in suspected intracranial hemorrhage or tumor.
- **Fetal biparietal diameter (BPD):** measuring fetal head diameter between the two parietal bones in early pregnancy.
- **Cyst vs. solid lesion differentiation:** a cyst produces a clean baseline (anechoic), while a solid tumor produces internal echoes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Depth From Time-of-Flight (The Core A-Mode Equation)

This derivation is the most heavily tested in KTU examinations.

**Given:**
- A short ultrasound pulse is emitted at time $t = 0$.
- The pulse travels downward through tissue at speed $c$ (≈ 1540 m/s).
- It strikes a tissue interface at depth $d$.
- The echo travels back upward at the same speed $c$ and arrives at the transducer at time $t = T$.

**To derive:** A direct formula relating the measured time $T$ to the depth $d$.

---

**Step 1: Write the one-way distance in terms of speed and time.**
The pulse travels from the transducer (depth 0) to the interface (depth $d$) in time $T/2$ (since it must return). By the definition of constant velocity:

$$d = c \cdot \left(\frac{T}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2: Algebraically isolate $d$.**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot T}{2}$$

This is the fundamental A-mode depth equation.

**Step 3: Solve for the round-trip time $T$ (useful when given depth, asked for time).**
Multiply both sides by 2:

$$2d = c \cdot T$$

Divide by $c$:

$$T = \frac{2d}{c}$$

**Step 4: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style).**

> *An A-mode ultrasound system uses a transducer frequency of 5 MHz. The CRO shows a spike at a horizontal position corresponding to a time delay of $T = 13 \mu s$. Calculate the depth of the reflecting interface. Assume $c = 1540$ m/s.*

**Solution:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot T}{2} = \frac{1540 \times 13 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{20020 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 10010 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 10.01 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5: Sanity Check.**
A 13 µs round-trip in soft tissue (1540 m/s) corresponds to a one-way distance of $1540 \times 6.5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.01001$ m = 10 mm. ✓ The result is physically reasonable for a near-surface structure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Unambiguous Depth

**Why we need this:** If the PRF is too high, the next pulse is transmitted before the deepest echo from the previous pulse has returned. This creates *range ambiguity* — the system cannot tell whether an echo came from the *current* pulse or the *previous* one.

**Derivation:**

The pulse repetition period is:

$$T_{PRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PRF}}$$

In one PRF period, the maximum distance the pulse can travel (round trip) is:

$$2 \cdot d_{\max} = c \cdot T_{PRF} = \frac{c}{\text{PRF}}$$

Solving for $d_{\max}$:

$$\boxed{d_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}}$$

**Worked Example:**

> *If PRF = 10 kHz and $c = 1540$ m/s, find the maximum unambiguous depth.*

$$d_{\max} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 10000} = \frac{1540}{20000} = 0.077 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d_{\max} = 7.7 \text{ cm}}$$

This is sufficient for ocular or superficial applications but too shallow for abdominal imaging.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Axial Resolution

**Definition:** Axial resolution $\Delta x$ is the *minimum separation along the beam axis* between two reflectors such that they can still be distinguished as separate spikes on the A-mode display.

**Derivation using the pulse length:**

The shortest pulse has a spatial extent equal to one wavelength $\lambda = c/f_0$ (for a single-cycle pulse). If two reflectors are closer than half this wavelength, their echoes overlap and merge into a single spike.

$$\Delta x = \frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{c}{2 f_0}$$

For a more realistic short pulse with bandwidth $\Delta f$, the axial resolution becomes:

$$\boxed{\Delta x = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f}}$$

**Worked Example:**

> *A 5 MHz transducer has a –6 dB bandwidth of 3 MHz. Compute the axial resolution in soft tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s).*

$$\Delta x = \frac{1540}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1540}{6 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\Delta x = 256.7 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta x \approx 0.257 \text{ mm}}$$

This means two reflectors separated by more than ~0.26 mm can be distinguished as separate spikes — explaining why high-frequency transducers produce better A-mode detail.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reflection Coefficient at a Tissue Interface

**Why this matters for A-mode spike amplitude:** The amplitude of each vertical spike is *not* arbitrary — it is governed by the impedance mismatch at that interface.

**Derivation of intensity reflection coefficient:**

When a wave traveling in medium 1 strikes an interface with medium 2, boundary conditions (continuity of pressure and particle velocity) yield the reflected pressure amplitude ratio:

$$\frac{P_r}{P_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

Since intensity is proportional to pressure squared:

$$R = \left(\frac{P_r}{P_i}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$

**Worked Example:**

> *Acoustic impedance of fat = $1.38 \times 10^6$ Rayl. Acoustic impedance of muscle = $1.70 \times 10^6$ Rayl. Calculate the intensity reflection coefficient at the fat-muscle interface.*

$$R = \left(\frac{1.70 \times 10^6 - 1.38 \times 10^6}{1.70 \times 10^6 + 1.38 \times 10^6}\right)^2$$

$$R = \left(\frac{0.32}{3.08}\right)^2 = (0.1039)^2$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.0108 \text{ or } 1.08\%}$$

This explains why fat-muscle interfaces produce a small but visible A-mode spike, while a soft tissue–bone interface (large impedance mismatch) produces a very large spike.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 A-Mode System Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete signal flow inside a typical A-mode ultrasound system, from transducer to display.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pgen["Pulser and Timing Clock"]
    tx["Transmitter HV Amplifier"]
    tdc["T R Switch Time Gated Duplexer"]
    trd["Single Piezoelectric Transducer"]
    tissue["Tissue Layers with Interfaces"]
    rx["Receiver RF Amplifier"]
    det["Envelope Detector and Log Compressor"]
    amp["Video Amplifier"]
    cro["Cathode Ray Oscilloscope CRO Display"]
    tb["Calibrated Time Base Sweep"]

    pgen --> tx
    pgen --> tdc
    pgen --> tb
    tx --> tdc
    tdc --> trd
    trd --> tissue
    tissue --> trd
    trd --> tdc
    tdc --> rx
    rx --> det
    det --> amp
    amp --> cro
    tb --> cro
```

**Reading the diagram:**

1. The **Pulser and Timing Clock** generates a high-voltage pulse and synchronizes the time base.
2. The pulse passes through a **T/R (Transmit/Receive) switch** that protects the receiver during transmission.
3. The **single transducer** emits the pulse into tissue; the same transducer receives echoes.
4. Returning echoes pass back through the T/R switch into the **RF amplifier**.
5. The **envelope detector** removes the high-frequency carrier, leaving only the slowly-varying spike envelope.
6. The **log compressor** maps the wide dynamic range (~100 dB) into a viewable range on the CRO.
7. The **time base sweep** moves the CRO beam horizontally at a calibrated rate (µs/cm).
8. The final result: a series of **vertical spikes** at positions proportional to depth.

---

## 4.2 A-Mode Display Trace — Conceptual Spike Pattern

The following diagram depicts the typical appearance of an A-mode oscilloscope trace from an ocular examination (eye axial scan).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axis["Horizontal Axis Time or Depth"]
    s1["Spike 1 Initial Transmit Pulse"]
    s2["Spike 2 Cornea Anterior Surface"]
    s3["Spike 3 Lens Anterior Surface"]
    s4["Spike 4 Lens Posterior Surface"]
    s5["Spike 5 Retina Posterior Surface"]
    s6["Spike 6 Sclera or Orbital Fat"]
    s7["Flat Baseline Vitreous Humor Anechoic"]
    s8["Flat Baseline Anterior Chamber Anechoic"]

    axis --- s1
    axis --- s2
    axis --- s3
    axis --- s4
    axis --- s7
    axis --- s5
    axis --- s8
    axis --- s6
```

**Interpretation of the spike pattern:**

- **Spike 1:** the initial transmit pulse (always present, very tall, at $t = 0$).
- **Spike 2 → Spike 5:** four tall spikes representing the cornea, both lens surfaces, and the retina. The *distances between* these spikes give the clinician the **axial length of the eye, anterior chamber depth, and lens thickness**.
- **Spikes 7 and 8 (flat baselines):** fluid-filled regions (vitreous humor and anterior chamber) contain no reflectors, so the baseline is silent — an *anechoic* region.
- **Spike 6:** the last strong reflector (back wall of the eye / orbital fat).

---

## 4.3 A-Mode vs. B-Mode vs. M-Mode — Comparative Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph oneD["ONE DIMENSIONAL MODES"]
        aMode["A Mode Amplitude vs Time"]
        mMode["M Mode Motion vs Time used in Echocardiography"]
    end

    subgraph twoD["TWO DIMENSIONAL MODE"]
        bMode["B Mode Brightness vs XY Position Gray Scale Image"]
    end

    trans["Single Transducer Pulse Echo"]

    trans --> aMode
    trans --> mMode
    trans --> bMode

    aMode --> bright["B Mode uses A Mode spikes as building blocks along many scan lines"]
    bright --> bMode
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** Every B-mode image is built from thousands of A-mode scans, each taken along a slightly different beam direction. Scan conversion turns the 1-D spike trains into a 2-D grayscale picture. This is a *very high-yield* conceptual question for KTU 2024.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define A-Mode ultrasound display. Mention any two clinical applications. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A-Mode (Amplitude Mode) is a one-dimensional pulse-echo ultrasound display in which the returning echoes are represented as vertical spikes on a time base. The horizontal position of each spike represents the depth (or time of flight) of the reflecting interface, and the amplitude (height) of the spike is proportional to the strength of the echo, which in turn depends on the acoustic impedance mismatch at that interface. **(2 marks)**

**Clinical applications (any two):**
- Ocular biometry — measurement of axial length of the eye for intraocular lens (IOL) implantation.
- Echoencephalography — detection of midline shift of the brain in suspected hemorrhage/tumor.
- Fetal biparietal diameter measurement in early pregnancy. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2: Why is the factor '2' used in the A-mode depth formula $d = ct/2$? `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The factor **2** appears because the ultrasonic pulse must travel from the transducer to the tissue interface AND back to the transducer before being detected. **(1 mark)**

Hence the measured time $t$ is a **round-trip time-of-flight**, and the total distance covered by the wave is $c \cdot t$, but the actual depth of the reflector is only half of this distance. **(1 mark)**

Forgetting this factor leads to depth values that are exactly double the correct value, which would be clinically dangerous — for example, an intraocular lens power calculation error of 2× would result in severe refractive error post-surgery. **(1 mark)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: (a) Describe the working principle of A-Mode ultrasound with a neat block diagram. (7 marks) (b) Derive the expression $d = ct/2$ and explain how the amplitude of the echo spike depends on the acoustic impedance mismatch between tissues. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

---

#### Part (a) — Working Principle and Block Diagram (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A-Mode ultrasound operates on the **pulse-echo principle**. The block diagram consists of the following stages:

**(1) Pulser / Timing Clock (1 mark):**
Generates a high-voltage short-duration electrical pulse (typically 100–400 V, lasting 1–5 µs) at a Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) of 1–4 kHz. The same clock synchronizes the CRO time base sweep.

**(2) Transmitter (0.5 marks):**
The electrical pulse is applied to a piezoelectric crystal (e.g., lead zirconate titanate — PZT) housed in the transducer. The crystal mechanically deforms and emits a short burst of ultrasound at its resonant frequency (1–15 MHz).

**(3) T/R Switch (Duplexer) (1 mark):**
An electronic switch that directs the transmit pulse to the transducer during transmission and routes the returning echoes to the receiver during reception. It also protects the sensitive receiver from the high-voltage transmit pulse using diode limiters.

**(4) Single Transducer (1 mark):**
A single piezoelectric element acts as both transmitter and receiver. Acoustic gel is applied between the transducer and skin to eliminate the air-tissue impedance mismatch (otherwise 99.9% of the energy would be reflected at the skin surface).

**(5) Tissue Interfaces (0.5 marks):**
The acoustic wave propagates through tissue; at each interface where acoustic impedance changes, a fraction of the wave is reflected (echo) and the rest continues deeper.

**(6) Receiver RF Amplifier (1 mark):**
Amplifies the weak returning echoes (often only microvolts to millivolts in amplitude) by 60–100 dB.

**(7) Envelope Detector and Log Compressor (1 mark):**
Removes the high-frequency carrier (rectification + low-pass filtering) to extract the spike envelope. Logarithmic compression maps the wide dynamic range (~100 dB) of returning echoes into a viewable 0–30 dB range on the CRO.

**(8) CRO Display with Calibrated Time Base (1 mark):**
The vertical deflection of the CRO electron beam is driven by the detected echo amplitude; the horizontal sweep is calibrated so that 1 division = a known microsecond interval. Each echo appears as a vertical spike whose **x-position = depth** and **y-amplitude = echo strength**.

---

#### Part (b) — Derivation of $d = ct/2$ and Echo Amplitude Dependence (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let the transducer emit a pulse at $t = 0$. The pulse travels downward at speed $c$ and strikes the reflector at depth $d$ after time $t_1 = d/c$. The echo then travels upward at the same speed and reaches the transducer after additional time $t_2 = d/c$.

Total round-trip time:

$$T = t_1 + t_2 = \frac{d}{c} + \frac{d}{c} = \frac{2d}{c}$$

Rearranging for depth:

$$\boxed{d = \frac{c \cdot T}{2}}$$

**Echo Amplitude Dependence on Impedance Mismatch (3 marks):**

The amplitude of each A-mode spike is determined by the **intensity reflection coefficient** $R$ at the tissue interface:

$$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$

where $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ are the acoustic impedances of the two tissues. The reflected intensity is:

$$I_r = R \cdot I_0$$

A **large difference** between $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ (e.g., soft tissue ↔ bone, $R \approx 0.41$, ~41%) produces a **very tall spike**. A **small difference** (e.g., fat ↔ muscle, $R \approx 0.01$, ~1%) produces a **small spike**. Identical impedances ($Z_1 = Z_2$, $R = 0$) produce **no echo** — this is why fluid-filled structures (cyst, vitreous humor) appear as flat baselines in A-mode.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): (a) Compare A-Mode, B-Mode, and M-Mode ultrasound displays. (7 marks) (b) An A-mode transducer operating at 5 MHz with a bandwidth of 3 MHz is used in soft tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s). Calculate (i) the axial resolution, (ii) the maximum unambiguous depth if the PRF is 5 kHz, and (iii) the depth corresponding to an echo time-of-flight of 26 µs. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

---

#### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Feature | A-Mode | B-Mode | M-Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full form** | Amplitude Mode | Brightness Mode | Motion Mode |
| **Dimensionality** | 1-D (along beam) | 2-D (image) | 1-D (along beam) |
| **Display** | Spikes on time base | Gray-scale 2-D image | Moving dots/strip |
| **Horizontal axis** | Time (depth) | Lateral position | Time (slow sweep) |
| **Vertical axis** | Echo amplitude | Depth | Echo amplitude |
| **Brightness meaning** | Not applicable | Echo amplitude | Echo amplitude |
| **Transducer** | Single, fixed | Multiple scan lines, scanned | Single, fixed |
| **Clinical use** | Eye, brain midline, fetal BPD | General anatomy imaging | Heart valve motion, fetal heart rate |
| **Output** | Quantitative depth | Qualitative anatomy | Time-motion graph |

**(7 marks — 1 mark per row, or proportional marking)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 marks)

**Given:** $c = 1540$ m/s, $f_0 = 5$ MHz, $\Delta f = 3$ MHz, PRF = 5 kHz, $T = 26$ µs.

**(i) Axial Resolution (2 marks):**

$$\Delta x = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\Delta x = \frac{1540}{6 \times 10^{6}} = 256.7 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta x \approx 0.257 \text{ mm}}$$

**(ii) Maximum Unambiguous Depth (2 marks):**

$$d_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 5000} = \frac{1540}{10000}$$

$$\boxed{d_{\max} = 0.154 \text{ m} = 15.4 \text{ cm}}$$

**(iii) Depth for $T = 26$ µs (3 marks):**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot T}{2} = \frac{1540 \times 26 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{40040 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 20020 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 0.020 \text{ m} = 20.0 \text{ mm}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 2 in depth formulas (–2 marks):** Always use $d = cT/2$, not $d = cT$. This is the single most common mistake in A-mode numericals.
> 2. **Unit errors:** Convert MHz to Hz and µs to seconds *before* plugging into the formula. A 5 MHz = $5 \times 10^6$ Hz, NOT 5 Hz. Marks are routinely lost here.
> 3. **Confusing axial resolution with lateral resolution:** A-mode is along the beam axis — only *axial* (range) resolution is meaningful. Lateral resolution applies only to B-mode imaging.
> 4. **Mixing up reflection coefficient vs. transmission coefficient:** $R$ gives the *reflected* fraction; $1 - R$ gives the *transmitted* fraction. Make sure the question asks for the right one.
> 5. **Ignoring acoustic gel in clinical setup:** Students often forget to mention that acoustic coupling gel is required to overcome the air-skin impedance mismatch; otherwise, 99.9% of the ultrasound would be reflected before entering the body.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **A-Mode** = **Amplitude Mode**, a 1-D pulse-echo display using a **single transducer**.
- Display = **vertical spikes** on a calibrated time base; **x = depth**, **y = echo amplitude**.
- Core equation: $\boxed{d = \dfrac{c \cdot T}{2}}$, with $c \approx 1540$ m/s in soft tissue.
- Factor **2** is mandatory (round-trip travel) — never omit it.
- **Maximum unambiguous depth:** $d_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$.
- **Axial resolution:** $\Delta x = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$.
- **Reflection coefficient:** $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$, where $Z = \rho c$.
- **Large $\Delta Z$ → tall spike**; **$\Delta Z = 0$ → no spike** (anechoic fluid).
- Typical transducer frequency: **1–15 MHz**; typical PRF: **1–4 kHz**.
- Clinical uses: **ocular biometry, echoencephalography, fetal BPD**.
- **T/R switch** protects the receiver; **acoustic gel** couples the transducer to the skin.
- **A-mode is the conceptual ancestor of all modern ultrasound** — B-mode images are built from many A-mode scan lines.
- **Anechoic regions** (flat baselines) indicate homogeneous fluid → clinical hallmark of cysts, vitreous humor, bladder, and blood-filled chambers.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**M-Mode Echocardiography** (also called **Motion Mode** or **Time-Motion Mode**) is a one-dimensional ultrasound display modality in which the position of reflecting structures along a single, fixed ultrasound beam is plotted as a function of time.

Formally, M-mode can be defined as:

> *M-mode is a pulsed-echo ultrasound display technique in which the depth (vertical axis) of a tissue interface is displayed against time (horizontal axis), with the brightness of the trace proportional to the amplitude of the returning echo. It is generated by repeatedly firing ultrasound pulses along a stationary single line of sight and recording the depth of each echo on a moving time-base display.*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, M-mode is classified as a **1-D real-time ultrasound imaging modality** belonging to Module 1 (Fundamentals of Medical Image Formation). It is typically studied immediately after the discussion of A-mode, since M-mode is, in effect, a *time-stretched version* of A-mode data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> M-mode is one of the three classical pulse-echo display modes, alongside **A-mode (Amplitude)** and **B-mode (Brightness)**. Unlike B-mode which produces a 2-D cross-sectional image, M-mode sacrifices spatial coverage to achieve **extremely high temporal resolution** (up to ~2000 frames/second), making it uniquely suited for capturing rapidly moving cardiac structures such as valve leaflets and chamber walls.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

To make M-mode intuitive, imagine the following real-world analogy:

### 🎯 The "Sonar Lighthouse" Analogy

Imagine you are standing on the deck of a ship in foggy weather, holding a **flashlight that you switch ON-OFF-ON-OFF rapidly** (pulsed light). You point it straight down at the water.

1. **Each flash** travels down, hits a fish, a rock, or the seabed, and the echo bounces back to your eyes.
2. The **time taken** for the echo to return tells you **how deep** that object is.
3. Now, instead of recording this only once, you do it **thousands of times per second**, and a **moving paper scroll** moves sideways under your recording pen, plotting a new "dot at that depth" every time.

If a fish is swimming up and down:
- When the fish is **deeper**, the dot appears **lower on the paper**.
- When the fish is **shallower**, the dot appears **higher**.
- As the paper scrolls sideways, you see a **wavy line** that traces the fish's movement through time.

That wavy line, with **depth on the vertical axis** and **time on the horizontal axis**, is **exactly what an M-mode echocardiogram looks like** for a heart valve or chamber wall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why M-mode is brilliant for the heart:**
> The heart valves and walls move at speeds of 50–150 mm/s. A standard 2-D ultrasound gives only 30–60 frames per second, which is too slow to crisply resolve valve motion. M-mode can sample the same line up to **2000 times per second**, producing razor-sharp motion curves ideal for timing measurements (e.g., valve opening/closing intervals, wall thickening, ejection fraction surrogate).

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Typical Value |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Ultrasound frequency (f)** | Operating frequency of the transducer | **2 – 10 MHz** (cardiac: 2.5–5 MHz) |
| **Speed of sound in soft tissue (c)** | Average propagation velocity used for ranging | **1540 m/s** |
| **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)** | Number of ultrasound pulses emitted per second along the M-line | **1000 – 2000 Hz** |
| **Wavelength (λ)** | Spatial period of the ultrasound wave | **0.3 – 0.8 mm** |
| **Axial resolution (Δd)** | Minimum separable depth between two reflectors | **~1 mm** |
| **Maximum displayable depth** | d_max, limited by PRF and c | **~15 cm** for cardiac probes |
| **Sweep speed** | Lateral speed of the time-base on the display | **25, 50, 100 mm/s** |
| **Temporal resolution** | Effective frame rate = PRF for M-mode | **Up to 2000 fps** |

> [!NOTE]
> All M-mode depth measurements ultimately rely on the assumed constant **c = 1540 m/s** in soft tissue. Fat, bone, and lung deviate from this value, introducing small ranging errors (~3–5%) that the KTU examiner often tests.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step by Step

The M-mode imaging chain consists of the following conceptual stages:

### Step 1: Pulsed Transmission
A single piezoelectric crystal in the transducer is excited by a brief high-voltage RF pulse (typically 1–3 cycles of the centre frequency, lasting ~0.5–1 µs). This generates an **ultrasound pulse** that propagates into the body along a narrow beam.

### Step 2: Echo Reception
The same crystal (in pulse-echo mode) listens for returning echoes. Each tissue interface (e.g., blood–myocardium, valve leaflet) reflects a small portion of the energy back. The transducer converts the pressure echo back into an electrical signal.

### Step 3: Time-of-Flight (TOF) Ranging
The ultrasound system measures the **round-trip time t** between pulse emission and echo reception. Because the speed of sound in soft tissue is known (c = 1540 m/s), the depth of the reflecting interface is computed as:

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

The factor of 2 accounts for the **round trip** (down and back).

### Step 4: Amplitude-to-Brightness Mapping
The peak amplitude of each returning echo (which itself reflects acoustic impedance mismatch at the interface) is mapped to a **grey-scale intensity** (brightness) on the display. This is identical to B-mode pixel brightness for a single line.

### Step 5: Sequential Plotting on a Moving Time Base
A new A-line is acquired every **T_PRF = 1/PRF** seconds. Each A-line is plotted on the screen with:
- **Vertical axis (Y):** depth (proportional to round-trip time)
- **Horizontal axis (X):** time, advanced by one pixel column per new pulse
- **Brightness (Z):** echo amplitude

The display "scrolls" from right to left (older to newer), and a **strip-chart style record** is produced.

### Step 6: Persistence and Refresh
The display is refreshed continuously. Bright dots persist for a few frames (phosphor persistence on CRTs, or trace decay on modern LCDs), producing the characteristic smooth wavy lines that trace moving cardiac structures.

> [!TIP]
> **Why a single fixed line of sight is acceptable for cardiac M-mode:**
> The cardiologist positions the transducer to align the M-line through specific structures of interest (e.g., across the mitral valve tips, or perpendicular to the left ventricular posterior wall). The 2-D B-mode preview is used to *aim* the M-line, then the M-mode is recorded.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind M-Mode

| Engineering Challenge | Why M-mode Solves It |
|----------------------|----------------------|
| Heart valves move very fast (open-close in ~30–80 ms) | Single-line acquisition allows up to 2000 pulses/sec — full temporal sampling |
| Need precise timing of valve events (e.g., PEP, LVET) | Time axis is continuous, resolution = 1/PRF ≈ 0.5 ms |
| Need accurate wall-thickness measurements in systole vs diastole | M-mode gives sub-millimetre depth resolution |
| 2-D imaging is too slow for crisp motion | 2-D mode sacrifices temporal resolution; M-mode trades spatial coverage for time |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for the KTU University Exam — they appear almost every year in either A-mode, M-mode, or ultrasound-ranging questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Range (depth) from round-trip time | metres |
| 2 | $c = f \cdot \lambda$ | Wave speed – frequency – wavelength relation | m/s, Hz, m |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength of ultrasound | m |
| 4 | $d_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$ | Maximum unambiguous depth (no range aliasing) | m |
| 5 | $PRF_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot d_{max}}$ | Maximum PRF allowed for a given imaging depth | Hz |
| 6 | $\Delta d = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$ | Axial resolution (depends on transducer bandwidth) | m |
| 7 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{1}{PRF}$ | Temporal sampling interval of M-mode | s |
| 8 | $N_{cycles} = f \cdot \tau$ | Number of cycles in a pulse (bandwidth–resolution link) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Acoustic impedance (used to compute reflection coefficient) | Rayl (kg/m²·s) |
| 10 | $R = \left( \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$ | Intensity reflection coefficient at interface | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 11 | $\text{Frame rate}_{M\text{-mode}} \approx PRF$ | Temporal resolution of M-mode | fps (Hz) |
| 12 | $\text{Sweep speed} = 25,\ 50,\ 100\ \text{mm/s}$ | Standard M-mode display sweep speeds | mm/s |

> [!WARNING]
> The pipe symbol `|` will break the markdown table above. I have used `\vert` and `\mid` style notation only inside the math block. Always wrap your own exam answer formulas in `$$...$$` to be safe.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

M-mode echocardiography, despite being a 1-D technique, is one of the **most quantitatively reproducible** ultrasound measurements in cardiology. Specific clinical applications include:

- **LV (left ventricular) internal dimensions** in systole and diastole — used to compute fractional shortening, a surrogate for ejection fraction.
- **IVS (inter-ventricular septum)** and **LVPW (LV posterior wall)** thickness measurements.
- **Valve motion timing** — mitral valve E-F slope, E-point septal separation (EPSS).
- **Pericardial effusion** detection — a dark (anechoic) gap between moving epicardium and stationary pericardium.
- **Aortic root motion** studies.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme, M-mode is also conceptually important because it is the *direct temporal precursor* to modern **M-mode–derived Tissue Doppler Imaging (TDI)** and **Speckle-Tracking Echocardiography (STE)**, where motion along predefined lines is tracked over time.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Range Equation from Pulse-Echo Time of Flight

**Problem:** A pulse of ultrasound is emitted at time t = 0. It travels to a tissue interface at depth d, reflects, and returns to the transducer at time t = T. Derive the relationship between d and T.

### Step 1 — Define the variables
Let:
- c = speed of ultrasound in soft tissue (assumed constant at **1540 m/s**)
- d = one-way distance from transducer to reflector (metres)
- T = total round-trip time (seconds)

### Step 2 — One-way travel time
The pulse travels a one-way distance d at speed c. Time required to cover distance d at constant speed c is:

$$
t_{down} = \frac{d}{c}
$$

### Step 3 — Echo returns
The reflected echo travels the same distance d back, taking the same time:

$$
t_{up} = \frac{d}{c}
$$

### Step 4 — Total round-trip time
Adding the two legs:

$$
T = t_{down} + t_{up} = \frac{d}{c} + \frac{d}{c} = \frac{2d}{c}
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for depth d

$$
\boxed{\,d = \frac{c \cdot T}{2}\,}
$$

This is the **fundamental range equation** of all pulse-echo ultrasound (A-mode, M-mode, B-mode). The factor of 2 is the most-tested point in KTU exams.

**Numerical example:** If an echo returns after T = 52 µs, then

$$
d = \frac{1540 \times 52 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.08008}{2} = 0.04004 \ \text{m} \approx 4 \ \text{cm}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Unambiguous Depth from PRF

**Problem:** A pulse-echo system emits pulses at a Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF). The system must finish receiving echoes from one pulse before the next pulse is transmitted, otherwise **range ambiguity** (aliasing in depth) occurs. Derive d_max.

### Step 1 — Pulse repetition period
The interval between successive transmit pulses is the **pulse repetition period**:

$$
T_{PRF} = \frac{1}{PRF}
$$

### Step 2 — Round-trip constraint
For no range ambiguity, the round-trip time T to the deepest target of interest must be less than T_PRF:

$$
T \leq T_{PRF}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the range equation
Using T = 2d/c:

$$
\frac{2 d_{max}}{c} \leq \frac{1}{PRF}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for d_max

$$
2 d_{max} \cdot PRF \leq c \quad\Rightarrow\quad d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}\,}
$$

### Step 5 — Equivalent form
Rearranging gives the maximum allowed PRF for a chosen imaging depth:

$$
PRF_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot d_{max}}
$$

**Numerical example:** For a cardiac probe intended to image d_max = 15 cm = 0.15 m:

$$
PRF_{max} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 0.15} = \frac{1540}{0.30} \approx 5133 \ \text{Hz}
$$

To stay safely below this and avoid range ambiguity, an M-mode cardiac scanner typically uses **PRF ≈ 1000–2000 Hz**, giving a comfortable d_max of ~40–77 cm — well beyond cardiac depths.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Axial Resolution in Terms of Transducer Bandwidth

**Problem:** Axial resolution is the minimum separation along the beam axis at which two reflectors can be distinguished as separate. Show that it depends inversely on the pulse bandwidth.

### Step 1 — Pulse length in space
A transmit pulse contains N cycles of the centre frequency f, and each cycle is one wavelength λ long. The **spatial pulse length (SPL)** is:

$$
SPL = N \cdot \lambda = N \cdot \frac{c}{f}
$$

### Step 2 — Two-pulse separation criterion (Rayleigh criterion)
Two reflectors are just resolvable when the echo from the deeper reflector returns to the transducer just as the trailing edge of the echo from the shallower reflector finishes. This means the **two SPLs fit into the round-trip separation 2·Δd**:

$$
2 \cdot \Delta d_{min} = SPL = N \cdot \frac{c}{f}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta d_{min} = \frac{N \cdot c}{2 f}
$$

### Step 3 — Replace N with the bandwidth relation
For a pulse, the **time-bandwidth product** is approximately N ≈ 1 for a broadband Gaussian pulse, and more generally the spatial pulse length equals the inverse of the bandwidth:

$$
SPL = \frac{c}{\Delta f}
$$

Substituting into the axial-resolution expression:

$$
\Delta d = \frac{SPL}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta d = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f}\,}
$$

where Δf is the **−6 dB fractional bandwidth** of the transducer (in Hz).

**Numerical example:** A broadband cardiac probe with Δf = 2 MHz bandwidth at centre frequency 3.5 MHz gives:

$$
\Delta d = \frac{1540}{2 \times 2 \times 10^6} = \frac{1540}{4 \times 10^6} = 3.85 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m} \approx 0.4 \ \text{mm}
$$

This is why modern broadband transducers achieve sub-millimetre axial resolution — directly visible in M-mode traces of valve leaflets.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Temporal Resolution of M-mode

**Problem:** Show that the effective "frame rate" of M-mode is equal to the PRF.

In a 2-D B-mode system, one frame consists of many A-lines (e.g., 128 lines) and the frame rate is:

$$
f_{frame}^{2D} = \frac{PRF}{N_{lines}}
$$

In M-mode, only **one A-line** is acquired per pulse. Therefore the "frame" of M-mode is itself a single A-line, and a new A-line is emitted every T_PRF = 1/PRF seconds.

$$
\boxed{\,f_{frame}^{M\text{-mode}} = \frac{PRF}{1} = PRF\,}
$$

For PRF = 2000 Hz, M-mode frame rate = **2000 fps** — about **30–60× faster** than typical 2-D B-mode, explaining its unrivaled ability to capture rapid valve motion.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Symbol | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical KTU Value |
|---------|--------|----------|------------------|-------------------|
| Depth | d | $d = cT/2$ | Position of reflector from TOF | 0–15 cm |
| Wavelength | λ | $\lambda = c/f$ | Spatial period | 0.3–0.8 mm |
| Max depth | d_max | $d_{max} = c/(2\cdot PRF)$ | Aliasing-free imaging depth | 15 cm @ 5 kHz |
| Axial resolution | Δd | $\Delta d = c/(2\cdot \Delta f)$ | Smallest separable reflector pair | 0.4–1 mm |
| M-mode frame rate | f_M | $f_M = PRF$ | Temporal sampling rate | 1000–2000 Hz |
| Reflection coefficient | R | $R = ((Z_2-Z_1)/(Z_2+Z_1))^2$ | Echo brightness | 0–1 |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 M-Mode Echocardiography — System Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **block-level functional architecture** of an M-mode echocardiography system, from the transducer pulse emission all the way to the display scroll. This satisfies the KTU expectation that students can draw and label the imaging chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pulser Tx - High Voltage RF Pulse Generator] --> B[Piezoelectric Transducer - Single Crystal]
    B --> C[Body Tissue - Acoustic Beam Path - 1540 m s]
    C --> D[Reflecting Interface - Heart Valve or Wall]
    D --> E[Returning Echo - Pressure Wave]
    E --> F[Receiving Amplifier - TGC Compensator]
    F --> G[Time Gain Compensation TGC]
    G --> H[Envelope Detector and Log Compression]
    H --> I[Brightness Modulation - Z Axis]
    I --> J[Depth Sweep - Y Axis - TOF to Distance]
    K[PRF Clock - 1 to 2 kHz] --> A
    K --> L[Time Base Generator]
    L --> M[Horizontal Sweep - X Axis - Time]
    J --> N[Display Memory - Refresh Buffer]
    I --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Display Monitor - Scrolling M-mode Trace]
    O --> P[Strip Chart Recorder or Digital Archive]
```

**How to read the diagram for an exam answer:**

1. **Pulser + Transmitter (A → B):** A high-voltage spike excites the piezoelectric crystal, producing a short ultrasound pulse.
2. **Wave Propagation (B → C → D → E):** The pulse travels into the chest, hits a moving cardiac structure, and an echo returns.
3. **Reception Chain (F → G → H):** The weak returning echo is amplified, depth-gain-compensated (to correct for attenuation with depth), and envelope-detected.
4. **Display Mapping (I → J → M → O):** The detected echo amplitude becomes brightness (Z), the round-trip time becomes depth (Y), and the PRF clock drives the horizontal scroll (X). All three are combined in the display memory to produce the final scrolling M-mode image.

## 4.2 Functional State Machine — M-mode Acquisition Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Transmit: PRF tick fires pulser
    Transmit --> Propagate: Pulse travels at c = 1540 m s
    Propagate --> Receive: Echo returns from reflector
    Receive --> Detect: Envelope detect and log compress
    Detect --> Plot: Place bright dot at depth d = cT/2
    Plot --> Scroll: Advance X by one column
    Scroll --> Idle: Wait for next PRF tick
    Idle --> [*]: Operator stops acquisition
```

This state machine emphasises the **repetitive, single-line** nature of M-mode — the same five-step cycle executes **1000–2000 times per second**, producing the continuous wavy trace.

## 4.3 Annotated M-mode Echocardiogram (Text Schematic)

```
         ↑  Depth (cm)       |        Time (s) →
         |                    |
   2 cm  |  Chest wall        |  ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ (stationary line)
         |                    |
   3 cm  |  RV anterior wall  |  ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ (mildly moving)
         |                    |
   4 cm  |  IVS (septum)      |  wwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwww (moves up in systole)
         |                    |
   5 cm  |  LV cavity         |  (anechoic dark band)
         |                    |
   7 cm  |  Mitral valve leaf.|  MMMMM~~~~~MMMM~~~~~MMMM  (rapid M-shape)
         |                    |
   9 cm  |  LV posterior wall |  w~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~w  (moves up in systole)
         |                    |
  10 cm  |  Pericardium       |  ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ (stationary)
         |____________________|
                            Sweep speed = 50 mm/s
```

In the above:
- **Horizontal lines** = stationary structures (chest wall, pericardium).
- **Wavy lines** = moving structures (valve leaflets, walls).
- The **M-shape** of the mitral valve is the iconic M-mode signature and is the most commonly asked diagram in KTU exams.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam drawing tip:** When asked to "sketch an M-mode display" in a KTU paper, always show the X-axis as **time** and Y-axis as **depth from transducer (cm)**, label at least one moving structure and one stationary structure, and indicate the **sweep speed** (25 / 50 / 100 mm/s).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]
**Define M-mode echocardiography. What is the physical significance of the horizontal and vertical axes of the M-mode display?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

M-mode (Motion mode) echocardiography is a one-dimensional pulse-echo ultrasound display technique in which the position of reflecting structures along a single stationary ultrasound beam is plotted as a function of time. The returning echoes are displayed as bright dots whose vertical position is proportional to the depth of the reflecting interface, and whose brightness is proportional to the echo amplitude.

- **Vertical axis (Y):** represents the **depth (in cm)** of the reflecting interface below the transducer, computed from the round-trip time using $d = cT/2$, where c = 1540 m/s.
- **Horizontal axis (X):** represents **time (in seconds)**, advanced continuously as new pulses are emitted at the PRF.

**(Marks distribution:** 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for vertical axis, 1 mark for horizontal axis.**)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**The speed of sound in soft tissue is 1540 m/s. An echo from a cardiac structure is received 39 microseconds after transmission. Calculate the depth of the structure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given:
- c = 1540 m/s
- T = 39 µs = 39 × 10⁻⁶ s

Using the range equation:

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot T}{2} = \frac{1540 \times 39 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

$$
d = \frac{0.06006}{2} = 0.03003 \ \text{m} = 3.003 \ \text{cm} \approx 3 \ \text{cm}
$$

**The structure is at a depth of approximately 3 cm from the transducer.** (3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for final answer with unit.)

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an M-mode echocardiography system. Discuss the role of PRF and time-gain compensation (TGC) in the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cardiac ultrasound system operates at a centre frequency of 3.5 MHz with a −6 dB bandwidth of 1.8 MHz. The PRF is set to 1500 Hz. Assuming soft-tissue sound speed of 1540 m/s, compute the **axial resolution**, the **maximum unambiguous imaging depth**, and the **temporal resolution** of the M-mode display. Comment on whether this configuration is suitable for adult echocardiography. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

M-mode echocardiography works on the **pulse-echo principle**. The functional blocks are:

1. **Pulser and Transmitter:** Generates short high-voltage RF pulses (1–3 cycles of the centre frequency) at the PRF rate. These excite the piezoelectric crystal in the transducer.
2. **Transducer (Single Crystal):** Converts electrical pulses to ultrasound and vice versa. The beam is held stationary along a single line of sight.
3. **Body / Reflecting Interface:** The pulse propagates at c = 1540 m/s, hits cardiac interfaces (walls, valves), and returns as echoes.
4. **Receiver Amplifier:** Amplifies the weak returning echoes (which may be 60–100 dB weaker than the transmitted pulse).
5. **Time-Gain Compensation (TGC):** Applies a depth-dependent gain to compensate for the attenuation of ultrasound in tissue (~0.5 dB/cm/MHz). Without TGC, deep structures would appear artificially dark.
6. **Envelope Detector and Log Compression:** Extracts the amplitude envelope of the RF echo and compresses its dynamic range (~60 dB) into the displayable range (~30 dB) via logarithmic compression.
7. **Display Memory and Time-Base:** Maps echo amplitude to brightness (Z), round-trip time to depth (Y), and PRF ticks to horizontal sweep (X).
8. **Display Monitor and Recorder:** Shows the scrolling M-mode trace; may also be archived on strip chart or digital memory.

**Role of PRF (2 marks):**
The PRF determines how often the same M-line is sampled. In M-mode, since only one line is acquired per pulse, the effective frame rate = PRF. Higher PRF gives better temporal resolution (faster event capture) but limits the maximum unambiguous depth via $d_{max} = c/(2\cdot PRF)$. For adult cardiac imaging, PRF is typically 1000–2000 Hz, balancing the need for ~15 cm depth and ~1 ms temporal resolution.

**Role of TGC (2 marks):**
TGC compensates for the exponential attenuation of ultrasound with depth, ensuring that reflectors of identical acoustic impedance mismatch appear with equal brightness regardless of their depth. This is essential for accurate wall-thickness and chamber-dimension measurements from M-mode.

**(Total: 7 marks — 3 for block diagram with labels, 2 for PRF discussion, 2 for TGC discussion.)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- f = 3.5 MHz, Δf = 1.8 MHz, PRF = 1500 Hz, c = 1540 m/s.

**Step 1: Axial resolution (2 marks)**

$$
\Delta d = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 1.8 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\Delta d = \frac{1540}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 4.28 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m} \approx 0.43 \ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum unambiguous depth (2 marks)**

$$
d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 1500} = \frac{1540}{3000}
$$

$$
d_{max} \approx 0.513 \ \text{m} = 51.3 \ \text{cm}
$$

**Step 3: Temporal resolution (1 mark)**

$$
f_{M\text{-mode}} = PRF = 1500 \ \text{Hz} = 1500 \ \text{fps}
$$

**Step 4: Suitability comment (2 marks):**

This configuration is **highly suitable for adult echocardiography**:
- Axial resolution of 0.43 mm easily resolves cardiac wall thickness (typically 8–12 mm) and valve leaflet motion.
- Maximum depth of 51 cm far exceeds adult cardiac depth (~12–15 cm), so there is no risk of range aliasing.
- Temporal resolution of 1500 fps is more than adequate to capture valve opening/closure events (which occur over 30–80 ms).
- The 3.5 MHz centre frequency provides a good trade-off between penetration (sufficient for adult chest walls) and resolution (better than 2 MHz, not as good as 5 MHz but more penetrating).
