### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare A-mode, B-mode, and M-mode ultrasound displays in terms of (i) number of dimensions displayed, (ii) display axis mapping, (iii) clinical application, and (iv) typical use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between maximum unambiguous depth and Pulse Repetition Frequency in a pulse-echo ultrasound system. An M-mode system is set to image up to 20 cm depth in soft tissue. Calculate the maximum PRF that can be used without range ambiguity, and explain what happens if this PRF is exceeded. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

| Feature | A-Mode | B-Mode | M-Mode |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|
| **Dimensionality** | 1-D (single line, no image) | 2-D (cross-sectional image) | 1-D in space + 1-D in time (wavy trace) |
| **Vertical axis (Y)** | Echo amplitude (deflection) | Depth | Depth |
| **Horizontal axis (X)** | Time of flight (fixed trace) | Lateral position of beam | Time (continuously advancing) |
| **Brightness (Z)** | Not used | Echo amplitude (grey scale) | Echo amplitude (grey scale) |
| **Display form** | Spikes on a baseline | 2-D grey-scale image | Wavy bright lines on dark background |
| **Clinical use** | Ophthalmology, echoencephalography (largely obsolete) | General imaging — abdomen, obstetrics, cardiac | Cardiac (valve motion, wall timing) |
| **Temporal resolution** | Very high (essentially analogue) | 30–60 fps | 1000–2000 fps |
| **Example use case** | Biometry of the eye | Fetal imaging, abdominal scans | Mitral valve E-F slope, LV dimensions |

(7 marks: ~1.5 marks per meaningful row, with 1 mark for the dimensionality, 1 mark for the Y-axis, 1 mark for clinical use, and the rest for completeness.)

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Let T_PRF be the pulse repetition period, PRF = 1/T_PRF, and T be the round-trip time to a reflector at depth d. To avoid range ambiguity, all echoes from pulse n must be received before pulse (n+1) is transmitted:

$$
T \leq T_{PRF} = \frac{1}{PRF}
$$

Substituting $T = 2d/c$:

$$
\frac{2d_{max}}{c} \leq \frac{1}{PRF} \quad\Rightarrow\quad d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}
$$

**Numerical calculation (2 marks):**

For d_max = 20 cm = 0.20 m:

$$
PRF_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot d_{max}} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 0.20} = \frac{1540}{0.40} = 3850 \ \text{Hz}
$$

**Consequence of exceeding PRF_max (2 marks):**

If the PRF exceeds 3850 Hz, the system emits a new pulse before echoes from deeper targets have returned. The receiver cannot distinguish echoes from pulse n versus pulse (n+1), and **range ambiguity aliasing** occurs. Deep reflectors appear to "wrap around" to shallower displayed positions, and the M-mode image becomes diagnostically unreliable. In practice, the operator always sets PRF *below* PRF_max to leave a safety margin.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in M-mode / pulse-echo questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $d = cT/2$. Examiners *deliberately* award only partial credit if you write $d = cT$.
> 2. **Mixing up A-mode and M-mode**: A-mode plots *amplitude vs depth*; M-mode plots *brightness vs depth vs time*. Conflating the two costs full marks.
> 3. **Skipping units in numerical answers**. KTU strictly penalises missing units (cm, m/s, Hz).
> 4. **Confusing PRF with frame rate**: in 2-D B-mode, frame rate = PRF/N_lines; in M-mode, frame rate = PRF. This is a favourite trick question.
> 5. **Drawing M-mode with depth on X-axis and time on Y-axis** — this is reversed. Examiners will deduct marks.
> 6. **Not mentioning TGC** when asked about M-mode image quality. TGC is essential for correct brightness uniformity.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ M-mode = **Motion mode** = 1-D ultrasound with **depth on Y-axis**, **time on X-axis**, and **brightness as echo amplitude**.
- ✅ M-mode is generated by repeatedly firing pulses along a **single fixed beam line** at the PRF.
- ✅ Temporal resolution of M-mode = **PRF** (typically 1000–2000 Hz, vastly superior to 2-D B-mode).
- ✅ **Range equation:** $d = cT/2$ where c = 1540 m/s, T = round-trip time. **Never forget the 2.**
- ✅ **Maximum unambiguous depth:** $d_{max} = c/(2\cdot PRF)$.
- ✅ **Axial resolution:** $\Delta d = c/(2\cdot \Delta f)$ — depends on transducer bandwidth, not on PRF.
- ✅ **TGC (Time-Gain Compensation)** corrects for depth-dependent attenuation; without it, deep structures appear falsely echo-poor.
- ✅ The **mitral valve M-shape** is the iconic M-mode signature — be able to sketch it and label the E, F, A, and C points.
- ✅ M-mode is ideal for: LV dimensions, wall thickness, valve motion timing, pericardial effusion, IVS/LVPW measurements.
- ✅ Standard sweep speeds: **25, 50, 100 mm/s** — examiners may ask which is used for which measurement.
- ✅ M-mode is a *precursor* to modern **Tissue Doppler** and **Speckle-Tracking** echocardiography.
- ✅ Units: always quote c in m/s, f in Hz, d in m (or cm), PRF in Hz, frame rate in fps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: B-Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT602 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** B-Mode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **B-Mode (Brightness Mode) Imaging** is a 2-D grayscale ultrasound imaging technique in which the *amplitude envelope* of the time-gated, depth-resolved radio-frequency (RF) echo signal returning from biological tissue is mapped to a discrete **brightness (luminance) level** at the corresponding spatial location of the scan converter, producing a real-time, cross-sectional (tomographic) anatomical image of the interrogated tissue plane.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Course Outcome mapping: **CO1 — *Recall the physical principles of medical image acquisition modalities***), B-Mode is positioned as the most clinically ubiquitous modality derived from the **pulse-echo (active acoustic) imaging principle**. It is the diagnostic imaging workhorse of obstetrics, cardiology, abdominal radiology, and emergency medicine because it is **non-ionising, real-time, portable, and cost-effective**.

### 1.1.1 Historical Lineage — From A-Mode to B-Mode

| Mode | Y-axis Display | X-axis Display | Information Content | Clinical Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A-Mode** | Echo Amplitude | Time (depth) | 1-D depth trace | Largely obsolete (used in ophthalmology) |
| **M-Mode** | Echo Amplitude (brightness) | Time | 1-D motion vs. time | Cardiac wall/echo timing |
| **B-Mode** | Brightness (grayscale) | Lateral position + Time (depth) | **2-D cross-section** | **Universal standard** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Brightness** in B-Mode does NOT correspond to *acoustic intensity* in a linear way. The raw envelope is passed through a **logarithmic compression** stage (typically 40–60 dB dynamic range compression) before grayscale mapping, because the dynamic range of tissue echoes (≳ 100 dB) vastly exceeds the human eye's ~30 dB perceptual range.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Foghorn & The Echo Map"

Imagine you are standing in a thick fog at the edge of a lake. You cannot see anything, so you shout a short, sharp "ping!" (the **transmitted ultrasound pulse**, frequency 2–15 MHz). The sound travels outward, and you listen carefully with a stopwatch.

- The **further away an object** is, the **longer it takes** for the echo to return → this is *time-of-flight depth mapping* (`d = c·t/2`).
- The **loudness (amplitude) of the returning echo** tells you about the object's *acoustic impedance mismatch* — a hard boulder reflects more than a soft log. In B-Mode, this loudness is converted into a **dot of brightness** at the corresponding depth.
- Now imagine doing this with a **line of microphones along the shore** and reconstructing a 2-D map of the lake bed by emitting pings at slightly different angles — that is exactly what a **phased-array or linear-array transducer** does to build a B-Mode image.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "B" and not "2D"?** In the early 1950s, the engineering community used **A** for *amplitude-display* and **M** for *motion-display*. When the brightness-mapped 2-D extension was invented, it was simply called **"B-Mode"** by historical convention — a label that has stuck for over seven decades.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants (Bold as Required)

The following constants are **standardised across the ultrasound engineering community** and must be memorised verbatim for KTU numerical problems:

- **Speed of sound in soft tissue:** $c \approx \mathbf{1540 \; m/s}$
- **Acoustic impedance of soft tissue:** $Z \approx \mathbf{1.6 \times 10^6 \; kg \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}}$ (Rayl)
- **Diagnostic frequency range:** $\mathbf{2 \; MHz}$ to $\mathbf{15 \; MHz}$
- **Speed of sound in air:** $\approx \mathbf{330 \; m/s}$ (used for coupling-gel justification)
- **Speed of sound in bone:** $\approx \mathbf{3500 \; m/s}$ (used in transcranial/trans-osseous problems)
- **Speed of sound in fat:** $\approx \mathbf{1450 \; m/s}$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **assumption of $c = 1540$ m/s** and may award partial credit if a student explicitly states that they are using the standardised tissue value rather than the more accurate 1480–1580 m/s range. Always state the assumption in your answer.

---

## 1.4 Course Outcome (CO) & Bloom's Level Tagging

| KTU 2024 Course Outcome | Bloom's Cognitive Level | Coverage in this Section |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CO1** — Recall the physical principles of medical image acquisition | **L1 (Remember)** | Definition, constants |
| **CO2** — Explain the signal-processing chain | **L2 (Understand)** | Brightness mapping, log compression |
| **CO3** — Apply mathematical models to imaging parameters | **L3 (Apply)** | (carried into Sections 2 & 3) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — The B-Mode Signal Processing Chain

The B-Mode image is the end-product of a **seven-stage processing chain**. Each stage contributes to either the *spatial fidelity*, *contrast resolution*, or *frame rate* of the final image. KTU examiners expect you to be able to **draw and label this chain** in the ESE (End Semester Examination).

The chain is:

1. **Pulse Generation (TX)** — A high-voltage (≈ ±100 V) short burst (typically 1–3 cycles) of RF voltage is applied to the piezoelectric elements of the transducer.
2. **Acoustic Coupling** — A water-based gel ($Z_{gel} \approx 1.5 \times 10^6$ Rayl) is used to bridge the impedance mismatch between the transducer ($Z \approx 30$ MRayl) and the skin ($Z \approx 1.6$ MRayl). This prevents ~99.9% of the energy from being reflected at the air-skin interface.
3. **Wave Propagation & Attenuation** — The acoustic wave travels through tissue, undergoing **attenuation** due to absorption, scattering, and reflection. Attenuation in soft tissue is approximately $\mathbf{0.5 \; dB/cm/MHz}$ (rule of thumb).
4. **Echo Reception (RX)** — Returning echoes strike the same (or adjacent) piezoelectric elements, generating a tiny RF voltage (microvolts to millivolts).
5. **Beamforming** — Time-delayed summation of element signals (in *receive* mode) dynamically focuses the received beam at the depth corresponding to the round-trip time of each sample.
6. **Envelope Detection & Log Compression** — The RF line is **Hilbert-transformed** (or rectified + low-pass filtered) to extract the *amplitude envelope*, then compressed logarithmically from ~100 dB to a 30–50 dB display range.
7. **Scan Conversion & Display** — The Cartesian pixel grid is filled by interpolating from the polar (sector) or trapezoidal (linear) raw scan lines, then mapped to a grayscale look-up table (LUT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why log compression?** A specular reflector (e.g., bladder wall) may return an echo 10,000× larger than a diffuse scatterer in the liver parenchyma. On a linear scale, the weaker echoes would be invisible. Log compression (typically $S_{out} = 20 \log_{10}(A_{in})$) compresses the dynamic range so that both strong and weak reflectors are *simultaneously visible* in a single image.

---

## 2.2 The B-Mode Image — A Pixel-by-Pixel Construction

Every pixel in the final B-Mode image carries **three pieces of metadata**:

- **Lateral position (x):** Determined by the *azimuthal angle* of the active scan line (set by transmit/receive delays in the array).
- **Axial position (y or depth, z):** Determined by the *time-of-flight* of the echo.
- **Brightness (grayscale value):** Determined by the *envelope amplitude* after log compression and time-gain compensation (TGC).

> [!TIP]
> **Time-Gain Compensation (TGC)** is a depth-dependent amplification ramp applied to the RF signal. Because deeper echoes have suffered more attenuation, the receiver gain is *gradually increased* with time (depth) so that echoes from a 10 cm depth appear at a similar brightness to echoes from a 1 cm depth — assuming identical acoustic impedance mismatches. TGC is **operator-adjustable** and is one of the most critical "knobs" on a clinical ultrasound machine.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe character `|` is forbidden inside table cells (it breaks markdown table syntax). For absolute-value and determinant notation, use the LaTeX commands `\vert` and `\mid` respectively.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Symbol Definitions | Typical Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **One-way depth** | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $c$ = speed of sound in tissue, $t$ = round-trip echo time | $c = 1540 \; m/s$, $d$ in m |
| 2 | **Round-trip time** | $t = \dfrac{2 d}{c}$ | same as above | microseconds (μs) for cm depths |
| 3 | **Acoustic impedance** | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | $\rho$ = tissue density, $c$ = local sound speed | $Z_{tissue} \approx 1.6 \times 10^6$ Rayl |
| 4 | **Reflection coefficient (pressure)** | $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | $Z_1, Z_2$ = impedances of media 1, 2 | $0 \le \vert R \vert^2 \le 1$ for intensity |
| 5 | **Transmission coefficient** | $T = \dfrac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | (pressure amplitude) | $T = 1 + R$ |
| 6 | **Intensity reflection coefficient** | $R_I = \vert R \vert^2$ | energy ratio | dimensionless, 0–1 |
| 7 | **Attenuation (soft tissue rule)** | $\alpha \approx 0.5 \;\text{dB/cm/MHz}$ | rule of thumb | used in TGC design |
| 8 | **Wavelength** | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_0}$ | $f_0$ = centre frequency of pulse | 0.1–0.77 mm (clinical range) |
| 9 | **Axial resolution** | $\Delta z = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot BW}$ | $BW$ = transducer fractional-bandwidth × $f_0$ | typically 0.5–2 mm |
| 10 | **Lateral resolution** | $\Delta x \approx \lambda \cdot \dfrac{z}{D}$ or $\lambda \cdot f_{\#}$ | $D$ = aperture, $z$ = depth, $f_{\#} = z/D$ | degrades with depth |
| 11 | **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)** | $PRF \le \dfrac{c}{2 z_{max}}$ | $z_{max}$ = maximum imaging depth | upper bound set by aliasing |
| 12 | **Maximum unambiguous depth** | $z_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$ | governs frame-rate / depth tradeoff | metres |
| 13 | **Number of scan lines per frame** | $N_{lines} = \dfrac{FOV_{lateral}}{pitch}$ | $pitch$ = inter-element spacing (≈ $\lambda$) | 64–256 lines |
| 14 | **Frame rate** | $FR = \dfrac{PRF}{N_{lines} \cdot N_{foc}$ | $N_{foc}$ = number of transmit focal zones | typically 15–60 Hz |
| 15 | **Log compression** | $S_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A}{A_{ref}}\right)$ | $A$ = envelope amplitude | 30–50 dB display range |
| 16 | **Acoustic intensity (pulse-average)** | $I_{PA} = \dfrac{P_{avg}}{Area}$ | used for safety (FDA limits) | $\le 720 \; mW/cm^2$ (FDA Track 3) |
| 17 | **Mechanical Index (MI)** | $MI = \dfrac{P_{r,0.3}}{\sqrt{f_0}}$ | $P_{r,0.3}$ = derated peak rarefactional pressure in MPa | $\le 1.9$ (FDA limit) |
| 18 | **Thermal Index (TI)** | $TI = \dfrac{W_{p}}{W_{deg}}$ | $W_{p}$ = acoustic power, $W_{deg}$ = power raising tissue 1 °C | $\le 6.0$ (FDA limit) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equations 11 and 12 are a coupled pair** — KTU ESE problems frequently ask students to compute *maximum unambiguous depth* given a PRF, or vice versa. Equation 14 is the central **trade-off relationship**: *you cannot simultaneously maximise depth, line density, and frame rate — you must sacrifice one.*

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The B-Mode architecture establishes the **fundamental engineering template** for all subsequent medical imaging modalities covered in this course:

- **Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR)** and **ultrasound B-Mode** share mathematically identical *range-Doppler* signal processing foundations.
- **The pulse-echo time-of-flight principle** taught here is reused (with electromagnetic waves) in **CT (X-ray)** and (with longer wavelengths) in **Ground-Penetrating Radar**.
- **Beamforming + scan conversion** concepts reappear directly in **MRI k-space traversal** and in **PET ring geometry**.
- The **log-compression + grayscale LUT** is the conceptual ancestor of **window/level display** in CT and the **DICOM presentation LUT** standard.

> [!TIP]
> When answering KTU questions on *why B-Mode uses logarithmic compression*, always cite the **three orders of magnitude dynamic range** of tissue echoes versus the **two orders of magnitude** perceptual range of human vision. This is the marker of a top-band answer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section contains the **complete, exhaustive derivations** of the foundational B-Mode equations. No step is skipped; every algebraic transition is explicitly shown. All display equations are isolated with one blank line before and after.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Fundamental Time-of-Flight Depth Equation

### 3.1.1 Physical Setup

Consider a single piezoelectric element emitting a short acoustic pulse at time $t_0 = 0$. The pulse travels through soft tissue at velocity $c$, strikes a planar reflector located at axial distance $d$ from the transducer face, and the echo returns along the same path.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

We start from the most primitive kinematic statement: distance equals rate multiplied by time.

$$
d_{one\text{-}way} = c \cdot t_{one\text{-}way}
$$

The echo must travel **twice** the one-way distance — once going from the transducer to the reflector, and once returning. Therefore the total round-trip time $t$ is twice the one-way time:

$$
t = 2 \cdot t_{one\text{-}way}
$$

Solving the first equation for the one-way time gives us $t_{one\text{-}way} = \dfrac{d}{c}$. Substituting:

$$
t = 2 \cdot \dfrac{d}{c}
$$

Inverting (solving for the unknown depth $d$) is the operation we actually need for image reconstruction:

$$
\dfrac{t \cdot c}{2} = d
$$

Rearranging into the canonical form used in every KTU paper:

$$
\boxed{\, d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2} \,}
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Question (Sample):** A B-Mode scanner uses a centre frequency of $5 \; MHz$ and a speed of sound $c = 1540 \; m/s$. An echo is detected $26 \; \mu s$ after transmission. At what depth is the reflecting structure located?

**Solution:**

We identify the knowns: $c = 1540 \; m/s$, $t = 26 \; \mu s = 26 \times 10^{-6} \; s$.

Substitute into the depth formula:

$$
d = \dfrac{1540 \times 26 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

Evaluating the numerator:

$$
1540 \times 26 = 40040
$$

Therefore:

$$
d = \dfrac{40040 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

$$
d = 20020 \times 10^{-6} \; m
$$

Converting to millimetres:

$$
d = 20.02 \; mm \approx 2.0 \; cm
$$

> **Answer:** The reflector is at a depth of approximately $\mathbf{2 \; cm}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Axial Resolution (Range Resolution)

### 3.2.1 Definition

**Axial resolution** $\Delta z$ is the *minimum separation along the beam axis* at which two distinct reflectors can be resolved as separate echoes (i.e., the pulse echoes from each do not overlap in time).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

A transmit pulse of finite duration contains $N$ acoustic cycles. If the centre frequency is $f_0$ and the pulse contains $N$ cycles, the **pulse length in space** (spatial extent of the acoustic burst) is:

$$
L_{pulse} = N \cdot \lambda = N \cdot \dfrac{c}{f_0}
$$

For two reflectors to be resolved, the trailing edge of the echo from the *nearer* reflector must return *strictly after* the leading edge of the echo from the *farther* reflector has been emitted. Equivalently, the round-trip delay between the two echoes must exceed the pulse duration.

The **pulse duration in time** is:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{N}{f_0}
$$

For two reflectors separated by axial distance $\Delta z$, the difference in round-trip times is:

$$
\Delta t = \dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta z}{c}
$$

Setting $\Delta t = \tau$ (the Rayleigh-like criterion for axial resolution):

$$
\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta z}{c} = \dfrac{N}{f_0}
$$

Solving for $\Delta z$:

$$
\Delta z = \dfrac{c \cdot N}{2 f_0}
$$

Now, recognise that the **transducer bandwidth** $BW$ is defined such that the pulse contains $N = \dfrac{BW}{f_0}$ cycles (for a broad-band Gaussian pulse, $N \approx 1$ per bandwidth ratio). Substituting:

$$
\Delta z = \dfrac{c}{2 f_0} \cdot \dfrac{BW}{f_0} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot BW}
$$

In the canonical form used in KTU papers:

$$
\boxed{\, \Delta z = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot BW} \,}
$$

### 3.2.3 Interpretation

> [!TIP]
> This derivation proves the **central design rule of ultrasound transducers**: to achieve fine axial resolution, you need **large fractional bandwidth** (i.e., short, broad-band pulses). This is why modern transducers use *composite piezo materials* (e.g., PZT-ceramic + epoxy composite) — they have fractional bandwidths of 60–80%, whereas pure PZT crystals have only ~20%.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Unambiguous Depth vs. PRF

### 3.3.1 Physical Setup

If the pulse-repetition period $T_{PRF} = \dfrac{1}{PRF}$ is too long, the scanner "wastes time" between pulses. If it is too short, the echo from a *deep* reflector may return *after* the next pulse has been transmitted — the scanner would then misinterpret that deep echo as coming from a *shallow* depth in the *next* pulse-echo cycle. This phenomenon is called **range ambiguity aliasing**.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

For the deepest echo to unambiguously return within the same pulse cycle, the round-trip time for the maximum depth $z_{max}$ must be less than or equal to the pulse repetition period:

$$
t_{max} \le T_{PRF} = \dfrac{1}{PRF}
$$

But by Derivation 1, the round-trip time for depth $z_{max}$ is:

$$
t_{max} = \dfrac{2 z_{max}}{c}
$$

Combining:

$$
\dfrac{2 z_{max}}{c} \le \dfrac{1}{PRF}
$$

Inverting (and writing as an equality for the maximum-allowable case):

$$
2 z_{max} \cdot PRF = c
$$

Solving for $z_{max}$:

$$
\boxed{\, z_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot PRF} \,}
$$

Equivalently, solving for $PRF$:

$$
\boxed{\, PRF = \dfrac{c}{2 z_{max}} \,}
$$

### 3.3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Question (Sample):** A vascular ultrasound scanner images to a maximum depth of $z_{max} = 12 \; cm$ in soft tissue. What is the highest pulse-repetition frequency (PRF) that can be used without range ambiguity?

**Solution:**

Known: $c = 1540 \; m/s$, $z_{max} = 0.12 \; m$.

$$
PRF = \dfrac{1540}{2 \times 0.12}
$$

$$
PRF = \dfrac{1540}{0.24}
$$

$$
PRF = 6416.67 \; Hz \approx 6.42 \; kHz
$$

> **Answer:** The maximum PRF is approximately $\mathbf{6.42 \; kHz}$. Using a higher PRF would cause deep echoes to be misinterpreted as shallow ones in the next cycle.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Acoustic Impedance & the Reflection Coefficient

### 3.4.1 Acoustic Impedance

For a longitudinal acoustic wave in a fluid/tissue, the **specific acoustic impedance** is defined as the product of the medium's mass density and the speed of sound within it:

$$
\boxed{\, Z = \rho \cdot c \,}
$$

Units: $kg \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}$, traditionally called the **Rayl** (after Lord Rayleigh). The MKS Rayl is exactly $1 \; kg \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}$.

### 3.4.2 Pressure Reflection Coefficient

When a plane acoustic wave strikes a planar interface between two media of impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$, the boundary conditions of continuity of pressure and continuity of normal particle velocity yield (after some algebra):

$$
\boxed{\, R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \,}
$$

This is the ratio of the reflected pressure amplitude to the incident pressure amplitude.

### 3.4.3 Intensity Reflection Coefficient

Intensity is proportional to the *square* of the pressure amplitude. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\, R_I = \vert R \vert^2 = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2 \,}
$$

### 3.4.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Question (Sample):** An acoustic wave travels from soft tissue ($Z_1 = 1.63 \times 10^6$ Rayl) into a gallstone ($Z_2 = 7.0 \times 10^6$ Rayl). Compute (a) the pressure reflection coefficient, and (b) the percentage of incident intensity reflected.

**Solution (a):**

$$
R = \dfrac{(7.0 - 1.63) \times 10^6}{(7.0 + 1.63) \times 10^6}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{5.37}{8.63} = 0.622
$$

So the reflected pressure amplitude is $62.2\%$ of the incident pressure amplitude.

**Solution (b):**

$$
R_I = (0.622)^2 = 0.387
$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$
R_I \times 100\% = 38.7\%
$$

> **Answer:** Approximately $\mathbf{38.7\%}$ of the incident intensity is reflected at the tissue–gallstone interface, which is why gallstones appear as **bright (hyperechoic) structures with strong posterior acoustic shadowing** in B-Mode images.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Frame Rate Trade-off Equation

The frame rate $FR$ (number of complete 2-D B-Mode images produced per second) is determined by how many scan lines can be acquired per unit time, given the PRF and the number of focal-zone acquisitions per line:

$$
\boxed{\, FR = \dfrac{PRF}{N_{lines} \cdot N_{focal}} \,}
$$

If we substitute the maximum-allowable PRF (Derivation 3) for a given $z_{max}$:

$$
PRF_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 z_{max}}
$$

We obtain the **fundamental frame-rate trade-off equation**:

$$
\boxed{\, FR_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 z_{max} \cdot N_{lines} \cdot N_{focal}} \,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the equation KTU examiners love to test. It states the **three-way physical trade-off**: you cannot increase depth, line density, *and* frame rate simultaneously — increasing any one of them forces a decrease in at least one of the others. This is why cardiac ultrasound (high FR needed) uses small $z_{max}$ and $N_{lines}$, while obstetric scans (deep imaging needed) use lower FR.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (per the Mermaid safety rules). Node labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold, italics, or HTML.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete B-Mode Signal Processing Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX1[STAGE 1 PULSE GENERATOR TX VOLTAGE] --> TX2[STAGE 2 ACOUSTIC COUPLING VIA GEL]
    TX2 --> TX3[STAGE 3 WAVE PROPAGATION THROUGH TISSUE]
    TX3 --> TX4[STAGE 4 ECHO RECEPTION BY ARRAY ELEMENTS]
    TX4 --> TX5[STAGE 5 BEAMFORMING TIME DELAYED SUMMATION]
    TX5 --> TX6[STAGE 6 ENVELOPE DETECTION AND LOG COMPRESSION]
    TX6 --> TX7[STAGE 7 SCAN CONVERSION AND GRAYSCALE LUT]
    TX7 --> OUT[FINAL B MODE 2D GRAYSCALE IMAGE]
```

**Reading the diagram (L→R):** The signal path begins at the high-voltage pulser on the left, traverses the patient's tissue as an acoustic wave, returns as a weak RF echo, and is progressively transformed into a viewable 2-D grayscale image on the right. Each stage has an *inverse* path during transmission (beamforming + pulser) and a *forward* path during reception (the chain shown).

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Transducer Array Architectures Used in B-Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph arrA[LINEAR ARRAY RECTANGULAR FOV]
        nodeLA1[Elements arranged in straight line]
        nodeLA2[FOV trapezoidal in shape]
        nodeLA3[Used for vascular and small parts]
    end
    subgraph arrB[CURVILINEAR ARRAY CONVEX FOV]
        nodeCA1[Elements arranged on curved surface]
        nodeCA2[FOV sector shaped diverging]
        nodeCA3[Used for abdominal and obstetric]
    end
    subgraph arrC[PHASED ARRAY SECTOR FOV]
        nodePA1[Small footprint typically 64 elements]
        nodePA2[Electronic steering via delay laws]
        nodePA3[Used for cardiac and intercostal]
    end
    arrA --> q1{Choose probe type}
    arrB --> q1
    arrC --> q1
    q1 --> out1[Drive appropriate beamforming delay tables]
```

**Interpretation:** All three transducer geometries use the **same fundamental B-Mode signal chain** — only the *transmit/receive delay table* (which determines scan-line geometry) differs. This is the engineering elegance of modern multi-purpose ultrasound systems: the *digital beamformer* is reconfigurable in software, allowing the same hardware platform to serve linear, curvilinear, and phased arrays.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — B-Mode Image Construction from a Single Scan Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1[RF Echo A line voltage versus time] --> nodeA2[Time Gain Compensation depth dependent amplification]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Hilbert Transform to extract analytic signal]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Envelope Detection magnitude of analytic signal]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5[Logarithmic Compression 20 log10]
    nodeA5 --> nodeA6[Re sample onto Cartesian pixel grid scan conversion]
    nodeA6 --> nodeA7[Map to grayscale LUT 256 levels]
    nodeA7 --> nodeA8[Display pixel on monitor]
```

**Interpretation:** This is the per-line processing flow. A complete B-Mode frame consists of $N_{lines}$ such pipelines executed in *parallel* by the beamformer hardware, then *interleaved* in the scan converter buffer.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — The Resolution Trade-off Triangle (Frame Rate vs. Depth vs. Line Density)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    p1[Increase maximum imaging depth zmax] --> e1[Forces lower PRF]
    e1 --> e2[Lower frame rate available]
    p2[Increase number of scan lines Nlines] --> e3[More time consumed per frame]
    e3 --> e2
    p3[Increase number of focal zones Nfoc] --> e4[Multiple pulses per line]
    e4 --> e2
    p1 --> c1[Better visualization of deep structures]
    p2 --> c2[Better lateral resolution]
    p3 --> c3[Better focal sharpness]
    e2 --> w1[CONSEQUENCE Real time imaging requires sacrificing at least one parameter]
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **central engineering trade-off** of all B-Mode scanner design. The triangle is closed — every gain has a cost. KTU ESE problems frequently ask students to "explain why a cardiac scanner uses a small footprint and shallow depth" — the answer is the apex of this triangle: high frame rate requires sacrificing depth and line density.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are aligned to **KTU 2024 Scheme** assessment regulations. Marks are distributed as: **Part A = 3 marks (short answer)**, **Part B = 14 marks (deep answer with internal choice)**. Sub-parts are structured as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, consistent with the KTU model question paper template.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define B-Mode imaging. State any two standard physical constants used in B-Mode ultrasound signal processing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
B-Mode (Brightness Mode) is a 2-D grayscale ultrasound imaging technique in which the amplitude envelope of the depth-resolved radio-frequency (RF) echo signal returning from biological tissue is mapped to a discrete brightness level at the corresponding spatial location, producing a real-time, cross-sectional (tomographic) anatomical image of the interrogated tissue plane.

**Constants (1 mark — any two):**
- Speed of sound in soft tissue: $c = 1540 \; m/s$
- Acoustic impedance of soft tissue: $Z \approx 1.6 \times 10^6$ Rayl
- Diagnostic frequency range: $2$–$15 \; MHz$
- Attenuation coefficient in soft tissue: $\alpha \approx 0.5 \; dB/cm/MHz$

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is Time-Gain Compensation (TGC) in a B-Mode scanner? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Time-Gain Compensation is a depth-dependent amplification ramp applied to the received RF signal in which the receiver gain is gradually increased with echo arrival time (i.e., with depth) to compensate for the progressive attenuation suffered by the acoustic wave as it travels through tissue.

**Necessity (1.5 marks):**
Because attenuation in soft tissue is approximately $0.5 \; dB/cm/MHz$, echoes returning from a $10 \; cm$ depth will be ~50 dB weaker than echoes from a $1 \; cm$ depth (at 5 MHz). Without TGC, deep structures would appear artificially hypoechoic (dark) even if they have identical acoustic impedance to shallow structures. TGC restores the *true* relative echogenicity of tissues at different depths.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Deep-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** For every Part B question on B-Mode, students consistently lose marks in the following three ways:
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the time-of-flight equation ($d = c \cdot t / 2$, NOT $d = c \cdot t$). This single error costs 1–2 marks per question and is the *single most common mistake* flagged in KTU valuation reports.
> 2. **Stating the speed of sound incorrectly** — using $c = 1500 \; m/s$ or $c = 330 \; m/s$ (the air value) instead of the standardised tissue value $c = 1540 \; m/s$.
> 3. **Confusing the intensity reflection coefficient** ($\vert R \vert^2$) with the *pressure* reflection coefficient ($R$). The intensity ratio is what governs *energy deposition* and B-Mode brightness; the pressure ratio is what governs the *received voltage* on the transducer. Examiners *expect* you to specify which one you are computing.

---

### **Question B — Option A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete B-Mode signal processing chain from pulse generation to display. State the function of each stage in one sentence.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasound scanner operates at a centre frequency of $3.5 \; MHz$ in soft tissue ($c = 1540 \; m/s$). The transducer has a usable bandwidth of $2 \; MHz$. A patient is being scanned to a maximum depth of $15 \; cm$.
    (i) Compute the axial resolution of the system.
    (ii) Compute the maximum allowable PRF without range-ambiguity aliasing.
    (iii) If the system acquires 128 scan lines per frame with a single transmit focal zone, what is the maximum achievable frame rate?
**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the seven-stage chain (refer to SECTION 4.1 of these notes). The examiner expects the seven blocks: Pulse Generator → Coupling → Propagation → Reception → Beamforming → Envelope Detection + Log Compression → Scan Conversion + Display.

**Stage Functions (4 marks — 0.5 to 1 mark each):**

1. **Pulse Generator:** Produces a high-voltage (≈ ±100 V), short (1–3 cycle) RF burst that excites the piezoelectric elements.
2. **Acoustic Coupling:** Water-based gel bridges the impedance mismatch between the high-$Z$ transducer and the low-$Z$ skin, preventing near-total reflection at the air-skin interface.
3. **Wave Propagation:** The acoustic wave travels through tissue, undergoing frequency-dependent attenuation (~0.5 dB/cm/MHz).
4. **Echo Reception:** Returning echoes strike the piezoelectric elements, inducing microvolt-to-millivolt RF voltages proportional to the local acoustic impedance contrast.
5. **Beamforming:** Time-delayed summation of element signals dynamically focuses the received beam at each depth, producing one focused RF scan line.
6. **Envelope Detection + Log Compression:** The Hilbert transform (or rectification + LPF) extracts the amplitude envelope, which is then logarithmically compressed from ~100 dB dynamic range to ~30–50 dB.
7. **Scan Conversion + Display:** The polar/trapezoidal scan-line data is resampled onto a Cartesian pixel grid and mapped to a 256-level grayscale LUT for monitor display.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $f_0 = 3.5 \; MHz$, $c = 1540 \; m/s$, $BW = 2 \; MHz$, $z_{max} = 0.15 \; m$, $N_{lines} = 128$, $N_{foc} = 1$.

**(i) Axial Resolution (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta z = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot BW}
$$

$$
\Delta z = \dfrac{1540}{2 \times 2 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{1540}{4 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\Delta z = 3.85 \times 10^{-4} \; m = 0.385 \; mm
$$

> **Answer:** Axial resolution $\approx \mathbf{0.385 \; mm}$ (sub-millimetre, clinically excellent for abdominal imaging).

**(ii) Maximum PRF (2 marks):**

$$
PRF_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 z_{max}}
$$

$$
PRF_{max} = \dfrac{1540}{2 \times 0.15} = \dfrac{1540}{0.30}
$$

$$
PRF_{max} = 5133.3 \; Hz \approx 5.13 \; kHz
$$

> **Answer:** Maximum PRF $\approx \mathbf{5.13 \; kHz}$.

**(iii) Maximum Frame Rate (3 marks):**

$$
FR_{max} = \dfrac{PRF_{max}}{N_{lines} \cdot N_{foc}}
$$

$$
FR_{max} = \dfrac{5133.3}{128 \times 1}
$$

$$
FR_{max} = 40.1 \; Hz
$$

> **Answer:** Maximum frame rate $\approx \mathbf{40 \; Hz}$, which is well within the >24 Hz threshold required for smooth real-time perception and is more than adequate for obstetric and abdominal B-Mode imaging.

---

### **Question B — Option B (Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Define acoustic impedance and the pressure reflection coefficient. An acoustic wave travels from soft tissue ($Z_1 = 1.63 \times 10^6$ Rayl) into fat ($Z_2 = 1.38 \times 10^6$ Rayl). Compute the percentage of incident *intensity* reflected at this interface, and comment on the B-Mode appearance of such a boundary.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, explain the construction of a B-Mode image from a single RF scan line. Include the role of the Hilbert transform and log compression in your answer. State, with justification, whether B-Mode displays amplitude or intensity.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks**

**Definitions (2 marks):**

**Acoustic impedance** $Z$ of a medium is the product of its mass density $\rho$ and the speed of sound $c$ in that medium: $Z = \rho \cdot c$. Units: Rayl ($kg \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}$).

**Pressure reflection coefficient** $R$ is the ratio of the reflected pressure amplitude to the incident pressure amplitude at a planar interface between two media of impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$:

$$
R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}
$$

**Calculation (4 marks):**

Substituting $Z_1 = 1.63 \times 10^6$ Rayl and $Z_2 = 1.38 \times 10^6$ Rayl:

$$
R = \dfrac{(1.38 - 1.63) \times 10^6}{(1.38 + 1.63) \times 10^6}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{-0.25}{3.01} = -0.0831
$$

The **negative sign** indicates a phase inversion of the reflected wave (the wave reflects from a *lower-impedance* medium).

The **intensity reflection coefficient** is:

$$
R_I = \vert R \vert^2 = (-0.0831)^2 = 0.00690
$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$
R_I \times 100\% = 0.69\%
$$

> **Answer:** Approximately $\mathbf{0.69\%}$ of the incident intensity is reflected.

**B-Mode Comment (1 mark):** Because the intensity reflection is small (<1%), the soft-tissue/fat interface will appear as a **mildly hyperechoic (slightly bright) line** in the B-Mode image. This is the typical appearance of tissue-lipid interfaces (e.g., renal sinus fat, perinephric fat), which are routinely visible as bright lines in abdominal ultrasound.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the per-line processing chain (refer to SECTION 4.3 of these notes). Key blocks: RF line → TGC → Hilbert Transform → Envelope Detection → Log Compression → Scan Conversion → Grayscale LUT → Display.

**Role of Hilbert Transform (2 marks):**
The Hilbert transform converts the real-valued RF signal $x(t)$ into its **analytic signal** $x_a(t) = x(t) + j \cdot \hat{x}(t)$, where $\hat{x}(t)$ is the Hilbert transform of $x(t)$. The magnitude $\vert x_a(t) \vert$ yields the **amplitude envelope** of the RF echo, which is a slowly-varying positive function suitable for grayscale display. (Equivalently, the system may use rectification + low-pass filtering, but the Hilbert approach is mathematically cleaner.)

**Role of Log Compression (1 mark):**
Log compression maps the large dynamic range of envelope amplitudes (~100 dB) into the much smaller display range (~30–50 dB) of the monitor, ensuring that both strong and weak reflectors are visible simultaneously.

**Amplitude or Intensity? (1 mark):**
The displayed pixel brightness is proportional to the **logarithm of the envelope amplitude** (which is proportional to the *pressure* reflection), not directly to intensity. However, in dB-scaled displays, the relationship to intensity is implicit through the $20 \log_{10}$ convention (since intensity is proportional to amplitude squared). Hence B-Mode displays are best described as **log-compressed pressure-amplitude** images, with intensity encoded implicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Print this page before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] **Definition:** B-Mode = Brightness-Mode 2-D grayscale ultrasound; amplitude envelope mapped to pixel luminance.
- [x] **Canonical equation #1:** $d = c t / 2$ — the round-trip time-of-flight depth formula. The factor of **2 is sacred**.
- [x] **Canonical constant:** $c_{tissue} = 1540 \; m/s$. State this assumption in every numerical answer.
- [x] **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho c$. Soft-tissue value $\approx 1.6 \times 10^6$ Rayl. Use it for reflection-coefficient problems.
- [x] **Reflection coefficients:** $R = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$ for *pressure*; $\vert R \vert^2$ for *intensity*. Specify which one you compute.
- [x] **Axial resolution:** $\Delta z = c / (2 \cdot BW)$ — depends on bandwidth, NOT centre frequency. Higher bandwidth = finer resolution.
- [x] **Lateral resolution:** depends on wavelength and depth ($\Delta x \approx \lambda \cdot z / D$). Degrades with depth — this is a *fundamental* property of focused beams.
- [x] **PRF–Depth trade-off:** $z_{max} = c / (2 \cdot PRF)$. Use the larger of the two derivations you will need.
- [x] **Frame rate trade-off:** $FR = PRF / (N_{lines} \cdot N_{foc})$. Cannot have high depth, high line density, AND high frame rate simultaneously.
- [x] **Dynamic range issue:** Raw echoes span ~100 dB; human eye perceives ~30 dB → **log compression is mandatory**, not optional.
- [x] **TGC (Time-Gain Compensation):** Depth-dependent gain ramp that compensates for $0.5 \; dB/cm/MHz$ tissue attenuation.
- [x] **Safety indices:** $MI \le 1.9$ and $TI \le 6.0$ (FDA Track 3 limits). Examiners may ask for definitions.
- [x] **Three transducer types:** Linear (trapezoidal FOV, vascular), Curvilinear (sector-diverging FOV, abdomen), Phased (sector FOV, cardiac). All share the same B-Mode signal chain.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Do NOT confuse $R$ (pressure reflection coefficient) with $\vert R \vert^2$ (intensity reflection coefficient). The B-Mode image brightness is related to *intensity* (or to the log of the envelope amplitude, which is proportional to pressure amplitude). State the relationship explicitly in your answer.
- [x] **Examiners' favourite derivations:** (1) Derivation of $d = c t / 2$; (2) Derivation of $\Delta z = c / (2 BW)$; (3) Derivation of the PRF–depth trade-off; (4) Derivation of the frame-rate trade-off. Memorise the *order of every algebraic step*, not just the final equation.

> **End of Module 1 / Topic: B-Mode notes — KTU 2024 Scheme (PCBRT602).**

topics.title: Principles of Doppler Ultrasonic Imaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT602 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles of Doppler Ultrasonic Imaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Doppler Ultrasonic Imaging** is a non-invasive medical imaging modality that exploits the **Doppler Effect** — the frequency shift experienced by a wave when there is relative motion between the wave source and the observer — to measure the **velocity and direction of blood flow** within the cardiovascular system.

In the medical context, the "source" is the **ultrasound transducer** emitting a high-frequency sound wave (typically **2 MHz to 10 MHz**), and the "observer" is the same transducer (operating in receive mode) detecting echoes back-scattered from **red blood cells (RBCs)** moving within vessels or cardiac chambers. The minute frequency difference between transmitted and received waves — known as the **Doppler Shift (Δf)** — is processed to yield real-time hemodynamic information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Doppler principle is the **physical foundation** for:
> 1. Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler
> 2. Pulsed Wave (PW) Doppler / Spectral Doppler
> 3. Color Flow Doppler Imaging (CFDI)
> 4. Power Doppler Imaging

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants in Doppler Ultrasound:**
> - Speed of sound in soft tissue, **c = 1540 m/s**
> - Standard diagnostic ultrasound frequency range, **f₀ = 2 MHz – 10 MHz**
> - Blood flow velocity range, **v = 0.1 m/s – 5 m/s** (clinical range)
> - Speed of blood (typical), **~0.5 m/s** in arteries, **~0.15 m/s** in veins

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are standing on a railway platform and a train is approaching you blowing its whistle. As the train comes *toward* you, the whistle sounds **higher pitched** (compressed wavelengths, higher frequency). As it moves *away* from you, the whistle sounds **lower pitched** (stretched wavelengths, lower frequency). This is the **Doppler Effect** — discovered by Austrian physicist **Christian Andreas Doppler in 1842**.

Now translate this analogy to the medical scenario:

- The **train** = moving **red blood cells** inside your artery.
- The **whistle** = **ultrasound wave** sent by the transducer.
- The **stationary listener (you)** = the **transducer** listening for the echo.

When the transducer sends a sound wave into the body:
1. It hits moving RBCs.
2. RBCs act as **moving reflectors**, sending back an echo whose frequency has shifted.
3. The transducer receives this shifted echo and the system computes the **frequency difference (Δf)**.
4. From Δf, the system calculates **blood flow velocity and direction**.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick memory hook:** "Coming = Higher pitch, Going = Lower pitch" → This is exactly how Doppler ultrasound detects flow direction. Toward the probe = **positive shift** (above baseline), away = **negative shift** (below baseline).

## 1.3 Why Doppler Ultrasound Matters in Medical Imaging

- **Non-invasive** and **ionizing-radiation-free** (unlike CT/angiography).
- Provides **real-time** hemodynamic information.
- Used to diagnose **stenosis, thrombosis, regurgitation, valvular heart disease, fetal heart rate**, and **tumor vascularity**.
- Forms the basis of **echocardiography**, **obstetric ultrasound**, and **vascular studies**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Doppler Effect — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The Doppler effect in ultrasound can be broken into two successive shifts (because the RBC acts both as a moving receiver AND a moving re-transmitter):

### Step 1: Wave hits moving RBC (First Doppler shift)
The moving red blood cell "sees" a different frequency than the one transmitted by the probe.

### Step 2: RBC re-emits (reflects) the wave back to the probe (Second Doppler shift)
Because the RBC is still moving as it re-radiates, the wave undergoes another frequency shift.

### Step 3: Transducer receives the back-scattered echo
The system compares the original transmitted frequency `f₀` with the received frequency `f_r` to extract Δf.

### Step 4: Signal processing chain
The received RF signal is **demodulated** (phase quadrature detection) → the in-phase (I) and quadrature (Q) components are extracted → the **instantaneous phase** is computed → **phase differentiation** over time gives the Doppler shift → mapped to a velocity display.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two shifts give a factor of 2?**
> Because in medical ultrasound, the **probe is stationary** and the **scatterer (RBC) is moving**. The signal undergoes the Doppler shift on the way IN (received by RBC) and on the way OUT (re-emitted by RBC). Both shifts add up, giving the famous factor of **2** in the Doppler equation.

### 2.2 The Cosine of the Angle (Doppler Angle, θ)

The Doppler equation contains `cos θ`, where **θ is the angle between the ultrasound beam axis and the direction of blood flow**.

- If **θ = 0°** (beam parallel to flow) → `cos θ = 1` → **maximum shift** detected.
- If **θ = 90°** (beam perpendicular to flow) → `cos θ = 0` → **no shift detected** (flow appears invisible to Doppler).
- If **θ = 60°** → `cos θ = 0.5` → only half of the true shift is detected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Practice (KTU Examiner Emphasis):**
> Sonographers are trained to keep the **Doppler angle below 60°** to minimize velocity measurement error. An **angle correction cursor** is placed on the B-mode image parallel to the vessel wall.

### 2.3 Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler — Operational Logic

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| Transducers | Two separate crystals (one transmits, one receives) |
| Waveform | Continuous sinusoidal emission |
| PRF | Unlimited (no pulses) |
| Aliasing | **No aliasing** — can measure very high velocities |
| Range Resolution | **None** — cannot localize depth of flow |
| Best Use | Quantifying high-velocity jets (e.g., aortic stenosis) |

### 2.4 Pulsed Wave (PW) Doppler — Operational Logic

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| Transducer | Single crystal (transmit + receive) |
| Waveform | Short pulses emitted at Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) |
| PRF | Limited → introduces **Nyquist limit** |
| Aliasing | **Yes** — occurs when velocity exceeds Nyquist limit |
| Range Resolution | **Yes** — sample volume (gate) selects specific depth |
| Best Use | Localized flow measurement at a chosen anatomical site |

### 2.5 Color Flow Doppler Imaging (CFDI) — Operational Logic

- Acquired in **real-time 2D B-mode** scanning.
- Uses **autocorrelation** technique on multiple pulses (typically 8–16 pulses per scan line).
- Encodes:
  - **Red** = flow **toward** transducer
  - **Blue** = flow **away from** transducer
  - **Color brightness** = velocity magnitude
- Multi-gated: each pixel has its own sample volume.

### 2.6 Aliasing and the Nyquist Limit

> [!WARNING]
> **Aliasing** is a *spatial-temporal sampling artifact* in PW Doppler and Color Doppler. It occurs when the Doppler shift frequency exceeds **half the PRF** (the Nyquist limit).

Visual symptom: the spectral waveform "wraps around" the baseline, or color flow shows a hue reversal (e.g., red becomes blue in the center of a high-velocity jet).

**Solutions to aliasing:**
- **Increase PRF** (push Nyquist limit higher).
- **Decrease transmitted frequency** (lower f₀ → lower Δf).
- **Shift the baseline** of the spectral display.
- **Switch to CW Doppler** (no aliasing).
- **Reduce depth** (shorter round-trip time allows higher PRF).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Doppler Shift (general) | $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ | Hz | Fundamental equation |
| 2 | Blood Velocity (from Δf) | $v = \dfrac{\Delta f \, c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | m/s | Inversion of Doppler equation |
| 3 | Doppler angle (typical) | $\theta = 0^\circ \text{ to } 60^\circ$ | degrees | Keep < 60° clinically |
| 4 | Speed of sound in tissue | $c = 1540$ | m/s | Standard medical constant |
| 5 | Nyquist Frequency | $f_{Nyq} = \dfrac{PRF}{2}$ | Hz | Maximum measurable Δf |
| 6 | Maximum measurable velocity | $v_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot PRF}{4 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | m/s | Aliasing threshold |
| 7 | Range equation (depth) | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | m | Round-trip time t |
| 8 | Maximum depth (from PRF) | $d_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$ | m | PRF-limited depth |
| 9 | Doppler Power (Power Doppler) | $P = \vert I^2 + Q^2 \vert$ | W (relative) | Angle-independent |
| 10 | Time-averaged velocity (TAV) | $TAV = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v(t)\,dt$ | m/s | Used in TAMEAN maps |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam hack:** Whenever you see a Doppler problem, immediately check **three** things:
> (1) What is the angle θ?
> (2) What is f₀?
> (3) Is the system PW or CW? (decides whether aliasing is possible).

### 2.8 Real-World Utility of Doppler Ultrasound

| Clinical Domain | Doppler Application |
|---|---|
| Cardiology | Valve stenosis/regurgitation, ejection fraction, congenital defects |
| Obstetrics | Fetal heart rate, umbilical cord flow, placental perfusion |
| Vascular Surgery | Carotid stenosis, DVT, arterial occlusions |
| Radiology | Tumor vascularity, organ perfusion |
| Anesthesiology | Intra-operative flow monitoring |
| Emergency Medicine | FAST scans, traumatic injury assessment |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Doppler Shift Equation for Medical Ultrasound

### Setup and Assumptions

Let:
- $f_0$ = transmitted frequency from the stationary transducer (Hz).
- $v$ = velocity of the red blood cell (RBC), positive when moving **toward** the transducer (m/s).
- $c$ = speed of ultrasound in soft tissue (m/s).
- $\theta$ = angle between the ultrasound beam axis and the direction of blood flow.
- $f_r$ = frequency of the received echo.

### Derivation: Two Successive Doppler Shifts

**Step 1 — Frequency perceived by the moving RBC (1st shift)**

A stationary source (transducer) emits a wave of frequency $f_0$. The RBC is moving with velocity component $v_r$ along the beam axis, where:

$$
v_r = v \cos\theta
$$

For a stationary observer (here, conceptually, the RBC is the observer), the received frequency would be $f_0$. But since the RBC is moving **toward** the source, the effective wavelength encountered is compressed. The frequency the RBC "hears" is:

$$
f' = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \right)
$$

> **Logic:** If the RBC moves toward the source, it intercepts wave crests at a faster rate → frequency increases. The factor $(c + v\cos\theta)/c$ accounts for the relative closing speed between the wave (speed $c$) and the moving scatterer (speed $v\cos\theta$).

**Step 2 — Frequency re-emitted by the moving RBC (2nd shift)**

The RBC now re-radiates (back-scatters) the wave at frequency $f'$. From the perspective of the stationary transducer (acting as observer to a moving source), the received frequency $f_r$ is:

$$
f_r = f' \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)
$$

> **Logic:** The RBC is now the moving source. The denominator $(c - v\cos\theta)$ represents the rate at which the wave "stretches away" from the receding RBC. This is the classical Doppler observer formula applied with the RBC as a moving source.

**Step 3 — Substituting and simplifying**

Substituting $f'$ from Step 1 into Step 2:

$$
f_r = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \right) \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)
$$

The $c$ in the numerator and denominator of the two fractions cancel:

$$
f_r = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)
$$

> **Logic:** This is the full relativistic form, applicable to *any* speed. In medical ultrasound, however, $v \ll c$, so we make the standard non-relativistic approximation.

**Step 4 — Non-relativistic approximation ($v \ll c$)**

For medical ultrasound, the blood velocity is at most ~5 m/s while $c = 1540$ m/s, so $v/c \approx 0.003$. The term $v\cos\theta$ is negligible compared to $c$. We can apply the small-velocity limit:

$$
\frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} \approx 1 + \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c}
$$

This comes from the identity:

$$
\frac{1 + x}{1 - x} \approx 1 + 2x \quad \text{for} \quad \vert x \vert \ll 1
$$

with $x = v\cos\theta/c$.

Therefore:

$$
f_r \approx f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Final Doppler shift expression**

The Doppler shift is defined as $\Delta f = f_r - f_0$:

$$
\Delta f = f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c} \right) - f_0
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}}
$$

> **Logic recap:** The factor **2** appears because the wave undergoes the Doppler shift **twice** — once on the way to the moving RBC and once on the way back. The cosine accounts for the geometric projection of the velocity vector onto the beam axis.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A 5 MHz ultrasound probe is used to insonate a carotid artery. The angle between the beam and flow direction is 60°. If the measured Doppler shift is 3.1 kHz, calculate the blood flow velocity. (Use $c = 1540$ m/s).

**Solution:**

Given: $f_0 = 5 \times 10^6$ Hz, $\theta = 60°$, $\Delta f = 3.1 \times 10^3$ Hz, $c = 1540$ m/s.

We have:

$$
v = \frac{\Delta f \cdot c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}
$$

Step 1: Compute $\cos 60° = 0.5$.

Step 2: Substitute:

$$
v = \frac{(3.1 \times 10^3)(1540)}{2 \cdot (5 \times 10^6) \cdot 0.5}
$$

Step 3: Numerator:

$$
(3.1 \times 10^3)(1540) = 4.774 \times 10^6
$$

Step 4: Denominator:

$$
2 \cdot (5 \times 10^6) \cdot 0.5 = 5 \times 10^6
$$

Step 5: Divide:

$$
v = \frac{4.774 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^6} = 0.9548 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Answer:** $v \approx 0.95$ m/s (normal systolic flow in the carotid artery).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Maximum Velocity Before Aliasing

**Problem:** A 4 MHz PW Doppler system operates at a PRF of 8 kHz. What is the maximum measurable blood velocity at $\theta = 0°$? What happens if the actual velocity exceeds this limit?

**Solution:**

Maximum measurable Doppler shift (Nyquist limit):

$$
\Delta f_{max} = \frac{PRF}{2} = \frac{8000}{2} = 4000 \text{ Hz}
$$

Rearranging the Doppler equation for velocity:

$$
v_{max} = \frac{\Delta f_{max} \cdot c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta} = \frac{4000 \cdot 1540}{2 \cdot (4 \times 10^6) \cdot 1}
$$

Step 1: Numerator: $4000 \times 1540 = 6.16 \times 10^6$.

Step 2: Denominator: $2 \times 4 \times 10^6 = 8 \times 10^6$.

Step 3: Divide:

$$
v_{max} = \frac{6.16 \times 10^6}{8 \times 10^6} = 0.77 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Answer:** $v_{max} \approx 0.77$ m/s. If actual velocity exceeds this, **aliasing** occurs — the high-velocity portion of the waveform wraps around the baseline. Solution: switch to CW Doppler, increase PRF, or lower $f_0$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Doppler Modalities

| Parameter | CW Doppler | PW Doppler | Color Doppler | Power Doppler |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Velocity info | Yes (spectral) | Yes (spectral) | Yes (color-coded) | No (intensity only) |
| Direction info | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| Depth localization | No | Yes (sample volume) | Yes (2D map) | Yes (2D map) |
| Aliasing | No | Yes | Yes | No |
| Angle dependence | Yes ($\cos\theta$) | Yes ($\cos\theta$) | Yes | No (angle-independent) |
| Sensitivity to low flow | High | Moderate | Moderate | Very High |
| Best for | High-velocity jets | Localized flow | Real-time 2D mapping | Slow flow, perfusion |
| Computational load | Low | Low | High (autocorrelation) | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Doppler Ultrasound Signal Processing Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transmitter Oscillator f0] --> B[Transducer Probe]
    B --> C[Patient Body - Moving RBCs]
    C --> D[Backscattered Echo fr]
    D --> E[Receive Amplifier TGC]
    E --> F[Quadrature Demodulator]
    F --> G[Inphase I Component]
    F --> H[Quadrature Q Component]
    G --> I[Phase Computation arctan QI]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Doppler Shift Extractor]
    J --> K{Velocity Mapping}
    K --> L[Spectral Display PW]
    K --> M[Color Map Autocorrelation]
    K --> N[Audio Output Speaker]
    L --> O[Final Clinical Image]
    M --> O
```

> **Reading guide:** The signal flows from the oscillator on the left to the clinical image on the right. The **Quadrature Demodulator** splits the RF signal into I and Q, which is the cornerstone of Doppler phase detection. The final **Velocity Mapping** block branches into three clinical displays.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Types of Doppler Ultrasound Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Doppler Ultrasound Systems] --> B[Continuous Wave CW]
    A --> C[Pulsed Wave PW Spectral]
    A --> D[Color Flow Doppler CFDI]
    A --> E[Power Doppler PDI]
    B --> B1[Two Crystals - No Aliasing - High Velocity]
    C --> C1[Single Crystal - Range Gating - Aliasing Possible]
    D --> D1[Autocorrelation - 2D Color Map - Real Time]
    E --> E1[Energy Based - No Velocity - No Aliasing]
```

## 4.3 Geometric Schematic — Doppler Angle and Velocity Projection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Transducer Probe] -->|Beam Axis| B[Blood Vessel]
    B --> C[Red Blood Cells Flow Direction v]
    A --> D[Velocity Component Along Beam v cos theta]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Only this component is detected by Doppler]
    E --> F[If theta = 0 deg, full velocity measured]
    E --> G[If theta = 90 deg, no Doppler shift detected]
    E --> H[If theta = 60 deg, only half measured]
```

## 4.4 Flowchart — Aliasing Diagnosis and Correction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spectral waveform wraps around baseline?] -->|Yes| B[Aliasing Detected]
    A -->|No| C[Normal Display]
    B --> D{Is depth shallow?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Increase PRF]
    D -->|No| F[Lower transmit frequency f0]
    E --> G{Velocity still exceeds Nyquist?}
    F --> G
    G -->|Yes| H[Shift Spectral Baseline]
    H --> I{Still aliasing?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Switch to CW Doppler]
    I -->|No| K[Diagnosis Complete]
    J --> K
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Complete Doppler Imaging Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        A1[Clinical Question - What is being investigated]
        A2[Transducer Selection 2 to 10 MHz]
        A3[B mode Image Acquisition - Anatomy First]
    end
    subgraph ACQUISITION[Doppler Acquisition]
        B1[Position Sample Volume on B mode]
        B2[Set Doppler Angle below 60 deg]
        B3[Choose PRF and Gain]
        B4[Acquire Doppler Data Pulses]
    end
    subgraph PROCESSING[Signal Processing]
        C1[Quadrature Detection I and Q]
        C2[Wall Filter Clutter Rejection]
        C3[Autocorrelation or FFT]
        C4[Velocity and Variance Estimation]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Display and Output]
        D1[Spectral Trace PW]
        D2[Color Map CFDI]
        D3[Numerical Velocity Values]
        D4[Audio Doppler Output]
    end
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
    C4 --> D3
    C4 --> D4
```
