# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled precisely on **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** with the standard Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks — Internal Choice) structure. Each question is tagged with a simulated past-year reference for familiarity.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Illumination–Reflectance Model used in Homomorphic Filtering. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Illumination–Reflectance Model expresses an image $f(x,y)$ as the product of two components:

$$f(x,y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$$

where:

- $i(x,y)$ = illumination component, representing the amount of source light falling on the scene. It is **slowly varying** and has energy concentrated in **low spatial frequencies**.
- $r(x,y)$ = reflectance component, representing the light reflected by objects/tissues. It varies **rapidly** with sharp transitions, and its energy lies in **high spatial frequencies**.

In medical imaging, $i(x,y)$ models scanner illumination, RF coil bias, or X-ray source falloff, while $r(x,y)$ carries the diagnostic anatomical information.

---

### Question 2: Why is a logarithmic transformation applied before Fourier filtering in the Homomorphic Filter? [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The logarithmic transformation is applied to **convert the multiplicative image-formation model into an additive form** so that linear Fourier filtering can be applied. Starting from:

$$f(x,y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$$

Taking the natural logarithm yields:

$$\ln f(x,y) = \ln i(x,y) + \ln r(x,y)$$

Since the Fourier Transform is linear, it can now independently process the illumination and reflectance components in the frequency domain. Without this step, the multiplicative model would lead to convolution in the frequency domain, making independent component separation impossible. Hence, $\ln(\cdot)$ is the **homomorphic mapping** that enables linear processing of a non-linear phenomenon.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the complete mathematical expression for the enhanced image $g(x,y)$ produced by a homomorphic filter, starting from the illumination–reflectance model. Clearly state the role of each transformation step. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a Gaussian homomorphic filter $H(u,v)$ for enhancing a chest X-ray image. Specify the choice of parameters $\gamma_L$, $\gamma_H$, $D_0$, and $c$ with justification. Explain how the filter behaves at low and high spatial frequencies. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (a)

**Step 1 — Image Formation Model**

The observed medical image is modeled as:

$$f(x,y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$$

**Step 2 — Logarithmic Decoupling**

Apply $\ln(\cdot)$ pixel-wise:

$$z(x,y) = \ln f(x,y) = \ln i(x,y) + \ln r(x,y)$$

This converts the multiplicative model to an additive one (homomorphic mapping).

**Step 3 — Forward Fourier Transform**

$$Z(u,v) = \mathcal{F}\{z(x,y)\} = F_I(u,v) + F_R(u,v)$$

**Step 4 — Frequency-Domain Filtering**

Multiply by filter $H(u,v)$:

$$S(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot Z(u,v) = H(u,v) F_I(u,v) + H(u,v) F_R(u,v)$$

**Step 5 — Inverse Fourier Transform**

$$s(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{S(u,v)\}$$

**Step 6 — Exponential Reconstruction**

$$g(x,y) = e^{s(x,y)}$$

**Final Closed-Form Expression:**

$$\boxed{\,g(x,y) = \exp\!\left[ \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left\{ H(u,v) \cdot \mathcal{F}\!\left[ \ln f(x,y) \right] \right\} \right]\,}$$

**Role of Each Step:**

| Step | Role |
|------|------|
| $\ln(\cdot)$ | Convert multiplication to addition |
| $\mathcal{F}$ | Move to frequency domain for spectral separation |
| $H(u,v)$ | Suppress low-freq (illumination), boost high-freq (reflectance) |
| $\mathcal{F}^{-1}$ | Return to spatial domain |
| $\exp(\cdot)$ | Reconstruct enhanced image |

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Gaussian Homomorphic Filter Design:**

$$H(u,v) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L)\left[ 1 - e^{-c \cdot D^{2}(u,v) \,/\, D_{0}^{2}} \right] + \gamma_L$$

where $D(u,v) = \sqrt{(u - M/2)^{2} + (v - N/2)^{2}}$.

**Parameter Selection for Chest X-Ray (justified):**

| Parameter | Value | Justification |
|-----------|-------|---------------|
| $\gamma_L$ | **0.4** | Attenuates low-freq illumination variation (X-ray source falloff, scatter) |
| $\gamma_H$ | **2.5** | Boosts high-freq anatomical edges (ribs, lung markings, lesions) |
| $D_0$ | **30** (in $M \times N$ image) | Splits illumination band from reflectance band |
| $c$ | **1.0** | Moderate Gaussian roll-off — smooth, no ringing artifacts |

**Filter Behavior:**

- At low frequencies ($D \to 0$): $H \to \gamma_L = 0.4$ → illumination is **compressed** (50%–60% reduction in brightness variation).
- At high frequencies ($D \to \infty$): $H \to \gamma_H = 2.5$ → anatomical edges are **amplified 2.5×**, increasing contrast and visibility of fine structures such as pulmonary nodules and rib fractures.

**Result:** The output X-ray exhibits a uniform background with sharply defined anatomical features, improving diagnostic confidence.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative to Question A)*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare the Gaussian and Butterworth forms of the homomorphic filter. Derive the expression for the Butterworth homomorphic filter and explain the role of order $n$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A noisy MRI brain image is corrupted by a multiplicative bias field. Explain how homomorphic filtering can be used to correct this. Show the frequency-domain interpretation of the bias field. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Gaussian Filter | Butterworth Filter |
|--------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Roll-off | Very smooth, monotonic | Depends on order $n$ |
| Sharpness control | Via $c$ | Via order $n$ |
| Ripple / Ringing | None | Mild (zero ripple for $n=1$) |
| Computational cost | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Medical preference | X-ray, mammography | MRI, CT (sharper edges desired) |

**Butterworth Homomorphic Filter Derivation:**

Starting from a standard Butterworth high-pass filter, the homomorphic form is:

$$H_{\text{BHP}}(u,v) = \frac{1}{1 + \left[ \dfrac{D_0}{D(u,v)} \right]^{2n}}$$

To convert this into a homomorphic form with controllable low/high gains, we apply the transformation:

$$H(u,v) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \cdot H_{\text{BHP}}(u,v) + \gamma_L$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\,H(u,v) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L)\left[ \frac{1}{1 + \left[\dfrac{D_0}{D(u,v)}\right]^{2n}} \right] + \gamma_L\,}$$

**Role of Order $n$:**

- $n = 1$: Gentle roll-off — smoothest transition, no ringing.
- $n = 2$ to $4$: Progressively sharper transition, resembles ideal high-pass filter.
- $n \to \infty$: Approaches an ideal high-pass filter but introduces severe ringing artifacts.

**In medical imaging**, $n = 2$ is the standard choice for MRI, balancing edge sharpness with artifact suppression.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Multiplicative Bias Field in MRI:**

A bias field is a low-frequency intensity variation caused by **RF coil inhomogeneity**, **patient-induced eddy currents**, and **static field ($B_0$) imperfections**. The observed MRI image can be modeled as:

$$f_{\text{MRI}}(x,y) = b(x,y) \cdot u(x,y)$$

where $b(x,y)$ is the **bias field** (smooth, multiplicative, low-frequency) and $u(x,y)$ is the **true underlying tissue signal** (high-frequency edges + mid-frequency texture).

**Frequency-Domain Interpretation:**

The Fourier Transform of the bias field $b(x,y)$ is **concentrated near the origin** (low frequencies), exactly analogous to the illumination component $i(x,y)$ in the standard homomorphic model. Hence, the standard homomorphic pipeline applies directly:

1. $\ln f_{\text{MRI}}(x,y) = \ln b(x,y) + \ln u(x,y)$.
2. FFT → low-freq $F_B(u,v)$ + high-freq $F_U(u,v)$.
3. Apply $H(u,v)$ with $\gamma_L \ll 1$ and $\gamma_H \approx 1$.
4. IFFT and exponentiate → corrected image $g_{\text{MRI}}(x,y)$.

**Clinical Impact:** After homomorphic correction, gray-white matter contrast in brain MRI is restored to its true anatomical values, enabling accurate volumetric analysis, tumor segmentation, and longitudinal follow-up studies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Exponential Step**: Many students write $g(x,y) = s(x,y)$ and forget the final $\exp(\cdot)$ — this loses **2 marks** in 14-mark derivations.
> 2. **Swapping $\gamma_L$ and $\gamma_H$**: The convention is $\gamma_L < 1 < \gamma_H$. Writing it backwards leads to an image that is brighter but more blurred — the opposite of enhancement.
> 3. **Confusing Homomorphic Filter with Simple Histogram Equalization**: They are different. Histogram equalization is spatial-domain and global; homomorphic filtering is frequency-domain and exploits the illumination–reflectance split.
> 4. **Not Justifying the Multiplicative Model**: Always state *why* the model is multiplicative (e.g., X-ray attenuation obeys Beer–Lambert law, MRI bias is multiplicative).
> 5. **Skipping the Distance Function $D(u,v)$**: When writing $H(u,v)$ expressions, the explicit form of $D(u,v)$ must be shown for full credit.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Homomorphic Filter**

- ⭐ **Core Model**: $f(x,y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$ — illumination (low-freq) × reflectance (high-freq).
- ⭐ **Pipeline**: $\ln \rightarrow \mathcal{F} \rightarrow H(u,v) \rightarrow \mathcal{F}^{-1} \rightarrow \exp$.
- ⭐ **Master Equation**: $g(x,y) = \exp\!\left[ \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left\{ H(u,v) \cdot \mathcal{F}\!\left[ \ln f(x,y) \right] \right\} \right]$.
- ⭐ **Filter Forms**: Gaussian (smooth, no ringing) and Butterworth (controllable sharpness via $n$).
- ⭐ **Parameter Rule**: $\gamma_L < 1 < \gamma_H$ — always.
- ⭐ **Cutoff $D_0$**: Should lie in the spectrum's transition band, typically **10–50** for $256 \times 256$ medical images.
- ⭐ **Sharpness $c$ (Gaussian) / Order $n$ (Butterworth)**: Controls transition steepness — higher = sharper but riskier.
- ⭐ **Clinical Applications**: X-ray contrast, MRI bias correction, mammography, CT artifact reduction, ultrasound speckle.
- ⭐ **Key Distinction**: Unlike simple contrast stretching, homomorphic filtering **separately** processes illumination and reflectance.
- ⭐ **Log Property Used**: $\ln(a \cdot b) = \ln a + \ln b$ — the foundation of homomorphic decomposition.
- ⭐ **Frequency-Domain Filtering Linear**: Only after log-transform, never directly on $f(x,y)$.
- ⭐ **Distance Formula**: $D(u,v) = \sqrt{(u - M/2)^{2} + (v - N/2)^{2}}$ — always centered, not from corner.
- ⭐ **Always Reconstruct**: End every derivation with $g(x,y) = e^{s(x,y)}$.
- ⭐ **Common Mistake**: Writing $H(u,v)$ without specifying $\gamma_L$, $\gamma_H$ — examiners deduct marks for unspecified parameters.
- ⭐ **Last-Line Clinical Note**: End with a sentence on diagnostic/clinical value to secure the final mark in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT602 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inverse Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse Filtering** is a classical, non-blind image restoration technique in the spatial frequency domain that attempts to recover an original (undegraded) image $f(x,y)$ from a degraded observation $g(x,y)$ by inverting the known degradation function (point spread function / optical transfer function) $H(u,v)$ under the assumption of a linear, shift-invariant (LSI) degradation model with additive noise.

Mathematically, the inverse filter is expressed in the frequency domain as:

$$F(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

where $G(u,v)$, $H(u,v)$, and $F(u,v)$ are the 2-D Discrete Fourier Transforms (DFT) of the degraded image, the degradation function, and the original image respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping (Module 4 - Image Restoration):** This topic is a direct sub-unit under "Image Restoration in the Frequency Domain." The expected cognitive level is **Apply / Analyze (Bloom Level 3–4)** — students must derive, plot, and critically discuss the noise-amplification failure mode of direct inverse filtering.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Blurred Audio Tape" Recovery:**

Imagine a music producer who has a *blurry, hissy* old audio recording of a 1980s concert. The blur was caused by a known low-pass characteristic of the old recording microphone (say, the microphone's frequency response is $H(f)$), and the hiss is environmental noise ($\eta$).

**Inverse filtering is analogous to the producer applying a software equalizer that does the *exact opposite* of the microphone's known frequency response** — boosting the highs because the mic cut the highs, boosting the deep lows because the mic softened the lows — to reconstruct what the concert *originally* sounded like.

**The catch (and the reason it usually fails):** The old tape also has tape hiss (noise). When the engineer boosts the extreme highs (where the mic was weakest, i.e., where $H(f)$ is near **zero**), the hiss gets amplified *much more* than the music, turning a soft hiss into deafening static. This is the **noise amplification pathology** of inverse filtering, and it is the single most important concept KTU examiners test.

**Engineering Analogy — Medical Imaging:** In CT (Computed Tomography) and MRI scanners, the point spread function (PSF) of the imaging system blurs fine anatomical details. Inverse filtering would theoretically "de-blur" the image, but the quantum noise in low-dose X-ray detectors becomes catastrophically amplified, producing a checkerboard noise pattern. This is why clinical scanners use **Wiener filtering** or **constrained iterative reconstruction** instead.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard image dimensions | $M \times N$ | $512 \times 512$ (CT), $256 \times 256$ (MRI) | DFT size |
| Sampling rate (medical) | $\Delta x, \Delta y$ | $0.5 \text{ mm}$ to $1.0 \text{ mm}$ | Nyquist criterion |
| Gaussian PSF standard deviation | $\sigma$ | $1\text{–}5$ pixels | Defines blur radius |
| Additive noise variance | $\sigma_\eta^2$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-2}$ (normalized) | Noise floor |
| PSF energy normalization | $\sum H = 1$ | Always **1** | Energy conservation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The degradation function $H(u,v)$ in the frequency domain is known as the **Optical Transfer Function (OTF)**, and its magnitude $|H(u,v)|$ is called the **Modulation Transfer Function (MTF)**. KTU questions frequently test whether students can draw a typical MTF curve (Gaussian or rectangular) and identify the "zero crossing" or "band-limit radius" where inverse filtering becomes unstable.

---

## 1.4 KTU Course Outcome Alignment

| CO ID | Course Outcome Statement | Topic Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **CO4** | Apply image restoration techniques in the frequency domain to medical images | Direct — inverse filter derivation & analysis |
| **CO5** | Analyze the limitations of direct restoration methods | Noise amplification pathology |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Image Degradation Model (Foundation)

Every KTU derivation of inverse filtering begins from the canonical **2-D linear, shift-invariant (LSI) degradation model with additive noise**:

$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

where:
- $g(x,y)$ = observed/degraded image (what the scanner outputs)
- $f(x,y)$ = original, undegraded image (ground truth)
- $h(x,y)$ = spatial-domain degradation function (PSF) — the *blur kernel*
- $\eta(x,y)$ = additive noise (sensor noise, quantization noise, electronic noise)
- $\ast$ = 2-D convolution operator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why convolution?** Because an LSI imaging system responds to a point source by producing the PSF, and by linearity, the response to a complex image is the convolution of the image with the PSF. This is the *shift-invariance principle* fundamental to all of medical image restoration.

---

## 2.2 Frequency-Domain Transformation (Convolution Theorem)

Applying the **2-D Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** to both sides, and invoking the **Convolution Theorem** (convolution in space ↔ multiplication in frequency):

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

where uppercase letters denote the DFT of the corresponding lowercase spatial functions, and $N(u,v)$ is the DFT of the noise.

---

## 2.3 The Inverse Filter — Definition

Rearranging the frequency-domain degradation equation to *solve* for the unknown original spectrum $F(u,v)$:

$$F(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v) - N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

If we (naively) **ignore noise** by setting $N(u,v) \approx 0$:

$$\boxed{\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}}$$

This is the **ideal inverse filter**. In words: *the restored image spectrum equals the degraded image spectrum divided by the degradation function spectrum.*

---

## 2.4 Why Inverse Filtering Fails — The Noise Amplification Problem

> [!WARNING]
> **THE MOST CRITICAL CONCEPT IN THIS MODULE:** Direct inverse filtering is mathematically correct in the **zero-noise** case but is *catastrophically unstable* in the presence of even infinitesimal noise. KTU examiners **always** test this.

Substituting the true degradation model into the inverse filter:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v)}{H(u,v)} = F(u,v) + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

The **restoration error** in the frequency domain is:

$$E(u,v) = \hat{F}(u,v) - F(u,v) = \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Analysis of the error term:**

- For low spatial frequencies (near the origin, $u,v \approx 0$): $|H(u,v)|$ is typically large (most imaging systems pass DC and low frequencies well) → the noise term $N(u,v)/H(u,v)$ is **bounded**.
- For high spatial frequencies (toward the corners of the DFT plane, $|u|,|v| \to M/2$): $|H(u,v)|$ is **small or zero** (MTF roll-off) → the noise term $N(u,v)/H(u,v)$ **explodes to infinity**.

**Physical interpretation:** High spatial frequencies correspond to *fine details, edges, and textures* in the image. The degradation function attenuates these frequencies (which is exactly why the image looks blurred). The inverse filter must therefore *amplify* them back — but it also amplifies the noise that lives in those same frequency bands, producing a **grainy, speckled, often unusable reconstruction**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~80% of all KTU exam questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ | Spatial degradation model | Restoration problem statement |
| 2 | $G(u,v) = H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v)$ | Frequency-domain degradation | Derivation starting point |
| 3 | $\hat{F}(u,v) = \dfrac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ | **Ideal inverse filter** | Zero-noise case |
| 4 | $\hat{F}(u,v) = \dfrac{G(u,v) - N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ | Noise-aware inverse filter | If $N(u,v)$ is known |
| 5 | $E(u,v) = \dfrac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ | Restoration error spectrum | Failure analysis |
| 6 | $\|e(x,y)\|^2 = \sum \sum \vert E(u,v) \vert^2$ | Total squared error (Parseval) | Error metric |
| 7 | $\text{MTF}(u,v) = \vert H(u,v) \vert$ | Modulation Transfer Function | Plotting $H$ roll-off |
| 8 | $H(u,v) = e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{u}{2\pi \sigma_u}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{v}{2\pi \sigma_v}\right)^2\right]}$ | Gaussian OTF (motion / defocus blur) | Specific PSF modeling |
| 9 | $H(u,v) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi u a)}{\pi u a} \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\pi v b)}{\pi v b}$ | Sinc OTF (rectangular aperture) | Camera/lens defocus |
| 10 | $d_{\text{cutoff}}$ where $\vert H(u,v) \vert < \epsilon$ | Band-limit radius | Choosing cutoff for pseudo-inverse |

---

## 2.6 Pseudo-Inverse Filter (Stabilized Variant)

To address the noise amplification at high frequencies, the **pseudo-inverse filter** imposes a hard cutoff at a radius $R$ beyond which $H(u,v)$ is treated as zero:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} & \text{if } \sqrt{u^2 + v^2} \leq R \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** The cutoff $R$ must be chosen smaller than the noise-dominant radius but larger than the signal-dominant radius. A small $R$ removes noise but *also removes fine image details* (over-smoothing). A large $R$ preserves details but amplifies noise. KTU Part B questions often ask students to **justify the choice of $R$** based on the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the imaging system.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

- **Astronomy:** Restoring atmospheric-turbulence-blurred telescope images (Hubble Space Telescope early days used Wiener filters, not pure inverse, for this reason).
- **Medical imaging:** Pre-processing step in CT/MRI reconstruction pipelines.
- **Forensics:** Restoring license plates from surveillance footage.
- **Microscopy:** Deconvolution of fluorescence microscope images (3-D inverse filtering of confocal stacks).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Spatial Model to Inverse Filter (Full Chain)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the canonical 8-step derivation that KTU examiners expect in a 7-mark Part B sub-question.**

**Step 1 — State the spatial-domain degradation model.**

The imaging system is assumed Linear and Shift-Invariant (LSI). The observed image is the convolution of the original with the PSF, plus additive noise:

$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

*Logic:* Any LSI system is fully characterized by its impulse response (here, the PSF $h(x,y)$). Convolution is the LSI system operator.

**Step 2 — Take the 2-D DFT of both sides.**

$$\mathcal{F}\{g(x,y)\} = \mathcal{F}\{h(x,y) \ast f(x,y)\} + \mathcal{F}\{\eta(x,y)\}$$

*Logic:* DFT is a linear operator, so it distributes over addition.

**Step 3 — Apply the 2-D Convolution Theorem.**

The DFT of a convolution equals the *pointwise product* of the individual DFTs:

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

*Logic:* Convolution Theorem — $\mathcal{F}\{a \ast b\} = A \cdot B$.

**Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate the original spectrum $F(u,v)$.**

$$H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v) = G(u,v) - N(u,v)$$

$$F(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v) - N(u,v)}{H(u,v)} \quad \text{provided } H(u,v) \neq 0$$

*Logic:* Algebraic isolation. The condition $H(u,v) \neq 0$ is the *invertibility requirement*.

**Step 5 — Define the ideal inverse filter operator.**

If noise is negligible ($N(u,v) \to 0$) or unknown, the **ideal inverse filter** is:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

*Logic:* Direct division is the *mathematical inverse* of the multiplicative degradation.

**Step 6 — Compute the restored image via inverse DFT.**

$$\hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\left\{\frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}\right\}$$

*Logic:* We have a spectrum; to view the image we transform back to space.

**Step 7 — Analyze the noise error term.**

Substituting the *true* $G(u,v) = H F + N$ into the inverse filter:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v)}{H(u,v)} = F(u,v) + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

*Logic:* The noise contribution propagates through the division and is amplified wherever $|H|$ is small.

**Step 8 — State the failure condition.**

$$\lim_{H(u,v) \to 0} \left|\frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}\right| = \infty$$

*Logic:* As the denominator tends to zero, the quotient diverges. The result is a **noise-dominated reconstruction** at high spatial frequencies. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gaussian PSF & Its Frequency Behavior (Numerical Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **This 6-step worked example is the most common Part B question (7 marks).**

**Step 1 — Define the Gaussian PSF in the spatial domain.**

A common model for out-of-focus blur or atmospheric turbulence:

$$h(x,y) = \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma^2} \exp\left(-\frac{x^2 + y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

*Logic:* The leading constant normalizes the PSF so that $\sum_x \sum_y h(x,y) = 1$ (energy conservation).

**Step 2 — Take the 2-D DFT to obtain the Gaussian OTF.**

The Fourier transform of a Gaussian is another Gaussian:

$$H(u,v) = \exp\left(-2\pi^2 \sigma^2 (u^2 + v^2)\right)$$

*Logic:* Self-Fourier property of the Gaussian function. The standard deviation *inverts* and squares under the Fourier transform.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the magnitude (MTF) at specific frequencies.**

$$|H(u,v)| = \exp\left(-2\pi^2 \sigma^2 (u^2 + v^2)\right)$$

*Logic:* The Gaussian is purely real and positive, so the magnitude equals the function itself.

**Step 4 — Identify the half-power radius.**

Setting $|H(u,v)|^2 = \frac{1}{2}$ (half-power / -3 dB point):

$$\exp\left(-4\pi^2 \sigma^2 (u^2 + v^2)\right) = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$4\pi^2 \sigma^2 (u^2 + v^2) = \ln 2$$

$$R_{-3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma}\sqrt{\ln 2} \approx \frac{0.1325}{\sigma}$$

*Logic:* Standard decibel convention. This radius defines the *effective bandwidth* of the imaging system.

**Step 5 — Compute the inverse filter.**

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = G(u,v) \cdot \exp\left(+2\pi^2 \sigma^2 (u^2 + v^2)\right)$$

*Logic:* The inverse filter is $\frac{1}{H(u,v)}$, which for a Gaussian becomes an *exponential growth function*.

**Step 6 — Identify the noise-amplification bandwidth.**

For frequencies $\sqrt{u^2 + v^2} > R_{\text{noise}}$ where the signal $|H \cdot F|$ drops below $|N|$, the noise term dominates. For Gaussian PSF with $\sigma = 3$ pixels, the exponential boost reaches $\exp(+1778) \approx 10^{772}$ at the DFT corner $(u,v) = (256,256)$ — an astronomically large value that immediately causes floating-point overflow in any practical implementation. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Restoration Methods

| Property | Ideal Inverse Filter | Pseudo-Inverse | Wiener Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Formula | $F = G/H$ | $F = G/H$ for $\rho \leq R$, else $0$ | $F = \frac{H^* G}{\vert H \vert^2 + K}$ |
| Noise handling | None — *fails* | Hard cutoff | Statistical (min MSE) |
| Requires noise PSD | No | No | Yes (or constant $K$) |
| High-frequency detail | Maximally restored | Truncated | Smoothly tapered |
| Computational cost | $O(MN \log MN)$ | $O(MN \log MN)$ | $O(MN \log MN)$ |
| KTU exam weight | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Inverse Filtering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Image f of x,y] --> B[Convolution with PSF h]
    C[Additive Noise eta] --> D[Noise Injection]
    B --> E[Degraded Image g]
    D --> E
    E --> F[2D DFT to Frequency Domain]
    F --> G[Frequency Domain G of u,v]
    G --> H{Divide by H of u,v}
    H --> I[Restored Spectrum F hat of u,v]
    I --> J[2D Inverse DFT]
    J --> K[Restored Image f hat of x,y]
    L[Noise Amplification at High Frequencies] --> M[Catastrophic Failure]
    H --> L
    M --> K
```

*Logic of the diagram:* This block diagram traces the data flow of the inverse filtering operation. Note the critical feedback arrow from "Noise Amplification at High Frequencies" to "Catastrophic Failure" — this is the central limitation of the method.

---

## 4.2 Frequency-Domain Behavior Schematic (MTF vs. Inverse Filter Gain)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LOW_FREQ[Low Spatial Frequencies]
        A1[Signal: STRONG] --> A2[Noise: WEAK]
        A2 --> A3[Inverse Gain: SMALL]
    end
    subgraph MID_FREQ[Mid Spatial Frequencies]
        B1[Signal: MODERATE] --> B2[Noise: MODERATE]
        B2 --> B3[Inverse Gain: MEDIUM]
    end
    subgraph HIGH_FREQ[High Spatial Frequencies]
        C1[Signal: WEAK] --> C2[Noise: DOMINANT]
        C2 --> C3[Inverse Gain: MASSIVE]
    end
    LOW_FREQ --> MID_FREQ
    MID_FREQ --> HIGH_FREQ
```

*Logic of the diagram:* The graph illustrates the relative dominance of signal versus noise as spatial frequency increases. At high frequencies, the *inverse filter gain explodes* (it is the reciprocal of the small MTF value), amplifying the noise catastrophically.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — When to Use Inverse Filtering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Restoration Task] --> Q1{Is the PSF known?}
    Q1 -- No --> Stop1[Use Blind Deconvolution]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is noise negligible?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use Ideal Inverse Filter]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Is noise PSD known?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Use Wiener Filter]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6[Use Pseudo-Inverse with cutoff R]
    Q5 --> End[Restored Image]
    Q6 --> End
    Q3 --> End
    Stop1 --> End
```

*Logic of the diagram:* KTU examiners frequently ask students to *justify* the choice of restoration method. This flowchart provides a defensible decision framework: inverse filtering is *only* appropriate in the vanishingly rare zero-noise case.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Define inverse filtering. State its main limitation."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Inverse filtering is a frequency-domain image restoration technique that recovers the original image spectrum $F(u,v)$ from the degraded image spectrum $G(u,v)$ by dividing by the known degradation function $H(u,v)$:
> $$F(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$
>
> **Main Limitation (1 mark):** It catastrophically amplifies additive noise at high spatial frequencies, where $|H(u,v)|$ is small or zero, because the noise term $N(u,v)/H(u,v)$ grows without bound.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"What is the Modulation Transfer Function (MTF) and how does it relate to inverse filtering?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The MTF is the magnitude of the Optical Transfer Function: $\text{MTF}(u,v) = |H(u,v)|$. It describes how the imaging system attenuates each spatial frequency (2 marks). Inverse filtering applies a *reciprocal gain* $1/\text{MTF}(u,v)$ to recover the original frequencies — but this reciprocal grows unboundedly at high frequencies, causing noise amplification (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each, with internal choice. Each part (a) and (b) is typically 7 marks. Solutions must show *every* intermediate step to receive full marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** Derive the frequency-domain expression for the ideal inverse filter starting from the spatial degradation model. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumption 1 (1 mark):** The imaging system is *Linear and Shift-Invariant* (LSI).

**Assumption 2 (1 mark):** Noise is *additive* and statistically independent of the signal.

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Spatial-domain degradation model:**
$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — 2-D DFT of both sides:**
$$G(u,v) = H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Rearrange to solve for $F(u,v)$:**
$$F(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v) - N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Ideal inverse filter (ignore noise):**
$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Inverse DFT to obtain restored image:**
$$\hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\left\{\frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}\right\}$$

---

**Q. (b)** With a Gaussian degradation function $H(u,v) = \exp(-2\pi^2 \sigma^2 (u^2+v^2))$ with $\sigma = 2$ pixels, compute the inverse filter gain at spatial frequency $(u,v) = (50, 50)$ for a $256 \times 256$ image. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Write the inverse filter formula:**
$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} = G(u,v) \cdot \exp\left(+2\pi^2 \sigma^2 (u^2+v^2)\right)$$

*Logic:* Taking the reciprocal of an exponential flips the sign of the exponent.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Substitute the given values:**
- $\sigma = 2$ pixels
- $u^2 + v^2 = 50^2 + 50^2 = 2500 + 2500 = 5000$

$$\text{Exponent} = 2\pi^2 \cdot (2)^2 \cdot 5000 = 2 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 4 \cdot 5000 = 394,784$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute the gain:**

$$\text{Gain} = \exp(394{,}784) \approx 10^{171{,}433}$$

*Logic:* This is a hyper-astronomical number — far beyond the dynamic range of any standard floating-point representation (IEEE 754 double-precision maxes out near $10^{308}$).

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Comment on the result:**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often stop at "the gain is large" without specifying the engineering consequence. Full marks require stating that **the gain exceeds the maximum representable floating-point value, causing numerical overflow, complete loss of meaningful image data, and a noise-dominated reconstruction**. This is the *practical demonstration* of why direct inverse filtering is unusable in the presence of noise.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**Q. (a)** Explain the noise amplification problem in inverse filtering. Derive the restoration error spectrum and show graphically (using a sketch description) how it depends on the magnitude of $H(u,v)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Substitute the true degradation model into the inverse filter:**

Starting from $G(u,v) = H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v)$ and applying the inverse filter:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} = \frac{H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Simplify:**

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = F(u,v) + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Define the error spectrum:**

$$E(u,v) = \hat{F}(u,v) - F(u,v) = \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Step 4 (3 marks) — Analyze the dependence on $|H|$:**

- **When $|H(u,v)|$ is large** (low frequencies, near origin): the divisor is large, so the error $|E| = |N|/|H|$ is **small** → restoration is accurate.
- **When $|H(u,v)|$ is small** (high frequencies, near DFT corners): the divisor is tiny, so the error **explodes** → noise dominates.
- **When $|H(u,v)| = 0$** (at or beyond the band-limit): the error is **mathematically infinite** (division by zero).

*Sketch description (verbal, since KTU paper does not support graphics):* Plot $|H(u,v)|$ as a decreasing curve (Gaussian or sinc-shaped) from a peak at the origin to near-zero at the edges. Overlay the error $|N/H|$ as an *inverse* curve that *shoots up to infinity* at the high-frequency edge.

---

**Q. (b)** What is a pseudo-inverse filter? How does it address the noise amplification limitation, and what is its main trade-off? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Definition:**

The pseudo-inverse filter imposes a *radial cutoff* $R$ in the frequency domain:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} & \text{if } D(u,v) = \sqrt{u^2+v^2} \leq R \\ 0 & \text{if } D(u,v) > R \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — How it addresses noise amplification:**

By setting $\hat{F}(u,v) = 0$ for frequencies beyond $R$, the pseudo-inverse filter *completely avoids* dividing by the small (or zero) values of $H(u,v)$ in the high-frequency region, thereby *suppressing* the noise-amplification pathology.

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Main trade-off:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The trade-off is between noise suppression and detail preservation.** A small cutoff $R$ aggressively removes noise but also discards legitimate high-frequency image details (edges, fine textures) — producing an *over-smoothed* image. A large $R$ preserves details but allows noise amplification. The optimal $R$ is chosen to lie at the *crossover frequency* where the signal spectrum $|H(u,v) F(u,v)|$ falls below the noise spectrum $|N(u,v)|$.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the noise term** in the derivation (loses 2–3 marks). Always write $G = HF + N$ first, *then* simplify.
> 2. **Writing $\hat{F} = G/H$ without stating the assumption** $N \approx 0$ (loses 1 mark). The assumption must be explicit.
> 3. **Confusing OTF and MTF.** OTF is complex (has phase); MTF is its magnitude. Inverse filter inverts the *complex* OTF, not just the MTF.
> 4. **Skipping the inverse DFT step.** Restoration is a spectrum until you apply $\mathcal{F}^{-1}$ — examiners expect to see this.
> 5. **Not commenting on noise amplification.** This is worth 3–4 marks by itself in most Part B questions. A derivation without the failure analysis is incomplete.
> 6. **Confusing inverse filtering with Wiener filtering.** Wiener filter has an additional $\vert H \vert^2 + K$ term in the denominator — they are *not* the same.
> 7. **Using the wrong normalization for the Gaussian PSF** (missing the $1/(2\pi\sigma^2)$ prefactor) — costs 1 mark in numerical questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Degradation model** (spatial): $g = h \ast f + \eta$
- [ ] **Degradation model** (frequency): $G = HF + N$ (Convolution Theorem)
- [ ] **Ideal inverse filter**: $\hat{F} = G/H$ (assumes $N=0$)
- [ ] **Restoration error**: $E = N/H$ — this is the *only* source of imperfection
- [ ] **Failure condition**: when $|H| \to 0$ (high frequencies), $E \to \infty$
- [ ] **OTF** = $H(u,v)$ (complex); **MTF** = $|H(u,v)|$ (real, non-negative)
- [ ] **Gaussian OTF**: $H = e^{-2\pi^2 \sigma^2 (u^2+v^2)}$ → decays to 0 at high frequencies
- [ ] **Sinc OTF** (rectangular aperture): $H = \text{sinc}(ua)\text{sinc}(vb)$ → has *zero crossings*
- [ ] **Pseudo-inverse**: hard cutoff at radius $R$ to avoid division by tiny $H$
- [ ] **Wiener filter** (superior): $\hat{F} = \frac{H^* G}{|H|^2 + K}$ where $K = $ noise-to-signal power ratio
- [ ] **Energy conservation**: $\sum \sum h(x,y) = 1$ for any normalized PSF
- [ ] **KTU-favorite question pattern**: "Derive and state the limitation" — always both parts!
- [ ] **Inverse DFT is mandatory**: $\hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{\hat{F}(u,v)\}$
- [ ] **The noise term is your enemy**: every formula must be examined for what happens as $H \to 0$
- [ ] **Domain of validity**: inverse filtering works *only* in the noise-free, known-PSF regime — a *theoretical* tool, rarely used in practice without modification

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT602 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wiener Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wiener Filtering** is a classical linear, *optimum mean-square-error (MSE) restoration filter* designed by Norbert Wiener in 1942 for the purpose of recovering an underlying (undegraded) image $f(x,y)$ from a noisy and blurred observation $g(x,y)$. In the context of medical image processing, it is one of the most widely taught and used techniques in KTU Module 4 because it simultaneously addresses two unavoidable degradations that occur during image acquisition: **blurring** (caused by the imaging system's point spread function, motion, or out-of-focus optics) and **additive random noise** (caused by sensors, X-ray quantum mottle, MR thermal noise, electronic circuitry, etc.).

In the **frequency domain**, the celebrated Wiener filter transfer function is:

$$
\hat{F}(u,v) = \left[ \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)/S_{ff}(u,v)} \right] G(u,v)
$$

where:
- $G(u,v)$ = Discrete Fourier Transform of the observed degraded image $g(x,y)$
- $H(u,v)$ = Degradation (blurring) transfer function in frequency domain
- $H^*(u,v)$ = Complex conjugate of $H(u,v)$
- $\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 = H^*(u,v) H(u,v)$
- $S_{ff}(u,v)$ = Power spectral density of the original (uncorrupted) image
- $S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)$ = Power spectral density of the additive noise
- $\hat{F}(u,v)$ = Estimated DFT of the restored image

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Wiener filter is the *optimum* linear filter under the Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE) criterion when the image and noise are modeled as **stationary random processes** with known second-order statistics (power spectra). It is the standard answer expected in Board questions comparing inverse filtering vs. Wiener filtering.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a radiologist reading an old, slightly damaged chest X-ray film. The film has two problems: (1) the patient's hand **moved** during exposure, causing motion blur (this is the deterministic degradation $h(x,y)$), and (2) the film has grainy speckles from chemical processing noise (this is $\eta(x,y)$).

A simple **inverse filter** would try to "un-smear" the blur by dividing the Fourier transform of the image by the blur function. But the moment you divide by very small values in $H(u,v)$ (which happen at high frequencies), the noise gets amplified to absurd levels — the result looks like television static, not a medical image.

The **Wiener filter** acts like a **judicious accountant** or a **smart audio engineer** running an equalizer on a noisy music recording. Instead of blindly boosting all frequencies equally, the engineer asks: *"How much of the signal do I expect at this frequency, and how much noise?"* If a frequency band has lots of noise and little signal, the filter **suppresses that band**. If a band has lots of signal and little noise, the filter **restores it** aggressively. The ratio $S_{\eta\eta}/S_{ff}$ acts as a **smart threshold** that prevents noise amplification while still undoing as much blur as possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy Summary:** Inverse filter = "turn every knob to maximum" (destroys the image). Wiener filter = "turn each knob to the optimum position based on signal-to-noise ratio at that frequency" (recovers a clean, usable image). This is exactly the behavior expected by the KTU 2024 examiner.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Table (Quick Reference)

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning in Medical Imaging |
|---|---|---|
| Original image | $f(x,y)$ | The "true" anatomy we want to recover (ideal CT slice, MR slice) |
| Degraded image | $g(x,y)$ | The actual image as captured by the scanner |
| Point Spread Function | $h(x,y)$ | System blur (e.g., X-ray focal spot, MR k-space truncation) |
| Degradation operator | $H(u,v)$ | Fourier transform of the PSF — describes which frequencies are attenuated |
| Additive noise | $\eta(x,y)$ | Random fluctuations (thermal, quantum, electronic) |
| Power spectrum of image | $S_{ff}(u,v)$ | $\vert F(u,v) \vert^2$, expected signal energy at each frequency |
| Power spectrum of noise | $S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)$ | $\vert N(u,v) \vert^2$, expected noise energy at each frequency |
| Estimated image | $\hat{f}(x,y)$ | The restored image after Wiener filtering |
| Noise-to-signal ratio | $K$ | Constant approximation used when spectra are unknown |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard exam metric:** The expected value operator $\mathbb{E}\{\cdot\}$ is heavily used. Always write $\mathbb{E}\{\,\cdot\,\}$ in symbolic answers — examiners reward clean notation.

---

## 1.4 The Degradation-Restoration Paradigm (Foundation Block)

Every Wiener filtering problem in KTU Module 4 begins with the **degradation model**:

$$
g(x,y) = h(x,y) * f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)
$$

where $*$ denotes 2-D spatial convolution. In the frequency domain, convolution becomes multiplication:

$$
G(u,v) = H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v)
$$

The **goal of restoration** is to obtain an estimate $\hat{F}(u,v)$ (and hence $\hat{f}(x,y)$ via inverse DFT) such that it is as close as possible to the original $F(u,v)$ under some optimality criterion. Wiener chose the **mean square error**:

$$
e^2 = \mathbb{E}\{(f(x,y) - \hat{f}(x,y))^2\}
$$

Minimizing $e^2$ leads directly to the famous Wiener filter formula. This derivation is shown exhaustively in SECTION 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of the Wiener Filter

The Wiener filter derivation is built on **four foundational assumptions** that examiners love to test. A student who lists these correctly earns immediate partial credit.

1. **The image and noise are zero-mean stationary random processes.** This means their statistical properties (mean, variance) do not change with spatial position.
2. **The image and noise are uncorrelated** with each other, i.e. $\mathbb{E}\{f(x,y)\,\eta(m,n)\} = 0$ for all $(x,y)$ and $(m,n)$.
3. **The degradation function $H(u,v)$ is linear and spatially invariant (LSI).** This permits the use of simple multiplication in the frequency domain rather than complex matrix operations.
4. **The power spectral densities $S_{ff}(u,v)$ and $S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)$ are known** (or can be estimated from sample data).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these assumptions matter in medical imaging:** X-ray quantum noise and MR thermal noise are well-modeled as zero-mean, uncorrelated, stationary random processes. Anatomical structures, although not truly stationary, can be approximated as locally stationary in small regions — which is why Wiener filtering works *reasonably well* in practice despite imperfect modeling.

---

## 2.2 The Two Canonical Forms of the Wiener Filter

### 2.2.1 Spectral Form (Most General)

$$
\hat{F}(u,v) = \left[ \frac{1}{H(u,v)} \cdot \frac{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + \dfrac{S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)}{S_{ff}(u,v)}} \right] G(u,v)
$$

### 2.2.2 Conjugate-Symmetric Form (Frequently Quoted)

$$
\hat{F}(u,v) = \left[ \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + \dfrac{S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)}{S_{ff}(u,v)}} \right] G(u,v)
$$

### 2.2.3 Constant-Ratio Form (Practical / Simplified)

When the power spectra are unknown or too complex to estimate, the ratio $S_{\eta\eta}(u,v) / S_{ff}(u,v)$ is approximated as a constant $K$:

$$
\hat{F}(u,v) = \left[ \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + K} \right] G(u,v)
$$

This is the form most commonly used in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for KTU exams on this topic.** Memorize the structure, not just the symbols.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Domain | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Degradation Model (Spatial) | $g(x,y) = h(x,y) * f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ | Spatial | Convolution of image with PSF plus noise |
| 2 | Degradation Model (Frequency) | $G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$ | Frequency | Convolution becomes multiplication |
| 3 | Inverse Filter | $\hat{F}(u,v) = G(u,v)/H(u,v)$ | Frequency | Fails badly when $H \to 0$ |
| 4 | Wiener Filter (General) | $\hat{F}(u,v) = \dfrac{H^*(u,v)G(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + S_{\eta\eta}/S_{ff}}$ | Frequency | The optimal MSE filter |
| 5 | Wiener Filter (Constant $K$) | $\hat{F}(u,v) = \dfrac{H^*(u,v)G(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + K}$ | Frequency | Practical form, $K \approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| 6 | MSE Objective | $e^2 = \mathbb{E}\{(f-\hat{f})^2\}$ | Spatial | What we minimize |
| 7 | Power Spectrum of Image | $S_{ff}(u,v) = \vert F(u,v) \vert^2$ | Frequency | Energy distribution of image |
| 8 | Power Spectrum of Noise | $S_{\eta\eta}(u,v) = \vert N(u,v) \vert^2$ | Frequency | Energy distribution of noise |
| 9 | Limit: Zero Noise | $K = 0 \Rightarrow \hat{F} = G/H$ | Frequency | Reduces to inverse filter |
| 10 | Limit: High Noise | $K \to \infty \Rightarrow \hat{F} \to 0$ | Frequency | Filter suppresses everything |

---

## 2.4 Frequency-Domain Behavior — A Step-by-Step Intuition

The Wiener filter acts as a **frequency-adaptive amplifier**. Its gain at each frequency $(u,v)$ is:

$$
W(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + \dfrac{S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)}{S_{ff}(u,v)}}
$$

- **At low frequencies** (where $\vert H \vert$ is large and $S_{ff} \gg S_{\eta\eta}$), the gain is approximately $1/H$ → behaves like inverse filter → strong restoration.
- **At high frequencies** (where $\vert H \vert$ is small and $S_{\eta\eta}$ dominates), the gain is approximately $H^* S_{ff}/S_{\eta\eta}$ → near zero → noise is suppressed.
- **At mid frequencies**, there is a smooth trade-off controlled by the noise-to-signal ratio.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In CT and MR imaging, the high-frequency components of the image contain edge information and fine texture (e.g., lung markings, microcalcifications). The Wiener filter *preserves* the meaningful high-frequency content while *suppressing* the random noise — making it invaluable for low-dose CT and fast MR acquisitions.

---

## 2.5 Practical Real-World Utility in Medical Imaging

1. **Low-Dose CT Reconstruction:** Reduces streak artifacts and noise while preserving lesion edges.
2. **MR Image Denoising:** Removes thermal noise in 3T/7T MR scans without blurring anatomical boundaries.
3. **Ultrasound Speckle Reduction:** Adapts the constant $K$ to suppress speckle while keeping tissue boundaries sharp.
4. **X-Ray Mammography Enhancement:** Used to improve the visibility of microcalcifications.
5. **Fundus Retinal Imaging:** Reduces sensor noise for early detection of diabetic retinopathy.
6. **PET/SPECT Denoising:** Suppresses Poisson noise in nuclear medicine images.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Wiener Filter in the Frequency Domain

This is the **central derivation** of KTU Module 4. Every step below is exam-worthy. We derive the filter by minimizing the mean square error $e^2 = \mathbb{E}\{(f(x,y) - \hat{f}(x,y))^2\}$.

### Step 1 — State the Restoration Goal

We want to find a linear filter $W(u,v)$ such that the restored image is:

$$
\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v) \cdot G(u,v)
$$

> *Why linear?* Because linear systems are analytically tractable, commutative, and well-defined by their frequency response. Nonlinear estimators exist (e.g., median, BM3D) but are not the focus of the Wiener filter.

### Step 2 — Express the Error in the Frequency Domain

Substitute $G = HF + N$:

$$
\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v) \bigl[ H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v) \bigr]
$$

Therefore, the error between the true image and the estimate is:

$$
E(u,v) = F(u,v) - \hat{F}(u,v) = F(u,v) - W(u,v) H(u,v) F(u,v) - W(u,v) N(u,v)
$$

Grouping the $F$ and $N$ terms:

$$
E(u,v) = \bigl[ 1 - W(u,v) H(u,v) \bigr] F(u,v) - W(u,v) N(u,v)
$$

### Step 3 — Write the MSE Objective Function

The mean square error is the expected value of the squared magnitude of the error in the frequency domain (Parseval-like relation):

$$
e^2 = \mathbb{E}\{\vert E(u,v) \vert^2\}
$$

Substituting the expression for $E$:

$$
e^2 = \mathbb{E}\bigl\{\bigl\vert [1 - W H] F - W N \bigr\vert^2\bigr\}
$$

### Step 4 — Expand the Squared Magnitude

$$
e^2 = \mathbb{E}\Bigl\{ \bigl([1 - W H] F - W N\bigr) \bigl([1 - W H]^* F^* - W^* N^*\bigr) \Bigr\}
$$

Expanding (using $(A - B)(A^* - B^*) = AA^* - AB^* - BA^* + BB^*$):

$$
e^2 = \mathbb{E}\bigl\{ (1-WH)(1-WH)^* FF^* - (1-WH) F\, W^* N^* - W N\, (1-WH)^* F^* + W W^* N N^* \bigr\}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Uncorrelatedness Assumption

Since $F$ and $N$ are **uncorrelated zero-mean random variables**, all cross-terms vanish:

$$
\mathbb{E}\{F N^*\} = \mathbb{E}\{F^* N\} = 0
$$

So the cross-product terms drop out completely:

$$
e^2 = \mathbb{E}\bigl\{ \vert 1 - W H \vert^2 \vert F \vert^2 + \vert W \vert^2 \vert N \vert^2 \bigr\}
$$

### Step 6 — Introduce Power Spectral Densities

By definition, $S_{ff}(u,v) = \mathbb{E}\{\vert F(u,v) \vert^2\}$ and $S_{\eta\eta}(u,v) = \mathbb{E}\{\vert N(u,v) \vert^2\}$. Substitute:

$$
e^2 = \vert 1 - W(u,v) H(u,v) \vert^2 \, S_{ff}(u,v) + \vert W(u,v) \vert^2 \, S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)
$$

### Step 7 — Minimize $e^2$ with Respect to $W$

Take the partial derivative of $e^2$ with respect to $W^*$ (treating $W$ and $W^*$ as independent variables — a standard technique in complex optimization):

$$
\frac{\partial e^2}{\partial W^*} = -H(u,v)\bigl[1 - W(u,v) H(u,v)\bigr] S_{ff}(u,v) + W(u,v) S_{\eta\eta}(u,v) = 0
$$

### Step 8 — Solve the Linear Equation for $W$

Rearrange the terms:

$$
-H(u,v) S_{ff}(u,v) + W(u,v) \vert H(u,v) \vert^2 S_{ff}(u,v) + W(u,v) S_{\eta\eta}(u,v) = 0
$$

Collect the $W$ terms:

$$
W(u,v) \bigl[ \vert H(u,v) \vert^2 S_{ff}(u,v) + S_{\eta\eta}(u,v) \bigr] = H(u,v) S_{ff}(u,v)
$$

Therefore:

$$
W(u,v) = \frac{H(u,v) S_{ff}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 S_{ff}(u,v) + S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)}
$$

### Step 9 — Convert to the Standard Form

Multiply numerator and denominator by $H^*(u,v) / S_{ff}(u,v)$:

$$
W(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + \dfrac{S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)}{S_{ff}(u,v)}}
$$

### Step 10 — Final Wiener Filter Output

The restored image spectrum is:

$$
\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v)\,G(u,v) = \left[ \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + \dfrac{S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)}{S_{ff}(u,v)}} \right] G(u,v)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Q.E.D.** — This is the Wiener filter formula as it appears in the KTU 2024 syllabus. Always write the **derivation steps (1) → (10)** in Board answers for full marks.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A medical image is degraded by a blurring function $H(u,v) = e^{-0.002(u^2 + v^2)}$ and corrupted by additive noise. The noise power spectrum is $S_{\eta\eta}(u,v) = 0.01$ and the image power spectrum is $S_{ff}(u,v) = 1.0$ (assumed constant). Compute the Wiener filter gain at the frequency point $(u,v) = (10, 10)$.

**Solution:**

*Step 1:* Compute the squared magnitude of $H$ at $(10, 10)$:

$$
H(10,10) = e^{-0.002(10^2 + 10^2)} = e^{-0.002 \cdot 200} = e^{-0.4}
$$

Numerically: $e^{-0.4} \approx 0.6703$

So:
$$
\vert H(10,10) \vert^2 = (0.6703)^2 \approx 0.4493
$$

*Step 2:* Compute the noise-to-signal ratio:

$$
\frac{S_{\eta\eta}}{S_{ff}} = \frac{0.01}{1.0} = 0.01
$$

*Step 3:* Compute the denominator of the Wiener filter:

$$
\vert H \vert^2 + \frac{S_{\eta\eta}}{S_{ff}} = 0.4493 + 0.01 = 0.4593
$$

*Step 4:* Since $H$ is real, $H^* = H = 0.6703$, so the filter gain is:

$$
W(10,10) = \frac{0.6703}{0.4593} \approx 1.459
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A gain greater than 1 means the filter is *amplifying* this frequency band. This is because the noise is low relative to the image signal at this frequency. The filter is being aggressive about restoring the blur.

**Contrast check at a high frequency $(u,v) = (50, 50)$:**

$$
H(50,50) = e^{-0.002(2500 + 2500)} = e^{-10} \approx 4.54 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\vert H \vert^2 \approx 2.06 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
W(50,50) = \frac{4.54 \times 10^{-5}}{2.06 \times 10^{-9} + 0.01} \approx \frac{4.54 \times 10^{-5}}{0.01} = 4.54 \times 10^{-3}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Critical insight:** The gain drops from 1.459 at low frequency to 0.00454 at high frequency — a 300× reduction! This is exactly how the Wiener filter suppresses noise amplification at high frequencies where $H$ is small. This numerical demonstration is *highly valued* in KTU valuation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Wiener vs. Inverse vs. Other Filters

| Property | Inverse Filter | Wiener Filter | Constrained LS | Geometric Mean |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Optimality criterion | None (direct division) | Minimum MSE | Minimum $\vert\vert \hat{f} \vert\vert^2$ | Generalized |
| Handles noise | **No (amplifies)** | **Yes (suppresses)** | Yes | Yes |
| Requires noise model | No | Yes ($S_{\eta\eta}$) | Yes | Yes |
| Requires image model | No | Yes ($S_{ff}$) | No | No |
| Computational cost | Lowest | Low | High (iterative) | High |
| KTU Module relevance | Core comparison topic | **Primary topic** | Brief mention | None |

---

## 3.4 Extension: Relation to the Deconvolution Filter

When the noise is negligible ($S_{\eta\eta}/S_{ff} \to 0$):

$$
\hat{F}(u,v) \to \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2}\,G(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}
$$

This is precisely the **inverse filter**, confirming that the Wiener filter generalizes the inverse filter to handle noise. This limiting case is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU exams.
