# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 3 marks]**

**Define image segmentation. What is the difference between discontinuity-based and similarity-based segmentation? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

Image segmentation is the process of partitioning a digital image into multiple non-overlapping homogeneous regions such that pixels within a region share similar properties (intensity, texture) and pixels across regions differ significantly.

**Discontinuity-based segmentation** partitions the image by detecting abrupt changes in gray level. The main technique is **edge detection** (e.g., Sobel, Canny, Prewitt operators). Example: detecting the boundary of a tumor in a CT scan.

**Similarity-based segmentation** groups pixels that satisfy a predefined homogeneity criterion. Examples include **thresholding** (global, local), **region growing**, **region splitting and merging**, and **clustering**. Example: extracting a bone structure from a radiograph by global thresholding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition (1 mark), Discontinuity-based explanation + example (1 mark), Similarity-based explanation + example (1 mark).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 3 marks]**

**What are the assumptions required for basic global thresholding to work effectively? When does it fail?**

**Model Answer:**

The basic global thresholding algorithm assumes the following:

1. **Bimodal histogram:** The image has two distinct intensity clusters — one for the object and one for the background — separated by a clear valley.
2. **Uniform illumination:** The lighting across the image is consistent with no significant shading or gradient.
3. **High object-background contrast:** The two classes are visually distinct in intensity.
4. **Single dominant object class:** Only one object class is of interest (binary segmentation).

**Failure cases:**
- **Non-uniform illumination** (e.g., MRI with bias field) causes the same object pixel to have different intensities in different regions.
- **Multimodal histograms** (multiple object classes) cannot be separated by a single threshold.
- **Low contrast images** (e.g., some ultrasound scans) where the histogram is unimodal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** List of assumptions (1.5 marks), Failure cases (1.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Iterative Global Thresholding

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of basic global thresholding. Discuss the role of the image histogram in selecting the threshold. Why is the valley between two peaks chosen as the threshold location? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the iterative algorithm for global threshold selection (Ridler–Calvard algorithm) with suitable mathematical expressions. Apply the algorithm to the following $4 \times 4$ image and find the optimal threshold: **(7 marks)**

| 8 | 8 | 9 | 10 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | 7 | 200 | 201 |
| 9 | 8 | 199 | 200 |
| 10 | 9 | 200 | 201 |

Take initial threshold $T^{(0)} = 70$ and $\varepsilon = 0.5$.

#### Model Solution for (a):

Image thresholding converts a grayscale image into a binary image using a single threshold $T$. The operation is:

$$g(x,y) = \begin{cases} 1 & f(x,y) \geq T \\ 0 & f(x,y) < T \end{cases}$$

**Role of Histogram:**  
The histogram $h(r_k)$ plots the frequency of each intensity level $r_k$ in the image. It reveals the **multi-modal structure** of the intensity distribution. For a bimodal histogram (two distinct peaks), one peak corresponds to the background and the other to the object. The histogram serves as a visual guide to locate the optimal threshold.

**Why the Valley?**  
- Pixels at the valley have the **lowest probability** of belonging to either pure class.
- Placing $T$ at the valley **minimizes the misclassification probability**.
- A small change in $T$ around the valley produces minimal change in segmentation, making the result **stable**.

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Image f x,y] --> B[Histogram h r k]
    B --> C[Identify Two Peaks: Background and Object]
    C --> D[Locate Valley between Peaks]
    D --> E[Set T at Valley Minimum]
    E --> F[Threshold: f x,y greater than or equal to T]
    F --> G[Output: Binary Image g x,y]
```

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Initial threshold:** $T^{(0)} = 70$

**Image sum:**

$$S = 8+8+9+10+8+7+200+201+9+8+199+200+10+9+200+201 = 1287$$

**Step 2 — Segment with $T^{(0)} = 70$:**  
- $G_1 = \{200, 201, 199, 200, 200, 201\}$ → $N_1 = 6$  
- $G_2 = \{8, 8, 9, 10, 8, 7, 9, 8, 10, 9\}$ → $N_2 = 10$

**Step 3 — Compute means:**

$$\mu_1^{(0)} = \frac{200+201+199+200+200+201}{6} = \frac{1201}{6} = 200.1667$$

$$\mu_2^{(0)} = \frac{8+8+9+10+8+7+9+8+10+9}{10} = \frac{86}{10} = 8.6$$

**Step 4 — Update threshold:**

$$T^{(1)} = \frac{200.1667 + 8.6}{2} = \frac{208.7667}{2} = 104.3833$$

**Step 5 — Convergence check:**

$$\vert T^{(1)} - T^{(0)} \vert = \vert 104.38 - 70 \vert = 34.38 > 0.5 \quad \text{Not converged}$$

**Iteration 2:**  
Since all object pixels ($> 70$) still exceed $T^{(1)} = 104.38$, the same groups $G_1$ and $G_2$ result. The means are unchanged:

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = 200.1667, \quad \mu_2^{(1)} = 8.6$$

$$T^{(2)} = \frac{200.1667 + 8.6}{2} = 104.3833$$

**Step 6 — Convergence:**

$$\vert T^{(2)} - T^{(1)} \vert = 0 < 0.5 \quad \checkmark \text{ Converged}$$

**Final threshold:** $T^* \approx 104.38$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update the group means** in each iteration (the same $G_1$ and $G_2$ will yield the same means, which is correct here, but students often make arithmetic errors).
> 2. **Not specifying the convergence tolerance** — KTU expects explicit mention of $\varepsilon$.
> 3. **Mixing up the threshold update formula** — the new $T$ is the **average of the two means**, not the mean of the threshold.
> 4. **Not showing the binary output image** after applying $T^*$.
> 5. **Rounding errors** — keep at least 2 decimal places for threshold values.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Otsu's Method

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Explain Otsu's method for automatic global thresholding. Derive the expression for the between-class variance $\sigma_B^2(T)$ and explain why maximizing it gives the optimal threshold. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The normalized histogram of a 4-gray-level image is given as $p(0) = 0.1$, $p(1) = 0.4$, $p(2) = 0.3$, $p(3) = 0.2$. Apply Otsu's method to find the optimal threshold and compute the separability measure $\eta$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Otsu's method (1979) automatically determines the optimal global threshold by exhaustively searching for the value $T$ that **maximizes the between-class variance** $\sigma_B^2(T)$ of the two classes $C_0$ and $C_1$ created by thresholding.

**Derivation:**

Let $p(k)$ be the normalized histogram. For a candidate threshold $T$:

$$w_0(T) = \sum_{k=0}^{T} p(k), \quad w_1(T) = \sum_{k=T+1}^{L-1} p(k) = 1 - w_0(T)$$

$$\mu_0(T) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{T} k \cdot p(k)}{w_0(T)}, \quad \mu_1(T) = \frac{\sum_{k=T+1}^{L-1} k \cdot p(k)}{w_1(T)}$$

The global mean is:

$$\mu_T = \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} k \cdot p(k)$$

The **between-class variance** is defined as:

$$\sigma_B^2(T) = w_0(T) \cdot w_1(T) \cdot \left[\mu_0(T) - \mu_1(T)\right]^2$$

**Why maximize $\sigma_B^2$?**  
- A larger between-class variance implies the two class means $\mu_0$ and $\mu_1$ are **far apart** relative to their weights.
- Equivalently, it **minimizes the within-class variance** $\sigma_W^2$, because $\sigma_T^2 = \sigma_B^2 + \sigma_W^2$ and $\sigma_T^2$ is constant.
- This gives the **best class separability** in the Fisher discriminant sense.

**Optimal threshold:**

$$T^* = \arg\max_{T \in \{0, 1, \ldots, L-1\}} \sigma_B^2(T)$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Global mean:**

$$\mu_T = 0(0.1) + 1(0.4) + 2(0.3) + 3(0.2) = 0 + 0.4 + 0.6 + 0.6 = 1.6$$

**Global variance:**

$$\sigma_T^2 = (0-1.6)^2(0.1) + (1-1.6)^2(0.4) + (2-1.6)^2(0.3) + (3-1.6)^2(0.2) = 0.256 + 0.144 + 0.048 + 0.392 = 0.84$$

**Test all thresholds:**

**$T = 0$:**  
$w_0 = 0.1, \quad w_1 = 0.9$  
$\mu_0 = 0/0.1 = 0, \quad \mu_1 = (0.4+0.6+0.6)/0.9 = 1.6/0.9 = 1.7778$

$$\sigma_B^2(0) = 0.1 \cdot 0.9 \cdot (0 - 1.7778)^2 = 0.09 \cdot 3.1605 = 0.2844$$

**$T = 1$:**  
$w_0 = 0.5, \quad w_1 = 0.5$  
$\mu_0 = (0+0.4)/0.5 = 0.8, \quad \mu_1 = (0.6+0.6)/0.5 = 2.4$

$$\sigma_B^2(1) = 0.5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (0.8 - 2.4)^2 = 0.25 \cdot 2.56 = \mathbf{0.6400} \leftarrow \text{max}$$

**$T = 2$:**  
$w_0 = 0.8, \quad w_1 = 0.2$  
$\mu_0 = (0+0.4+0.6)/0.8 = 1.0, \quad \mu_1 = 0.6/0.2 = 3.0$

$$\sigma_B^2(2) = 0.8 \cdot 0.2 \cdot (1.0 - 3.0)^2 = 0.16 \cdot 4.0 = 0.6400$$

**$T = 3$:**  
$w_1 = 0 \Rightarrow \sigma_B^2(3) = 0$ (invalid)

**Decision:** Both $T = 1$ and $T = 2$ give $\sigma_B^2 = 0.64$. By convention, the **lower threshold** is chosen: $T^* = 1$.

**Separability measure:**

$$\eta = \frac{\sigma_B^2(T^*)}{\sigma_T^2} = \frac{0.64}{0.84} = 0.7619$$

A high $\eta$ value (close to 1) confirms that the two classes are well-separated by this threshold.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the global mean and global variance** — required for the separability measure $\eta$.
> 2. **Not testing ALL thresholds** including edge cases ($T = 0$ and $T = L-1$).
> 3. **Tie-breaking:** When two thresholds yield the same $\sigma_B^2$, KTU expects the student to **state the convention** (e.g., choose the lower one).
> 4. **Confusing within-class variance $\sigma_W^2$ with between-class variance $\sigma_B^2$** — the formula and interpretation are different.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Basic Global Thresholding**

- **Definition:** A single threshold $T$ is applied to the entire image to produce a binary segmentation.
- **Core equation:** $g(x,y) = 1$ if $f(x,y) \geq T$, else $0$.
- **Underlying assumption:** Bimodal histogram (two distinct intensity modes).
- **Iterative algorithm (Ridler-Calvard):**
  - Initialize $T^{(0)} = $ mean intensity.
  - Split into $G_1$ (object) and $G_2$ (background).
  - Compute $\mu_1, \mu_2$.
  - Update: $T^{(k+1)} = (\mu_1 + \mu_2)/2$.
  - Iterate until $\vert T^{(k+1)} - T^{(k)} \vert < \varepsilon$.
- **Otsu's Method:**
  - Exhaustively search for $T$ that maximizes $\sigma_B^2(T) = w_0 w_1 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2$.
  - Fully automatic, no initial guess required.
  - Provides a **separability measure** $\eta = \sigma_B^2 / \sigma_T^2 \in [0, 1]$.
- **KTU High-Yield Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Mean: $\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum f(x,y)$
  - Threshold update: $T^{(k+1)} = \frac{\mu_1 + \mu_2}{2}$
  - Between-class variance: $\sigma_B^2 = w_0 w_1 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2$
- **Limitations:**
  - Fails on non-uniform illumination.
  - Fails on multimodal histograms (multiple object types).
  - Sensitive to noise.
- **When to use:** Ideal first-step segmentation for medical images with clear object-background contrast (CT bone, stained microscopy, radiographs).
- **Fails when:** Image has shading, low contrast, or multiple overlapping classes.
- **Exam tips:**
  - Always show histogram analysis.
  - For Otsu, compute $\sigma_B^2$ for **all** $L$ candidate thresholds.
  - For iterative method, perform **at least 2 iterations** and demonstrate convergence.
  - State the **convergence criterion** explicitly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variable Thresholding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT602 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Variable Thresholding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variable Thresholding** (also known as **Adaptive Thresholding** or **Local Thresholding**) is a non-linear binarization technique in which the threshold value **T(x, y)** is **not a single global constant**, but rather a **spatially varying function** computed dynamically from the **local neighborhood statistics** (e.g., local mean, local variance, local gradient) of each pixel in the image.

Mathematically, a general variable thresholding operation is expressed as:

$$
g(x, y) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } f(x, y) > T(x, y) \\ 0, & \text{if } f(x, y) \leq T(x, y) \end{cases}
$$

where the threshold function is given by:

$$
T(x, y) = \mathcal{F}\big( m_L(x,y),\ \sigma_L(x,y),\ \nabla f(x,y),\ \dots \big)
$$

Here, $m_L(x,y)$ and $\sigma_L(x,y)$ are the **local mean** and **local standard deviation** computed over a sliding window $W$ of size $w \times w$ centered at $(x, y)$.

### Key Distinction from Global Thresholding

| Feature | Global Thresholding | Variable Thresholding |
|---|---|---|
| Threshold value | Single constant **T** for entire image | Spatially varying $T(x, y)$ |
| Computational cost | Low | Higher (window-based statistics) |
| Robustness to illumination | Poor (fails with uneven lighting) | Excellent |
| Suitability for medical images | Limited to uniform images | High (handles inhomogeneity) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Reading Newspaper in a Dim Room" Analogy**

Imagine trying to read a newspaper where one half of the page is brightly lit (sunlight falling directly on it) and the other half is in deep shadow.

- **Global Thresholding** is like deciding a single "darkness level" for the **entire page**. The shadowed side becomes a black blob (you cannot read it), while the bright side has crisp text. The system fails on half the page.
- **Variable Thresholding** is like having a **smart magnifying glass** that automatically adjusts its "darkness sensitivity" based on the brightness of the small area you are currently looking at. In the bright area, it demands darker ink; in the shadowed area, it accepts lighter smudges as text. This is exactly what medical images need because MRI/CT scans often have **intensity inhomogeneity** (bias field artifacts) where the same tissue appears with different gray levels in different regions of the image.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

The following standard parameters and their typical values are used in KTU reference materials:

- **Window size $w$**: Typically an odd integer such as **$3 \times 3$, $5 \times 5$, $7 \times 7$, $15 \times 15$**, or **$31 \times 31$**. Must be **odd** to maintain a clear center pixel.
- **Bias parameter $k$**: A dimensionless tuning constant, typically in the range **$-0.2 \leq k \leq 0.5$**, used in Niblack-family methods.
- **Dynamic range constant $R$**: Typically set to **$R = 128$** for 8-bit grayscale images (Sauvola's method).
- **Local standard deviation $\sigma_L$**: Measured in **gray-level units (GLU)**, with valid range **$[0, 255]$** for 8-bit images.
- **Local mean $m_L$**: Measured in **gray-level units (GLU)**, with valid range **$[0, 255]$**.

## 1.4 Why Variable Thresholding is Critical for Medical Imaging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Medical images (MRI, CT, Ultrasound, X-ray) inherently suffer from:
> 1. **Intensity Inhomogeneity (Bias Field)**: Slow spatial variation of intensity due to RF coil non-uniformity in MRI.
> 2. **Tissue-Specific Intensity Drift**: Soft tissue, bone, and fluid have different baseline intensities.
> 3. **Noise Distributions**: Rician noise in MRI, Poisson noise in CT, Speckle noise in Ultrasound.
>
> Variable thresholding **locally adapts** to these conditions, making it the **de facto standard** for lesion segmentation, vessel extraction, and cell nuclei detection in histopathology.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The variable thresholding pipeline operates on the following logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Local Neighborhood Definition**: For every pixel $f(x, y)$, define a square sliding window $W(x, y)$ of size $w \times w$ centered at $(x, y)$.
- **Step 2 — Local Statistics Computation**: Compute the **local mean** $m_L(x,y)$ and **local standard deviation** $\sigma_L(x,y)$ over the window.
- **Step 3 — Threshold Function Formulation**: Construct $T(x, y)$ as a function of $m_L(x,y)$ and $\sigma_L(x,y)$, often with a tuning parameter $k$.
- **Step 4 — Pixel-wise Comparison**: Compare $f(x, y)$ against $T(x, y)$ to assign binary output $g(x, y) \in \{0, 1\}$.
- **Step 5 — Boundary Handling**: At image edges, the window is incomplete — use **zero-padding**, **mirroring**, or **replication** strategies.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Major Method

### 2.2.1 Chow and Kaneko Method (Variance-Based Region Approach)

- **Why**: Global thresholding fails when the histogram is multi-modal or skewed. By dividing the image into sub-regions and computing a threshold for each, the method adapts to local intensity distributions.
- **How**: The image is divided into $n \times n$ overlapping sub-images. For each sub-image, a histogram is computed and an optimal threshold is derived (e.g., using Otsu's method). The final threshold at each pixel is obtained by **bilinear interpolation** of the sub-image thresholds.

### 2.2.2 Niblack's Method

- **Why**: To capture local contrast in documents and medical images where text/structures appear on varying backgrounds.
- **How**: Threshold is set slightly above the local mean, scaled by the local standard deviation.

### 2.2.3 Bernsen's Method

- **Why**: Works without assuming any specific noise model — uses only local extrema.
- **How**: Threshold is the midpoint of the local maximum and local minimum intensities.

### 2.2.4 Sauvola's Method (Improved Niblack)

- **Why**: Niblack's method tends to produce noise in "white" background regions. Sauvola normalizes the standard deviation by the dynamic range $R$.
- **How**: Multiplicative form — assumes documents have high contrast near dark pixels.

### 2.2.5 Mean and Standard Deviation Based Method (Wellner's)

- **Why**: Faster than Niblack; uses a single running average rather than per-pixel window statistics.
- **How**: Computes the average of a moving strip of pixels, then compares each pixel to this average.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **all key formulas** required for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations on Variable Thresholding. Memorize the formula form and the role of each parameter.

| Method | Threshold Formula $T(x, y)$ | Key Parameters | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Local Mean** | $T(x, y) = m_L(x, y) - C$ | Window size $w$, constant $C$ | Simple adaptive binarization |
| **Local Median** | $T(x, y) = \text{median}_{W}(f)$ | Window size $w$ | Salt-and-pepper noise robustness |
| **Niblack** | $T(x, y) = m_L(x, y) + k \cdot \sigma_L(x, y)$ | $k \in [-0.2, 0.5]$ | Document/lesion segmentation |
| **Sauvola** | $T(x, y) = m_L(x, y) \cdot \left[ 1 + k \cdot \left( \dfrac{\sigma_L(x, y)}{R} - 1 \right) \right]$ | $k \in [0.2, 0.5]$, $R = 128$ | Document binarization |
| **Bernsen** | $T(x, y) = \dfrac{1}{2}\big( \max_{W} f + \min_{W} f \big)$ | Contrast threshold $C_{thr}$ | Histology images |
| **Chow-Kaneko** | $T_{ij}$ from Otsu per sub-image, interpolated | Sub-image size, overlap | Multi-modal histograms |
| **Wellner (Mean+Std)** | $T(x, y) = m_{\text{strip}} - C$ | Strip length $S$, $C$ | Real-time binarization |

### 2.3.1 Core Statistical Definitions (Recap of Module 1 Foundations)

The **local mean** over a window $W$ of size $N = w \times w$ centered at $(x, y)$ is:

$$
m_L(x, y) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{(i, j) \in W} f(i, j)
$$

The **local variance** and **local standard deviation** are:

$$
\sigma_L^2(x, y) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{(i, j) \in W} \big( f(i, j) - m_L(x, y) \big)^2
$$

$$
\sigma_L(x, y) = \sqrt{\sigma_L^2(x, y)}
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Variable thresholding is the **operational backbone** of:
> - **Lesion segmentation** in brain MRI (tumor boundary detection in non-uniform fields).
> - **Vessel segmentation** in retinal fundus images and coronary CT angiography.
> - **Nuclei segmentation** in digital histopathology (H&E stained slides).
> - **Cell counting** in Pap smear cytology images.
> - **Adaptive Optical Character Recognition (OCR)** for medical record digitization.
> - **Pre-processing** for 3D printing of patient-specific anatomical models.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Global to Variable Thresholding

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: The Limitation of Otsu's Global Method

Otsu's method finds a single threshold $T^*$ that maximizes the **between-class variance**:

$$
\sigma_B^2(T) = \omega_0(T)\,\omega_1(T)\,\big[\mu_0(T) - \mu_1(T)\big]^2
$$

where $\omega_0, \omega_1$ are the class probabilities and $\mu_0, \mu_1$ are the class means. This is **optimal** only when the image has a **bimodal histogram**. When the image has a **bias field** (e.g., $f(x, y) = b(x, y) \cdot f_{\text{true}}(x, y) + n(x, y)$), the histogram is no longer cleanly bimodal, and Otsu fails.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Niblack's Threshold from First Principles

**Step 1**: Assume the local intensity distribution within window $W$ can be approximated as Gaussian:

$$
p(f \mid W) \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_L} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(f - m_L)^2}{2\sigma_L^2} \right)
$$

**Step 2**: We want to find a threshold $T(x, y)$ such that the **probability of a foreground pixel** in the local region equals some target $\alpha$ (often 0.5):

$$
P(f > T \mid W) = \alpha
$$

**Step 3**: Using the complementary error function:

$$
\alpha = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left( \frac{T - m_L}{\sigma_L \sqrt{2}} \right)
$$

**Step 4**: Inverting:

$$
T(x, y) = m_L + \sigma_L \sqrt{2}\,\text{erfc}^{-1}(2\alpha)
$$

**Step 5**: Since $\text{erfc}^{-1}(2\alpha)$ is a constant for fixed $\alpha$, we can write $k = \sqrt{2}\,\text{erfc}^{-1}(2\alpha)$, yielding **Niblack's formula**:

$$
\boxed{\,T(x, y) = m_L(x, y) + k \cdot \sigma_L(x, y)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant $k$ is a **direct statistical interpretability knob**: it controls the **false positive rate** in the local Gaussian model. For $k = 0$, the threshold equals the local mean.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (Niblack's Method)

Consider a $5 \times 5$ local window $W$ with the following pixel intensities (centered at $(3, 3)$):

$$
W = \begin{bmatrix} 50 & 52 & 55 & 53 & 51 \\ 48 & 60 & 75 & 58 & 49 \\ 46 & 70 & 120 & 65 & 47 \\ 44 & 55 & 62 & 52 & 45 \\ 42 & 50 & 48 & 46 & 44 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step A — Compute the local mean $m_L$**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_L &= \frac{1}{25}\Big( 50+52+55+53+51 + 48+60+75+58+49 \\
&\quad + 46+70+120+65+47 + 44+55+62+52+45 + 42+50+48+46+44 \Big) \\
&= \frac{1}{25}(1425) = 57.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Compute the local variance $\sigma_L^2$**:

For each pixel, compute $(f - m_L)^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Sum of squared deviations:} \\
&\quad (50-57)^2 = 49,\ (52-57)^2 = 25,\ (55-57)^2 = 4,\ (53-57)^2 = 16,\ (51-57)^2 = 36 \\
&\quad (48-57)^2 = 81,\ (60-57)^2 = 9,\ (75-57)^2 = 324,\ (58-57)^2 = 1,\ (49-57)^2 = 64 \\
&\quad (46-57)^2 = 121,\ (70-57)^2 = 169,\ (120-57)^2 = 3969,\ (65-57)^2 = 64,\ (47-57)^2 = 100 \\
&\quad (44-57)^2 = 169,\ (55-57)^2 = 4,\ (62-57)^2 = 25,\ (52-57)^2 = 25,\ (45-57)^2 = 144 \\
&\quad (42-57)^2 = 225,\ (50-57)^2 = 49,\ (48-57)^2 = 81,\ (46-57)^2 = 121,\ (44-57)^2 = 169 \\
&\sum (f - m_L)^2 = 6045 \\
&\sigma_L^2 = \frac{6045}{25} = 241.8 \\
&\sigma_L = \sqrt{241.8} \approx 15.55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Compute the Niblack threshold with $k = 0.2$**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(3, 3) &= m_L + k \cdot \sigma_L = 57.0 + 0.2 \times 15.55 \\
&= 57.0 + 3.11 = 60.11
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Apply the threshold**: Since $f(3, 3) = 120 > 60.11$, the center pixel is classified as **foreground (1)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note how the bright center pixel (120, which is well above 57) is correctly detected as foreground even though the local mean is dragged up by it — the **standard deviation term** prevents under-thresholding.

## 3.2 Derivation of Sauvola's Correction Term

**Starting Point**: Niblack's threshold:

$$
T_N(x, y) = m_L + k \cdot \sigma_L
$$

**Problem**: In regions where the local standard deviation is small (e.g., uniform background), $\sigma_L \approx 0$, so $T_N \approx m_L$. This causes Niblack to **misclassify noise pixels** in supposedly empty regions as foreground.

**Step 1 — Normalize the standard deviation** by the dynamic range $R$:

$$
\sigma_{\text{norm}} = \frac{\sigma_L}{R}, \quad \text{where } R = 128
$$

**Step 2 — Define the threshold relative to $m_L$** with a multiplicative correction:

$$
T_S(x, y) = m_L(x, y) \cdot \left[ 1 + k \cdot \left( \frac{\sigma_L}{R} - 1 \right) \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Expand and simplify**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_S(x, y) &= m_L + k \cdot m_L \cdot \frac{\sigma_L}{R} - k \cdot m_L \\
&= m_L(1 - k) + k \cdot m_L \cdot \frac{\sigma_L}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify behavior**:
- If $\sigma_L = R$ (high contrast): $T_S = m_L \cdot 1 = m_L$.
- If $\sigma_L = 0$ (flat region): $T_S = m_L(1 - k)$, which is **below** $m_L$, suppressing noise.

$$
\boxed{\,T_S(x, y) = m_L(x, y) \left[ 1 + k \left( \frac{\sigma_L(x, y)}{R} - 1 \right) \right]\,}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Method Selection Guide for KTU Problems

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **single most important KTU exam takeaway** for method selection.

| Image Condition | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Uniform illumination, bimodal histogram | **Otsu (Global)** | Optimal closed-form solution |
| Smooth illumination gradient (bias field) | **Local Mean** or **Niblack** | Compensates for slow intensity drift |
| High local contrast variation | **Sauvola** | Normalizes by dynamic range |
| Heavy salt-and-pepper noise | **Local Median** or **Bernsen** | Median is robust to outliers |
| Document with shadows | **Sauvola** or **Niblack** with $k < 0$ | Pulls threshold down in dark regions |
| Real-time processing required | **Wellner's adaptive** | Single-pass running average |
| Multi-modal histogram, complex scene | **Chow-Kaneko** | Region-wise optimal thresholds |

## 3.4 Derivation of Chow-Kaneko Interpolation

Given an $M \times N$ image divided into $n \times n$ sub-images with overlap $\delta$, each sub-image $S_{pq}$ has an Otsu threshold $T_{pq}^*$ computed independently.

For an arbitrary pixel $(x, y)$ in the overlapping region of four sub-images $S_{pq}, S_{p+1,q}, S_{p,q+1}, S_{p+1,q+1}$ with thresholds $T_{00}, T_{10}, T_{01}, T_{11}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x, y) &= (1 - \alpha)(1 - \beta)\,T_{00} + \alpha(1 - \beta)\,T_{10} \\
&\quad + (1 - \alpha)\beta\,T_{01} + \alpha\beta\,T_{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\alpha, \beta \in [0, 1]$ are the normalized fractional distances of $(x, y)$ from the top-left corner of the overlapping tile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **bilinear interpolation** of the per-sub-image thresholds, producing a smooth, continuous threshold surface $T(x, y)$ across the image.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Variable Thresholding Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **complete functional architecture** of a variable thresholding system, from input image acquisition to the final binary segmentation mask.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Grayscale Image f of x y] --> B[Initialize Sliding Window W of size w x w]
    B --> C[Set Window Center at x y]
    C --> D[Extract Local Neighborhood Pixels]
    D --> E[Compute Local Mean mL]
    D --> F[Compute Local Std Dev sigmaL]
    E --> G[Threshold Function Builder]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Select Method Niblack Sauvola Bernsen]
    H --> I[Compute T x y using Formula]
    I --> J[Compare f x y with T x y]
    J --> K{Is f x y greater than T x y}
    K -->|Yes| L[Assign g x y = 1 Foreground]
    K -->|No| M[Assign g x y = 0 Background]
    L --> N[Store Output Pixel]
    M --> N
    N --> O{Is Window at Last Pixel}
    O -->|No| C
    O -->|Yes| P[Output Binary Image g]
```

## 4.2 Comparison Flowchart: Global vs Variable Thresholding Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Image Ready for Segmentation] --> Q1{Histogram Bimodal and Uniform Illumination}
    Q1 -->|Yes| G1[Apply Otsu Global Thresholding]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is Illumination Slowly Varying}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Are Sharp Local Contrast Edges Important}
    Q2 -->|No| Q4{Is Noise Salt Pepper Dominant}
    Q3 -->|Yes| V1[Apply Niblack Method k = 0.2]
    Q3 -->|No| V2[Apply Local Mean Method]
    Q4 -->|Yes| V3[Apply Bernsen or Local Median]
    Q4 -->|No| V4[Apply Sauvola Method k = 0.3]
    G1 --> Out[Segmented Binary Image]
    V1 --> Out
    V2 --> Out
    V3 --> Out
    V4 --> Out
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Chow-Kaneko Variance-Based Sub-Region Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image M x N] --> B[Divide into n x n Overlapping Sub-images]
    B --> C[For Each Sub-image S pq]
    C --> D[Compute Histogram H pq]
    D --> E[Apply Otsu to get T pq Star]
    E --> F{All Sub-images Processed}
    F -->|No| C
    F -->|Yes| G[Construct Threshold Grid T pq]
    G --> H[Apply Bilinear Interpolation]
    H --> I[Generate Continuous Surface T x y]
    I --> J[Apply T x y to Original Image]
    J --> K[Output Binary Segmentation]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Map: Taxonomy of Thresholding Methods (Module 4 Scope)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Image Thresholding Techniques] --> G[Global Thresholding]
    Root --> V[Variable Thresholding]
    G --> G1[Otsu Method]
    G --> G2[Iterative Selection]
    G --> G3[Histogram Based]
    V --> V1[Local Statistics Based]
    V --> V2[Region Based]
    V1 --> V1a[Niblack]
    V1 --> V1b[Sauvola]
    V1 --> V1c[Bernsen]
    V1 --> V1d[Local Mean Std]
    V2 --> V2a[Chow Kaneko]
    V2 --> V2b[Split Merge Region Growing]
```

## 4.5 Visual Schematic: Local Window Statistics Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph W["5x5 Sliding Window W centered at x y"]
        P1[Pixel f x-2 y-2] --> Sum[Sum All Pixels]
        P2[Pixel f x-1 y-1] --> Sum
        P3[Pixel f x y Center] --> Sum
        P4[Pixel f x+1 y+1] --> Sum
        P5[Pixel f x+2 y+2] --> Sum
    end
    Sum --> ML[Local Mean mL]
    Sum --> S2[Sum of Squared Deviations]
    ML --> S2
    S2 --> VL[Local Variance sigmaL squared]
    VL --> SL[Local Std Dev sigmaL]
    ML --> TF[Threshold Function T x y]
    SL --> TF
    TF --> Out[Adaptive Threshold at Center Pixel]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

> **Q: Differentiate between global and variable (adaptive) thresholding. When would you prefer variable thresholding in medical image processing?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — structured response):**

| Aspect | Global Thresholding | Variable Thresholding |
|---|---|---|
| Threshold | Single constant $T$ for the whole image | Spatially varying $T(x, y)$ |
| Computation | Histogram-based (e.g., Otsu) | Local window statistics |
| Robustness | Poor for uneven illumination | Excellent for bias fields |

**Preferred in medical imaging** (1 mark): Variable thresholding is preferred when the image suffers from **intensity inhomogeneity** (e.g., MRI bias field, CT cupping artifacts) or when **local contrast variation** must be preserved (e.g., small lesion detection, vessel segmentation in fundus images, nuclei segmentation in histopathology). (1 mark for application example)

**Examiner's Note**: Full 3 marks require both the comparison table and a justified medical application.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sessional]

> **Q: Define local mean and local standard deviation in the context of variable thresholding. Why is the choice of window size $w$ critical?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Local Mean** (1 mark): The average intensity of all pixels inside a sliding window $W$ of size $w \times w$ centered at $(x, y)$:

$$
m_L(x, y) = \frac{1}{w^2} \sum_{(i, j) \in W} f(i, j)
$$

**Local Standard Deviation** (1 mark): The square root of the local variance, measuring intensity dispersion within the window:

$$
\sigma_L(x, y) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{w^2} \sum_{(i, j) \in W} (f(i, j) - m_L(x, y))^2}
$$

**Importance of $w$** (1 mark): A small $w$ (e.g., $3 \times 3$) is sensitive to noise and may miss larger structures; a large $w$ (e.g., $31 \times 31$) blurs local details and increases computational cost. The choice balances **noise suppression** against **spatial resolution preservation**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **ONE** of two alternatives. Each long answer carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Niblack method** of variable thresholding in detail. Derive its threshold formula from a Gaussian local intensity model and discuss the role of the parameter $k$.

> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** For a $3 \times 3$ image patch:
> $$F = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 110 & 105 \\ 95 & 120 & 100 \\ 90 & 115 & 98 \end{bmatrix}$$
> Compute the Niblack threshold $T$ at the center pixel using $k = 0.3$. State whether the center pixel is classified as foreground or background.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a) [7 Marks]:

**1. Concept and Motivation (2 marks):**
Niblack's method is a **local statistics-based** variable thresholding technique. It computes a different threshold for every pixel based on the **local mean** and **local standard deviation** within a sliding window. It is particularly effective for images with **uneven illumination** and **varying local contrast**, such as document images and medical images with bias fields.

**2. Gaussian Local Model Assumption (2 marks):**
We assume that within the local window $W$ centered at $(x, y)$, the pixel intensities follow a **Gaussian distribution** with mean $m_L$ and standard deviation $\sigma_L$:

$$
p(f \mid W) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_L} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(f - m_L)^2}{2\sigma_L^2} \right)
$$

We seek a threshold $T(x, y)$ such that the probability of a pixel being foreground is a target value $\alpha$ (typically 0.5 for equal prior). Using the complementary error function:

$$
\alpha = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left( \frac{T - m_L}{\sigma_L \sqrt{2}} \right)
$$

**3. Inversion to Niblack's Formula (2 marks):**
Inverting the above:

$$
T(x, y) = m_L(x, y) + \sigma_L(x, y) \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot \text{erfc}^{-1}(2\alpha)
$$

Defining $k = \sqrt{2}\,\text{erfc}^{-1}(2\alpha)$:

$$
\boxed{\,T(x, y) = m_L(x, y) + k \cdot \sigma_L(x, y)\,}
$$

**4. Role of $k$ (1 mark):**
The parameter $k$ is a **dimensionless bias parameter** controlling the trade-off between **sensitivity** and **specificity**:
- **$k > 0$**: Threshold is *above* the local mean → stricter classification → fewer false positives but may miss faint structures.
- **$k < 0$**: Threshold is *below* the local mean → more permissive → better for dark or low-contrast regions.
- **Typical range**: $k \in [-0.2, 0.5]$; **$k = 0.2$** is most commonly used in document binarization.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b) [7 Marks]:

**Step 1 — Identify the local window and center pixel (1 mark):**
The center pixel is at position $(2, 2)$ with value $f(2, 2) = 120$. The window $W$ is the full $3 \times 3$ matrix $F$ (since the image is itself $3 \times 3$). Thus $N = 9$ pixels.

**Step 2 — Compute the local mean $m_L$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_L &= \frac{1}{9}(100 + 110 + 105 + 95 + 120 + 100 + 90 + 115 + 98) \\
&= \frac{1}{9}(933) \\
&= 103.67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the local variance $\sigma_L^2$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L^2 &= \frac{1}{9}\sum_{i=1}^{9}(f_i - m_L)^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{9}\big[ (100-103.67)^2 + (110-103.67)^2 + (105-103.67)^2 \\
&\quad + (95-103.67)^2 + (120-103.67)^2 + (100-103.67)^2 \\
&\quad + (90-103.67)^2 + (115-103.67)^2 + (98-103.67)^2 \big] \\
&= \frac{1}{9}\big[ 13.47 + 40.07 + 1.77 + 75.17 + 266.67 + 13.47 \\
&\quad + 186.87 + 128.37 + 32.15 \big] \\
&= \frac{1}{9}(758.0) \\
&\approx 84.22
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the local standard deviation $\sigma_L$ (1 mark):**

$$
\sigma_L = \sqrt{84.22} \approx 9.18
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Niblack's formula with $k = 0.3$ (1 mark):**

$$
T(2, 2) = m_L + k \cdot \sigma_L = 103.67 + 0.3 \times 9.18 = 103.67 + 2.75 = 106.42
$$

**Step 6 — Classification (mandatory final statement):**

Since $f(2, 2) = 120 > T(2, 2) = 106.42$, the center pixel is classified as **foreground (object)** in the binary output $g(2, 2) = 1$.

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the **Chow and Kaneko method** of variable thresholding. Explain how the image is divided into sub-regions, how thresholds are computed, and how they are combined using bilinear interpolation.

> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare the **Niblack, Sauvola, and Bernsen** methods of variable thresholding in terms of (i) threshold formula, (ii) noise robustness, (iii) handling of low-contrast regions, and (iv) computational complexity.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a) [7 Marks]:

**1. Motivation and Overview (1 mark):**
The Chow-Kaneko method is a **region-based** variable thresholding approach designed for images where a **single global threshold** fails due to **multi-modal histograms** or **spatial intensity variations**. It applies optimal thresholding (e.g., Otsu's method) to local sub-regions independently and then interpolates.

**2. Image Division into Sub-Regions (2 marks):**
The $M \times N$ input image is divided into $n \times n$ **overlapping sub-images** $S_{pq}$, where $p, q \in \{0, 1, \dots, m-1\}$ index the sub-image grid. The **overlap** $\delta$ (typically $\delta = w/2$, half the sub-image size) ensures smooth transitions between adjacent regions. Each sub-image $S_{pq}$ has dimensions $w \times w$.

**3. Per-Sub-Image Threshold Computation (2 marks):**
For each sub-image $S_{pq}$:
- Compute the **local histogram** $H_{pq}(f)$.
- Apply **Otsu's method** to find the optimal threshold $T_{pq}^*$ that maximizes the between-class variance:

$$
T_{pq}^* = \arg\max_{T} \left[ \omega_0(T)\,\omega_1(T)\,\big(\mu_0(T) - \mu_1(T)\big)^2 \right]
$$

- Store $T_{pq}^*$ in a **threshold grid** of size $m \times m$.

**4. Bilinear Interpolation to Obtain Continuous $T(x, y)$ (2 marks):**
For an arbitrary pixel $(x, y)$ falling within the overlap of four adjacent sub-images with thresholds $T_{00}, T_{10}, T_{01}, T_{11}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x, y) &= (1 - \alpha)(1 - \beta)\,T_{00} + \alpha(1 - \beta)\,T_{10} \\
&\quad + (1 - \alpha)\beta\,T_{01} + \alpha\beta\,T_{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\alpha, \beta \in [0, 1]$ are the normalized distances of $(x, y)$ from the top-left corner of the overlapping tile. This produces a **smooth, continuous threshold surface** $T(x, y)$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b) [7 Marks]:

**Comparison Table (1 mark for table structure, 6 marks for content):**

| Criterion | **Niblack** | **Sauvola** | **Bernsen** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Threshold Formula** | $T = m_L + k\sigma_L$ | $T = m_L[1 + k(\sigma_L/R - 1)]$ | $T = \tfrac{1}{2}(\max_W f + \min_W f)$ |
| **(ii) Noise Robustness** | Moderate — sensitive to outliers in $\sigma_L$ | Better — normalized by $R$ | High — uses local extrema robustly |
| **(iii) Low-Contrast Handling** | Poor — may produce noise in flat regions | Excellent — multiplicative form preserves faint structures | Moderate — contrast threshold $C_{thr}$ required |
| **(iv) Computational Cost** | High (per-pixel mean + std) | High (per-pixel mean + std) | Moderate (only max + min, no std) |
| **Key Parameter** | $k \in [-0.2, 0.5]$ | $k \in [0.2, 0.5]$, $R = 128$ | $C_{thr}$ (contrast threshold) |
| **Best Use Case** | General-purpose adaptive binarization | Document images, OCR | Histology, noisy images |

**Key Distinctions (1 mark):**
- **Niblack** tends to introduce noise in the background (low $\sigma_L$ regions).
- **Sauvola** corrects this by normalizing $\sigma_L$ by $R$, suppressing noise in flat areas.
- **Bernsen** is **distribution-free** (no Gaussian assumption) and uses only local extrema, making it highly robust to outliers but **slower to compute** in large windows (requires max/min scans).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Niblack with Sauvola**: Students often write $T = m_L(1 + k\sigma_L/R)$ for Niblack — this is **WRONG**. Niblack is additive ($+$), Sauvola is multiplicative.
> 2. **Forgetting units/parameters**: Always mention $R = 128$ for Sauvola, $w$ being odd, and the range of $k$.
> 3. **Computational errors in numerical problems**: Forgetting to divide by $N$ (window size) when computing mean/variance — KTU examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for missing normalization.
> 4. **Not stating the final classification**: In numerical problems, the final line "Therefore $f(x,y) > T(x,y)$, so pixel is foreground" **must** be written explicitly to secure the last mark.
> 5. **Confusing "variable thresholding" with "multi-level thresholding"**: Variable = spatial variation of a single threshold; multi-level = multiple thresholds to produce > 2 gray levels.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist for last-minute KTU exam revision.**

### 🔑 Core Concept
- [x] **Variable thresholding** uses a **spatially varying threshold** $T(x, y)$, unlike global thresholding.
- [x] It is **essential for medical images** with bias fields, inhomogeneity, and varying local contrast.

### 🔑 Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- [x] **Local Mean**: $m_L(x, y) = \dfrac{1}{w^2}\displaystyle\sum_{(i,j)\in W} f(i, j)$
- [x] **Local Standard Deviation**: $\sigma_L(x, y) = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{w^2}\displaystyle\sum_{(i,j)\in W} (f(i,j) - m_L(x,y))^2}$
- [x] **Niblack**: $T = m_L + k\sigma_L$
- [x] **Sauvola**: $T = m_L \left[ 1 + k\left(\dfrac{\sigma_L}{R} - 1\right) \right]$, $R = 128$
- [x] **Bernsen**: $T = \tfrac{1}{2}(\max_W f + \min_W f)$
- [x] **Chow-Kaneko**: Otsu per sub-image, then bilinear interpolation

### 🔑 Parameter Ranges
- [x] Window size $w$: **$3, 5, 7, 15, 31$** (always odd)
- [x] Niblack $k$: **$-0.2$ to $0.5$** (typical: $0.2$)
- [x] Sauvola $k$: **$0.2$ to $0.5$** (typical: $0.3$)
- [x] Sauvola $R$: **$128$** for 8-bit images

### 🔑 Method Selection Mnemonic
- [x] **"N-S-B-C"** → **N**iblack, **S**auvola, **B**ernsen, **C**how-Kaneko
- [x] **Niblack** → general purpose; **Sauvola** → documents; **Bernsen** → noisy; **Chow-Kaneko** → multi-modal

### 🔑 Key Advantages over Global Thresholding
- [x] Handles **illumination gradients** (bias fields)
- [x] Preserves **local contrast** for small structures
- [x] Adapts to **tissue-specific intensity ranges** in medical images

### 🔑 Key Disadvantages
- [x] **Computationally expensive** (per-pixel window statistics)
- [x] **Window size selection** is critical and problem-dependent
- [x] **Boundary artifacts** at image edges
- [x] **Niblack** introduces noise in flat regions; **Sauvola** corrects this

### 🔑 Exam Strategy Reminders
- [x] Always **state the formula first**, then substitute numerical values.
- [x] Always **normalize** by $N$ when computing mean/variance.
- [x] Always **end numerical answers** with an explicit classification statement.
- [x] Always **draw comparison tables** for "differentiate" type questions — KTU examiners award full marks for tabular responses.
- [x] **Cite medical applications** in any "discuss" question for context-specific credit.

topics.title: Region Based Segmentation - Region Growing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT602 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Region Based Segmentation - Region Growing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Region Growing** is a *pixel-based, region-oriented* image segmentation technique that partitions an image into homogeneous regions by iteratively aggregating neighbouring pixels whose feature attributes (typically intensity, grey-level, colour, or texture) satisfy a predefined **homogeneity predicate** (also called a *similarity criterion*), starting from one or more manually or automatically selected **seed points**.

Mathematically, given a seed pixel $s \in I$ and a candidate pixel $p \in \mathcal{N}(s)$, the pixel $p$ is merged into the region $R$ if and only if the predicate holds:

$$\mathcal{P}(R, p) = \text{TRUE} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{similarity}(p, R) \geq T$$

where $T$ is the user-defined **tolerance threshold** and $\mathcal{N}(\cdot)$ denotes the spatial neighbourhood of a pixel under either **4-connectivity** ($\mathcal{N}_4$) or **8-connectivity** ($\mathcal{N}_8$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight (Module 4):** Region Growing is grouped under *Region-Based Segmentation* alongside *Thresholding*, *Region Splitting and Merging*, and *Watershed Segmentation*. It is the **canonical introductory example** of bottom-up pixel aggregation in the syllabus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Board-Examiner Ready):**
> *Region Growing is a procedure that groups pixels or sub-regions into larger regions based on a predefined growth criterion, beginning from a set of seed points that belong to the object of interest. The procedure iteratively appends neighbouring pixels whose attributes are sufficiently similar to those of the seed, until no further pixels satisfy the criterion.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ripples in a Pond"

Imagine you drop a single drop of red dye into a still pond of clear water. The red colour **spreads outward** from the drop, but only through water that is *clear enough to absorb* the dye (i.e. regions whose local conditions allow the colour to blend smoothly). The spreading stops when the dye encounters a strong barrier, such as a wall, an oil-slick, or a turbulent zone.

| Pond Analogy Element | Region Growing Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Initial drop of dye | **Seed point** selected inside the object |
| Spreading outward | Iterative **neighbourhood scan** (4 or 8 connected) |
| "Clear enough water" test | **Homogeneity predicate** (grey-level similarity) |
| Wall / oil-slick barrier | **Intensity gradient / edge** — region stops growing |
| Final red patch | Extracted **segmented region** of interest |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Region Growing is *bottom-up* (you start small and grow), *local* (each decision uses only immediate neighbours), and *homogeneity-driven* (pixels belong together if they *look* alike). This is the opposite philosophy of edge-based segmentation, which *separates* regions by *discontinuities*.

---

## 1.3 Why Region Growing Matters in Medical Imaging

In Medical Image Processing (CT, MRI, X-ray, Ultrasound), anatomical structures often have **gradual intensity transitions** rather than sharp edges. Edge-based detectors (Canny, Sobel) struggle here. Region Growing excels because:

1. **Tumour segmentation** — homogeneous tumour mass can be carved out from heterogeneous background.
2. **Organ delineation** — liver, kidney, spleen exhibit near-uniform interior intensities in CT.
3. **Lesion detection** — small bright/dark blobs on MRI sequences.
4. **Connected-component grouping** — identifies the entire object body, not just its contour.

Standard metrics used to evaluate a Region Growing output are:

- **Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC)** between automated region $R_{auto}$ and ground-truth $R_{gt}$ :

$$\text{DSC} = \frac{2 \cdot \vert R_{auto} \cap R_{gt} \vert}{\vert R_{auto} \vert + \vert R_{gt} \vert}$$

- **Jaccard Index (JI)** :

$$\text{JI} = \frac{\vert R_{auto} \cap R_{gt} \vert}{\vert R_{auto} \cup R_{gt} \vert}$$

- **Hausdorff Distance (HD)** in millimetres for boundary accuracy.

> [!NOTE]
> All the above metrics use the **cardinality (count)** of overlapping pixels, hence the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ is reserved for *set size* and must not be confused with the absolute-value pipe (which is forbidden inside markdown tables and replaced by $\mid$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Anatomy — Six Operational Phases

A canonical Region Growing algorithm proceeds through the following structured stages:

### Phase 1 — Seed Point Selection
The algorithm must start from one or more seed pixels that are guaranteed (or strongly believed) to lie **inside the target object**.

- **Manual selection**: User clicks inside the object (most common in clinical workflows).
- **Automatic selection**: Highest-intensity pixel, centroid of an edge-detected cluster, or output of a classifier (e.g. k-means, morphological opening).
- **Multiple seeds**: One seed per disjoint object; over-seeding produces *over-segmentation*.

### Phase 2 — Predicate (Homogeneity Criterion) Definition
The decision rule that decides whether a candidate pixel $p$ should join the region $R$. Common variants:

**(a) Absolute Grey-Level Difference (Single-Threshold)**

$$|f(p) - f(s)| < T \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \mid f(p) - f(s) \mid < T$$

**(b) Region-Mean Comparison (Adaptive)**

$$|f(p) - \mu_R| < T \quad \text{where} \quad \mu_R = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} f(p_i)$$

**(c) Region Mean ± Standard Deviation (Statistical)**

$$\mid f(p) - \mu_R \mid < k \cdot \sigma_R$$

**(d) Variance-Based (Global)**

$$\sigma_R^2 < T_{\sigma}$$

### Phase 3 — Neighbourhood Definition
- **4-connectivity** $\mathcal{N}_4(p)$ — only horizontal and vertical neighbours.
- **8-connectivity** $\mathcal{N}_8(p)$ — adds the four diagonal neighbours.

8-connectivity captures object shape more completely but is more sensitive to noise bridges.

### Phase 4 — Iteration Control
Pixels satisfying the predicate are added to $R$; the region's statistics ($\mu_R$, $\sigma_R$, area) are updated. Candidates are drawn from a **FIFO queue**, **LIFO stack**, or **priority queue** (depending on whether breadth-first, depth-first, or best-first growth is desired).

### Phase 5 — Stopping Rule
Growth halts when **any one** of the following conditions is met:

1. No neighbouring pixel satisfies the predicate.
2. Region area exceeds an upper bound $A_{max}$.
3. Maximum iteration count $N_{max}$ is reached.
4. Region has touched a *boundary pixel* with gradient magnitude exceeding $G_{max}$.

### Phase 6 — Post-Processing (Optional)
Morphological smoothing, hole-filling, smallest-connected-component removal, and labelling of the final region mask.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these equations appear verbatim in KTU ESE papers.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Seed-based absolute threshold | $\|f(p)-f(s)\| < T$ | $T$ : grey-levels (0–255) | Direct growth, 3-mark question |
| 2 | Region mean | $\mu_R = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} f(p_i)$ | $n$ : pixel count | Update rule after each addition |
| 3 | Region variance | $\sigma_R^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(f(p_i)-\mu_R)^2$ | unit² (grey-levels²) | Global uniformity test |
| 4 | Adaptive threshold | $\|f(p)-\mu_R\| < T$ | $T$ : grey-levels | Medical CT/MRI segmentation |
| 5 | Statistical predicate | $\|f(p)-\mu_R\| < k\cdot\sigma_R$ | $k \in [2, 3]$ | Robust to noise |
| 6 | Incremental mean update | $\mu_{new} = \frac{n\cdot\mu_{old}+f(p_{new})}{n+1}$ | — | Saves computation |
| 7 | Gradient magnitude | $G(p) = \sqrt{G_x^2+G_y^2}$ | grey-levels per pixel | Edge-barrier stopping rule |
| 8 | Dice Coefficient | $\text{DSC}=\frac{2\mid R_{auto}\cap R_{gt}\mid}{\mid R_{auto}\mid + \mid R_{gt}\mid}$ | dimensionless $\in [0,1]$ | Validation in part-b |
| 9 | Jaccard Index | $\text{JI}=\frac{\mid R_{auto}\cap R_{gt}\mid}{\mid R_{auto}\cup R_{gt}\mid}$ | dimensionless $\in [0,1]$ | Validation |
| 10 | 4-neighbourhood size | $\|\mathcal{N}_4\| = 4$ | pixels | Connectivity question |
| 11 | 8-neighbourhood size | $\|\mathcal{N}_8\| = 8$ | pixels | Connectivity question |
| 12 | Region area | $A_R = \sum_{(x,y)\in R} 1$ | pixels | Termination bound |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Safeguard:** In the table above, the absolute-value bars are intentionally replaced by $\mid$ to avoid corrupting markdown table syntax. In your answer scripts, you may freely use the standard $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ since it appears outside tables.

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Engineering Rationale

- **Why seeds?** They *anchor* the algorithm — without an anchor, region growing has no defined starting location and the result depends on scan order.
- **Why a predicate?** It encodes the user/clinical definition of *what counts as the same tissue*. A lung nodule on CT may be defined by $\mid f(p) - 180 \mid < 25$.
- **Why 4 vs 8 connectivity?** 4-connectivity gives *thinner*, more conservative regions (less leakage); 8-connectivity gives *fatter*, more complete regions but is leakage-prone.
- **Why a stopping rule?** Without an explicit stopping rule, the region either overgrows into background or terminates only when it hits the image border — both are usually wrong.
- **Why incremental mean update?** A naïve re-computation of $\mu_R$ after every pixel addition costs $O(n^2)$; the incremental form costs $O(1)$ per pixel.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Region Growing |
|---|---|
| **Radiotherapy Planning** | Delineate Gross Tumour Volume (GTV) on planning CT |
| **Neurosurgery** | Segment white-matter lesions on FLAIR MRI |
| **Cardiology** | Carve out the left ventricle on cine-MRI |
| **Ophthalmology** | Extract optic disc / cup from fundus images |
| **Pathology** | Identify nuclei clusters on H&E-stained slides |
| **Industrial QC** | Surface-defect isolation on X-ray or thermal images |

The algorithm's **engineering appeal** is its simplicity, transparency, and clinical interpretability — a radiologist can *understand and modify* the seed and threshold without black-box AI explanations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Incremental Mean Update Rule

### Problem
After growing a region from $n$ pixels to $n+1$ pixels by adding a new pixel with intensity $f(p_{new})$, recompute the region mean without scanning all $n+1$ pixels.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let the original region have $n$ pixels with intensities $f(p_1), f(p_2), \dots, f(p_n)$ and mean:

$$\mu_{old} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} f(p_i)$$

When pixel $p_{new}$ is added, the new mean becomes:

$$\mu_{new} = \frac{1}{n+1}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} f(p_i) + f(p_{new})\right]$$

Substitute the closed-form sum from $\mu_{old}$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} f(p_i) = n \cdot \mu_{old}$$

Therefore:

$$\mu_{new} = \frac{n \cdot \mu_{old} + f(p_{new})}{n+1}$$

### Equivalent Operator Form

$$\boxed{\mu_{new} = \mu_{old} + \frac{f(p_{new}) - \mu_{old}}{n+1}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Board examiners frequently award a 7-mark sub-question to students who demonstrate this incremental update. Memorising it once is worth more than re-deriving it under exam pressure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Incremental Variance Update Rule

### Problem
Similarly, derive a constant-time update for the variance $\sigma_R^2$ when a single pixel is appended.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Define the **sum of squares** $S_2$ :

$$S_2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f(p_i)^2$$

Then the variance can be written as:

$$\sigma_R^2 = \frac{S_2}{n} - \mu_R^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} f(p_i)^2 - \mu_R^2$$

When $p_{new}$ is added, the new sum of squares is:

$$S_2^{new} = S_2^{old} + f(p_{new})^2$$

Hence:

$$\sigma_{new}^2 = \frac{S_2^{old} + f(p_{new})^2}{n+1} - \mu_{new}^2$$

Using the previous result for $\mu_{new}$ :

$$\boxed{\sigma_{new}^2 = \frac{n \cdot (\sigma_{old}^2 + \mu_{old}^2) + f(p_{new})^2}{n+1} - \mu_{new}^2}$$

### Practical Computational Impact

| Quantity | Naïve Recomputation | Incremental Update |
|---|---|---|
| Time per pixel added | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Total cost for $N$ pixels | $O(N^2)$ | $O(N)$ |
| Memory | Two passes over region | Two running scalars |

For a tumour of area $10^4$ pixels, the naïve approach performs $\approx 5 \times 10^7$ additions, while the incremental approach performs $10^4$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Exam Style)

### Question
A 5×5 image patch has the following grey values. Seed is at $(3,3)$ with $f(3,3)=120$. Use **8-connectivity** with predicate $\|f(p) - f(s)\| < 20$. Determine the final region.

```
  130  118  100   80   60
  125  122  110   95   70
  135  128  120  105   85
  140  132  118  100   78
  150  138  125  108   90
```

### Step 1 — Identify Seed & Predicate
- Seed: $s=(3,3)$, $f(s)=120$.
- Predicate: $\mid f(p) - 120 \mid < 20 \Rightarrow f(p) \in (100, 140)$.

### Step 2 — Initialise
- Region $R = \{(3,3)\}$, $\mu_R = 120$, $n=1$.
- Queue of candidates: $\mathcal{N}_8(3,3) = \{(2,2),(2,3),(2,4),(3,2),(3,4),(4,2),(4,3),(4,4)\}$.

### Step 3 — Iterative Acceptance (FIFO order)

Iteration 1: Examine $(2,2)$, $f=122$.

$$\mid 122 - 120 \mid = 2 < 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ACCEPT}$$

Update mean: $\mu_R = \frac{1\cdot 120 + 122}{2} = 121.0$, $n=2$.

Iteration 2: Examine $(2,3)$, $f=128$.

$$\mid 128 - 121 \mid = 7 < 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ACCEPT}$$

Update mean: $\mu_R = \frac{2 \cdot 121 + 128}{3} = 123.33$, $n=3$.

Iteration 3: Examine $(2,4)$, $f=110$.

$$\mid 110 - 123.33 \mid = 13.33 < 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ACCEPT}$$

Update mean: $\mu_R = \frac{3 \cdot 123.33 + 110}{4} = 120.0$, $n=4$.

Iteration 4: Examine $(3,2)$, $f=128$.

$$\mid 128 - 120 \mid = 8 < 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ACCEPT}$$

$\mu_R = \frac{4\cdot 120 + 128}{5} = 121.6$, $n=5$.

Iteration 5: Examine $(3,4)$, $f=105$.

$$\mid 105 - 121.6 \mid = 16.6 < 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ACCEPT}$$

$\mu_R = \frac{5\cdot 121.6 + 105}{6} = 118.83$, $n=6$.

Iteration 6: Examine $(4,2)$, $f=132$.

$$\mid 132 - 118.83 \mid = 13.17 < 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ACCEPT}$$

$\mu_R = \frac{6\cdot 118.83 + 132}{7} = 120.71$, $n=7$.

Iteration 7: Examine $(4,3)$, $f=118$.

$$\mid 118 - 120.71 \mid = 2.71 < 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ACCEPT}$$

$\mu_R = \frac{7\cdot 120.71 + 118}{8} = 120.25$, $n=8$.

Iteration 8: Examine $(4,4)$, $f=100$.

$$\mid 100 - 120.25 \mid = 20.25 \not< 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{REJECT}$$

### Step 4 — Re-examine After Queue Drain

We now extend the queue to include new neighbours of accepted pixels (e.g. $(1,2)$ from $(2,2)$, $(1,3)$ from $(2,3)$, etc.):

| Pixel | $f$ | $\|f - \mu_R\|$ | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(1,2)$ | 118 | $\mid 118-120.25 \mid = 2.25$ | ACCEPT |
| $(1,3)$ | 100 | $\mid 100-120.25 \mid = 20.25$ | REJECT |
| $(1,4)$ | 80  | $\mid 80-120.25 \mid = 40.25$ | REJECT |
| $(2,1)$ | 125 | $\mid 125-120.25 \mid = 4.75$ | ACCEPT |
| $(2,5)$ | 70  | $\mid 70-120.25 \mid = 50.25$ | REJECT |
| $(3,1)$ | 135 | $\mid 135-120.25 \mid = 14.75$ | ACCEPT |
| $(3,5)$ | 85  | $\mid 85-120.25 \mid = 35.25$ | REJECT |
| $(4,1)$ | 140 | $\mid 140-120.25 \mid = 19.75$ | ACCEPT (borderline) |
| $(4,5)$ | 78  | $\mid 78-120.25 \mid = 42.25$ | REJECT |
| $(5,2)$ | 138 | $\mid 138-120.25 \mid = 17.75$ | ACCEPT |
| $(5,3)$ | 125 | $\mid 125-120.25 \mid = 4.75$ | ACCEPT |
| $(5,4)$ | 108 | $\mid 108-120.25 \mid = 12.25$ | ACCEPT |
| $(5,5)$ | 90  | $\mid 90-120.25 \mid = 30.25$ | REJECT |

### Step 5 — Final Region Composition

$R = \{(2,2),(2,3),(2,4),(3,2),(3,3),(3,4),(4,2),(4,3),(1,2),(2,1),(3,1),(4,1),(5,2),(5,3),(5,4)\}$

Total area $A_R = 15$ pixels.

Final mean $\mu_R \approx 121.6$ (re-computed for verification).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Predicate Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this decision matrix in 14-mark design questions to demonstrate examiner-level reasoning.**

| Predicate Type | Best Suited When | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|
| $\|f(p) - f(s)\| < T$ | Object has **uniform** intensity; small $T$ | Misses intensity-gradient objects |
| $\|f(p) - \mu_R\| < T$ | Object has **slow gradient** (typical in MRI) | Slow drift, may swallow background |
| $\|f(p) - \mu_R\| < k\sigma_R$ | Image is **noisy** but region is statistically uniform | Over-grows in textured backgrounds |
| $\sigma_R^2 < T_\sigma$ | Region must remain **globally** uniform | Stops prematurely when small local variations exist |
| Multi-feature predicate | Multi-channel images (RGB, multi-modal MRI) | Higher computation, requires feature scaling |

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Region Growing vs. Other Module-4 Methods

| Property | Region Growing | Thresholding | Split & Merge | Watershed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Seed required | Yes | No | No (root tile) | Markers optional |
| Edge reliance | Low | None | Low | High (gradient image) |
| Noise robustness | Low–Medium | Very Low | Medium | Low |
| Computational cost | $O(N \cdot n)$ | $O(N)$ | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N)$ |
| Multi-object handling | Multiple seeds | Difficult | Moderate | Excellent |
| Over-segmentation risk | Low | Moderate | High | High |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Region Growing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Medical Image I] --> B[Pre-processing Filter]
    B --> C{Seed Selection Mode}
    C -->|Manual| D[User clicks ROI]
    C -->|Automatic| E[Highest intensity / Centroid]
    D --> F[Initialise Region R, Queue Q]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Q is empty?}
    G -->|No| H[Pop candidate pixel p]
    H --> I[Compute Similarity]
    I --> J{Predicate holds?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Add p to R, Update mu_R, sigma_R]
    J -->|No| L[Discard p]
    K --> M[Push N_neighbours of p into Q]
    L --> N{Stopping criterion met?}
    M --> N
    N -->|No| G
    N -->|Yes| O[Post-processing Morphology]
    G -->|Yes| O
    O --> P[Output Binary Mask M_R]
    P --> Q[Validation Dice, Jaccard, HD]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow enters at the *Input Medical Image*, proceeds through *Pre-processing*, then loops around a *queue-driven growth cycle* (centre of the figure). Termination occurs when the queue empties OR a stopping rule fires, after which *post-processing* refines the binary mask and validation metrics are computed.

---

## 4.2 State Diagram — Lifecycle of a Candidate Pixel

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unvisited
    Unvisited --> Candidate: Pushed into Queue
    Candidate --> UnderTest: Popped for Predicate Test
    UnderTest --> Accepted: Predicate TRUE
    UnderTest --> Rejected: Predicate FALSE
    Accepted --> RegionMember: Added to R, neighbours enqueued
    Rejected --> BorderPixel: Marked as boundary
    RegionMember --> [*]
    BorderPixel --> [*]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Board-exam tip:** A correctly drawn state-transition diagram is worth **2–3 bonus marks** in a 14-mark question. Examiners love visual flow because it proves you understand the *control logic*, not just the equations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        A[Candidate Pixel f_p] 
        B[Region Statistics mu_R, sigma_R, n]
    end
    subgraph Decision
        D[Predicate Module]
        E[Threshold T or Multiplier k]
    end
    subgraph Output
        F[Boolean TRUE / FALSE]
        G[Update Engine]
    end
    A --> D
    B --> D
    E --> D
    D --> F
    F -->|TRUE| G
```
